name: Acosta-Sojo, Yadrianna
recent_1: Outlaw, L., Acosta-Sojo, Y., Schall, M. C., Purdy, G. T., & Sesek, R. F. (2025). Comparing the effects of user preference and experience when using passive low back exoskeletons on physical and cognitive load while performing simulated manufacturing tasks. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251369813
recent_1_synopsis: This study evaluated how user preference and training experience interact with passive low-back exoskeleton use during simulated manufacturing work, measuring both physical (biomechanical/EMG) and cognitive load outcomes. It provides empirical guidance on fitting and adoption strategies for occupational exoskeletons by examining whether preferred devices actually reduce combined physical and cognitive demands.
recent_2: Stirling, L., Acosta-Sojo, Y., & Dennerlein, J. T. (2024). Defining a systems framework for characterizing physical work demands with wearable sensors. Annals of Work Exposures and Health, 68(5), 443–465. DOI: 10.1093/annweh/wxae024
recent_2_synopsis: The paper proposes an organizing systems-level framework that guides researchers and practitioners in selecting and deploying wearable sensors (IMUs, heart rate monitors, surface EMG) to measure physical job demands across different levels of analysis. The framework is intended to support exposure assessment research linking workplace conditions to musculoskeletal disorder risk.
recent_3: Thaper, R., Sesek, R., Garnett, R., Acosta-Sojo, Y., & Purdy, G. T. (2023). The combined impact of hand-arm vibration and noise exposure on hearing sensitivity of agricultural/forestry workers—a systematic literature review. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 20(5), 4276. DOI: 10.3390/ijerph20054276
recent_3_synopsis: This systematic review of 18 peer-reviewed articles examined how simultaneous exposure to hand-arm vibration (HAV) and occupational noise compounds hearing loss in agricultural and forestry workers, identifying an additive effect on temporary threshold shifts likely mediated by vibration-induced cochlear vasospasm. The review highlights a population underserved by OSHA protections and points to combined-hazard assessment as a priority for occupational health surveillance.
seminal_1: Peng, X., Acosta-Sojo, Y., Wu, M. I., & Stirling, L. (2022). Actuation timing perception of a powered ankle exoskeleton and its associated ankle angle changes during walking. IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering, 30, 869–877. DOI: 10.1109/TNSRE.2022.3162213
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper quantified how accurately users perceive the timing of actuation events in a powered ankle exoskeleton and how those perceptions correlate with measured ankle kinematics during walking. It is her most-cited work (45 citations) and is influential for establishing perceptual benchmarks needed to design intuitive human-exoskeleton control interfaces.
seminal_2: Acosta-Sojo, Y., & Stirling, L. (2022). Individuals differ in muscle activation patterns during early adaptation to a powered ankle exoskeleton. Applied Ergonomics, 98, 103593. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2021.103593
seminal_2_synopsis: This study documented substantial inter-individual variability in lower-limb muscle activation during the first exposures to a powered ankle exoskeleton, demonstrating that a single universal control strategy is insufficient for all users. With 33 citations it is foundational for personalizing exoskeleton assistance and for understanding the human-in-the-loop adaptation process.
expertise_overview: Occupational human factors; wearable assistive technology and human-exoskeleton interaction—how users perceive, adapt to, and are physically and cognitively burdened by powered and passive exoskeletons in manufacturing and rehabilitation. Passive low-back and powered ankle exoskeletons, linking user preference, training experience, and combined biomechanical/EMG and cognitive load outcomes to fitting and adoption. Perceptual accuracy of exoskeleton actuation timing and inter-individual variability in lower-limb muscle activation, motivating personalized human-in-the-loop assistance. Surface EMG, inertial measurement, and observation-based exposure assessment within a systems-level wearable-sensor framework gauging physical work demands and musculoskeletal disorder risk. Sensorimotor proprioception and aging; combined-hazard exposure to hand-arm vibration and occupational noise.
notes: Identification is confident: Acosta-Sojo is a named PR (Past/Provisional Reviewer) for Human Factors and is an active member of HFES (profile on connect.hfes.org). Her Google Scholar profile (user=Mg3K5a4AAAAJ) and Auburn University faculty page confirm affiliation and publication record. Citation counts are drawn from her Google Scholar profile as of mid-2025 and may have updated since. The 2026 Applied Ergonomics article on shoulder exoskeletons (listed on her Scholar profile) could not be independently verified with a live DOI fetch; it was therefore not included in the recent publications to avoid a potentially unconfirmed citation. The HFES Annual Meeting 2025 paper on low back exoskeletons was published via SAGE (DOI confirmed from the SAGE abstract page URL, though the full page rendered the SAGE homepage rather than the article during fetch).

name: Alsaid, Areen
recent_1: Alkurdi, D., & Alsaid, A. (2025). Trust in Level 3 Automated Vehicles: A Systematic Literature Review. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813251369499
recent_1_synopsis: A PRISMA-guided synthesis of 80 empirical studies on trust in SAE Level 3 automated vehicles, identifying six key categories (automation features, environment, driver characteristics, HMI design, non-driving tasks, and training) that shape trust formation and measurement. The review maps the unique trust challenges posed by the Level 3 handover paradigm, in which drivers must alternate between passive monitoring and active control.
recent_2: Alsaid, A., Alkurdi, D., Rasouli, S., Talamonti, W. J., & Nasir, M. (2024). From Categorical to Dimensional: A Multifaceted Approach to Emotions. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813241262446
recent_2_synopsis: Using 238 participants rating AI-generated images of driving scenarios with both Plutchik's Wheel and the Self-Assessment Manikin, the study maps categorical emotion labels onto dimensional valence-arousal space to compare and integrate the two frameworks. The work informs richer, more nuanced in-vehicle emotion monitoring by showing how the two paradigms relate and where they diverge.
recent_3: Alsaid, A., & Li, M., Chiou, E. K., & Lee, J. D. (2023). Measuring trust: A text analysis approach to compare, contrast, and select trust questionnaires. Frontiers in Psychology, 14, 1192020. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1192020
recent_3_synopsis: Applies GloVe word-embedding text analysis to quantify the semantic relationships among items across major trust questionnaires, enabling researchers to compare construct coverage and select instruments suited to their application domain. An accompanying web app lets users explore the semantic space of trust scales, providing practical guidance for trust measurement in human-automation research.
seminal_1: Alsaid, A., Lee, J. D., & Price, M. (2020). Moving Into the Loop: An Investigation of Drivers' Steering Behavior in Highly Automated Vehicles. Human Factors, 62(4), 671-683. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720819850283
seminal_1_synopsis: Introduces 'vicarious steering' — spontaneous steering inputs made by drivers while automation is engaged — as a behavioral indicator of driver engagement and situational readiness in highly automated vehicles. Published in Human Factors itself, the paper shows that an adaptive algorithm can promote this behavior, with implications for automation design and takeover-transition safety.
seminal_2: Zhou, F., Alsaid, A., Blommer, M., Curry, R., Swaminathan, R., Kochhar, D. S., Talamonti, W., Tijerina, L., & Lei, B. (2020). Driver fatigue transition prediction in highly automated driving using physiological features. Expert Systems with Applications, 147, 113204. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eswa.2020.113204
seminal_2_synopsis: Develops a machine-learning pipeline using physiological signals (with PERCLOS as ground truth) to predict the onset of driver fatigue before it manifests, rather than detecting it reactively, which is critical for safe takeover requests in highly automated driving. Cited approximately 141 times, this work is the most-cited output in Alsaid's career and is widely referenced in the driver-monitoring and automated-vehicle literature.
expertise_overview: Affective computing, driver-state estimation, and human-automation interaction in driving/transportation. Trust and reliance in automated vehicles, driver workload and engagement, emotion recognition, empathic in-vehicle interfaces, displays and controls for SAE Level 2-3 automation. PRISMA review of trust in Level 3 automated vehicles identifying automation features, environment, driver characteristics, HMI design, non-driving tasks, and training as trust determinants under the handover paradigm. Maps categorical and dimensional emotion frameworks for in-vehicle emotion monitoring; word-embedding text analysis comparing trust questionnaires. Introduced 'vicarious steering' as a behavioral indicator of driver engagement and readiness; machine-learning pipelines using physiological signals to predict driver fatigue onset before manifestation, supporting safe takeover requests and driver monitoring.
notes: Identification is confident: Alsaid's prior collaboration with John D. Lee (a longtime Human Factors editor and HFES figure) and her publication record in the HFES Annual Meeting proceedings and directly in the Human Factors journal strongly support her role as a Past/Provisional Reviewer for that journal. The 2024 HFES paper listed the first author as Alkurdi et al. with Alsaid as last/senior author; citation count for the 2020 Expert Systems paper (~141) is drawn from Google Scholar as reported in the WebFetch result and may differ slightly from current counts. The 2025 HFES papers were in conference proceedings (not yet assigned volume/page numbers in the search results retrieved).

name: Armstrong, Thomas J.
recent_1: Fu, A. Q., Shih, A. J., & Armstrong, T. J. (2023). Examination of the prediction of the planar piecewise continuous lumped muscle parameter model for walking gait with ankle-foot orthosis. Medical Engineering & Physics.
recent_1_synopsis: This paper evaluated a biomechanical gait model (PPCLMP) for predicting walking patterns in individuals using ankle-foot orthoses. It extended prior work on simple, clinically deployable models that use inertial sensors or video to estimate lower-limb mechanics, with relevance to rehabilitation ergonomics and assistive device design.
recent_2: Carrasquillo, V., Armstrong, T. J., & Hu, S. J. (2022). Field observation of hospital food service workers and the relationship between customer demand and biomechanical stress: A case study. IISE Transactions on Occupational Ergonomics and Human Factors, 10(1), 47–58.
recent_2_synopsis: Using video-based motion analysis of hospital kitchen workers, this study found that increased customer demand reduced recovery time by 18% and raised hand activity level (HAL) by 27%, without changing reaching posture, demonstrating how workload variability elevates musculoskeletal risk in healthcare service workers.
recent_3: Fu, Q. A., Simeonov, P., Hsiao, H., Woolley, C., & Armstrong, T. J. (2022). Selected movement and force pattern differences in rail- and rung-climbing of fire apparatus aerial ladders at 52.5° slope. Applied Ergonomics, 99, 103639.
recent_3_synopsis: This study compared hand and foot movement and force patterns for firefighters using rail-climbing vs. rung-climbing techniques on aerial ladders, finding that rail-climbing provided better body-position control and faster ascent/descent speeds, with implications for ladder design standards and firefighter safety.
seminal_1: Silverstein, B. A., Fine, L. J., & Armstrong, T. J. (1987). Occupational factors and carpal tunnel syndrome. American Journal of Industrial Medicine, 11(3), 343–358.
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark cross-sectional study of 652 industrial workers established a dose-response relationship between hand force and repetitiveness and carpal tunnel syndrome prevalence, with odds ratios exceeding 15 for high-force, high-repetitiveness jobs compared to low-exposure jobs. It became foundational evidence for occupational exposure standards and is among the most cited papers in occupational ergonomics with approximately 1,500 citations.
seminal_2: Armstrong, T., Buckle, P., Fine, L. J., Hagberg, M., Jonsson, B., Kilbom, A., Kuorinka, I. A., Silverstein, B. A., Sjogaard, G., & Viikari-Juntura, E. R. (1993). A conceptual model for work-related neck and upper-limb musculoskeletal disorders. Scandinavian Journal of Work, Environment & Health, 19(2), 73–84.
seminal_2_synopsis: This internationally co-authored paper proposed a cascading dose-response framework linking workplace exposures (force, repetition, posture, vibration) through tissue-level dose to musculoskeletal disorder outcomes, explicitly incorporating capacity, adaptation, and modifying factors. With approximately 1,300 citations, it became the conceptual scaffold for research programs and risk-assessment tools worldwide.
expertise_overview: Occupational ergonomics and biomechanics; analysis and prevention of work-related musculoskeletal disorders of the hand, wrist, and upper extremity. Hand grip force, tendon mechanics, upper-limb exposure assessment; task analysis and job design methods including hand activity level metrics and threshold limit value frameworks. Established a dose-response relationship between hand force, repetitiveness, and carpal tunnel syndrome prevalence, and a cascading dose-response framework linking workplace exposures (force, repetition, posture, vibration) through tissue-level dose to musculoskeletal outcomes, incorporating capacity, adaptation, and modifying factors. Video-based motion analysis of healthcare service work, showing workload variability elevates hand activity and reduces recovery time. Surgical and food-service worker biomechanics, firefighter ladder-climbing technique and design, fall prevention, and biomechanical gait modeling for ankle-foot orthoses.
notes: Identification is highly confident: there is a single Thomas J. Armstrong in the ergonomics/human factors field — a University of Michigan professor with a 2025 Festschrift in IISE Transactions on Occupational Ergonomics and Human Factors, HFES Fellowship, and a Google Scholar profile (user=i0B8_icAAAAJ) showing 20,600+ citations. The 2023 gait-model paper (Medical Engineering & Physics) is confirmed via PubMed (PMID retrieved from PubMed filtered search). The 2022 IISE Transactions paper is confirmed via DOI 10.1080/24725838.2021.2018372. The 2022 Applied Ergonomics paper is confirmed via PubMed (PMID 34753097). The two seminal papers are confirmed via PubMed (PMID 3578290 for Silverstein 1987; PMID 8316782 for Armstrong 1993 conceptual model). Armstrong retired from active teaching around 2007 but continued research; the Festschrift (2025) notes his recent retirement from the University. No volume/page details were obtainable for the 2023 Medical Engineering & Physics paper from open sources, so that sub-field is omitted from the citation rather than fabricated.

name: Bell, Herbert H.
recent_1: Bennett, W., Schreiber, B., Portrey, A.M., & Bell, H.H. (2013). Challenges in transforming military training: Research and application of advanced simulation and training technologies and methods. Military Psychology, 25(3), 173–176. https://doi.org/10.1037/mil0000002
recent_1_synopsis: This introductory article to a special issue of Military Psychology, co-authored by Bell from the AFRL 711th Human Performance Wing, frames the ongoing challenge of translating simulation-based training science into operational practice; it emphasizes empirical evaluation of distributed mission operations training and mission-essential-competency-based syllabi as key transformation levers.
recent_2: Covas-Smith, C.M., & Bell, H.H. (2011). Network latency and perceived fidelity of motion in a networked flight simulator. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 55(1), 1529–1532.
recent_2_synopsis: The paper examines how varying degrees of network latency degrade pilots' perceived fidelity of motion in a networked distributed flight simulator, providing empirical guidance for acceptable latency thresholds in Distributed Mission Operations (DMO) training environments.
recent_3: Bell, H.H. (2005). Can PC-based systems enhance teamwork in the cockpit? International Journal of Aviation Psychology, 15(2). https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327108ijap1502_4
recent_3_synopsis: Bell reviews a decade of aircrew coordination studies conducted in low-cost PC-based flight simulations and presents new experimental results, providing evidence for the ecological validity of desktop simulators and offering practitioner guidelines for maximising simulation fidelity in crew-resource-management research and training.
seminal_1: Bell, H.H., & Waag, W.L. (1998). Evaluating the effectiveness of flight simulators for training combat skills: A review. International Journal of Aviation Psychology, 8(3), 223–242. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327108ijap0803_4
seminal_1_synopsis: This widely cited review (over 220 citations) synthesized Air Force and Navy evaluations of flight-simulator training effectiveness for combat mission skills. It identified a critical evidence gap — conclusions had rested largely on expert-opinion data with very few controlled transfer-of-training experiments — and proposed a systematic multi-step evaluation framework that has shaped subsequent military simulation training research.
seminal_2: Bell, H.H. (1999). The effectiveness of distributed mission training. Communications of the ACM, 42(9). https://doi.org/10.1145/306549.306573
seminal_2_synopsis: This article synthesized early evidence that networked virtual-reality environments could support complex multi-team combat training, establishing empirical and theoretical grounding for the USAF Distributed Mission Training program. It catalysed investment in Live-Virtual-Constructive (LVC) training architectures and remains a foundational reference for networked simulation training research.
expertise_overview: Training system design and evaluation for high-stakes aviation and defense; skill acquisition, transfer of training, and human performance. Flight-simulator training effectiveness, networked Distributed Mission Operations using Live-Virtual-Constructive simulation, and Mission Essential Competency frameworks for defining and measuring warfighter performance. Synthesized Air Force and Navy evaluations of simulator training for combat mission skills, exposing reliance on expert-opinion data and proposing a systematic multi-step transfer-of-training evaluation framework. Examined how network latency degrades perceived motion fidelity in distributed flight simulators, establishing acceptable latency thresholds. Reviewed aircrew coordination and crew-resource-management research in low-cost PC-based simulation, supporting ecological validity of desktop simulators. Grounding for networked virtual-reality multi-team combat training and team coordination.
notes: Identity is confidently established: Herbert H. Bell, PhD (experimental psychology, Vanderbilt), former Branch Chief/Technical Director, AFRL Warfighter Readiness Research Division, currently Senior Scientist at Discerning Technologies, Mount Dora, FL. His email domain afresearchlab.com is confirmed via ZoomInfo; ResearchGate lists 61 publications. The three "recent" publications are the most recent peer-reviewed items I could verify; his ResearchGate profile exists (Herbert-Bell-2 / Herbert Harrington Bell) but is access-restricted. No peer-reviewed publications from 2023–2026 were identifiable — after moving to Discerning Technologies his indexed output appears to have diminished. Citation count of ~223 for the Bell and Waag 1998 paper comes from Semantic Scholar. The 2005 "Can PC-Based Systems Enhance Teamwork" paper is confirmed in the Taylor and Francis DOI (s15327108ijap1502_4) as International Journal of Aviation Psychology Vol 15 No 2, but the full author list could not be retrieved due to paywall; the paper is attributed to Herbert H. Bell in ResearchGate and web search summaries. The Bennett et al. 2013 Military Psychology paper is a short editorial introduction (pages 173–176) co-authored by Bell, not a full empirical article; it is the most recent peer-reviewed item I could confirm with full citation details.

name: Beringer, Dennis B.
recent_1: Fercho, K. A., Beringer, D. B., & Donovan, C. (2025). Time . . . Is (Not) on My Side: A Review of Helicopter Pilot Response Times to Alerts and In-Flight Malfunctions. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251363185
recent_1_synopsis: This literature review systematically surveys helicopter-specific factors that affect pilot response times to cockpit alerts and in-flight malfunctions, an understudied domain compared to fixed-wing research. The paper identifies key sources of variability and proposes directions for future empirical work to support rotorcraft display and alerting system design.
recent_2: Fercho, K. A., Beringer, D. B., & Donovan, C. (2024). Factors that Affect Pilot Response Times to Alerts: Findings From a Literature Review and Aviation Safety Reporting System (ASRS) Reports. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 68(1), 50–55. DOI: 10.1177/10711813241260286
recent_2_synopsis: Drawing on a literature review and NASA ASRS incident data, this paper catalogues the alerting system design features, pilot characteristics, and operational environment variables that modulate how quickly pilots respond to cockpit alerts. The work demonstrates that a single universal 'acceptable' response time cannot be specified and argues for context-sensitive alerting standards.
recent_3: Fercho, K. A., Beringer, D. B., & Donovan, C. (2023). The Role of Human Factors in Evaluating and Updating Requirements for Flight Deck Displays and Controls. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 67(1), 57–62. DOI: 10.1177/21695067231193651
recent_3_synopsis: This paper argues that FAA regulatory requirements for flight-deck displays and controls require periodic evidence-based re-evaluation even when the underlying technology is unchanged, because pilot populations and accumulated human factors data evolve over time. It outlines a systematic HF analysis framework for informing regulatory updates to aviation certification standards.
seminal_1: Worringham, C. J., & Beringer, D. B. (1989). Operator orientation and compatibility in visual-motor task performance. Ergonomics, 32(4), 387–399. DOI: 10.1080/00140138908966105
seminal_1_synopsis: This study formulated the 'Visual Field (VF) principle,' which holds that stimulus-response compatibility is maximized when the controlled element moves in the same direction as the controlling limb segment as seen in the operator's visual field — regardless of the physical orientation of the control relative to the display. The principle has become one of the most influential design guidelines for control-display layout in aviation, industrial equipment, and other domains where operator orientation varies, and continues to be tested and applied in ergonomics research decades later.
seminal_2: Worringham, C. J., & Beringer, D. B. (1998). Directional stimulus-response compatibility: A test of three alternative principles. Ergonomics, 41(6), 864–880. DOI: 10.1080/001401398186694
seminal_2_synopsis: With 128 participants performing a joystick-based cursor-targeting task, this paper empirically compared the Visual Field, Control-Display, and Muscle Synergy compatibility principles, demonstrating that VF compatibility produced reliably shorter reaction times, movement times, and fewer reversal errors across both sexes and two testing sites. The work solidified the VF principle as the dominant framework for designing directional control-display arrangements and is widely cited across ergonomics, HCI, and aviation human factors literature.
expertise_overview: Aviation and aerospace human factors; flight-deck displays and controls including multifunction displays, synthetic-vision and highway-in-the-sky systems, and head-up/head-down display integration. Formulated the Visual Field principle of stimulus-response compatibility: compatibility is maximized when the controlled element moves in the same direction as the controlling limb segment in the operator's visual field, regardless of physical control orientation. Empirically compared Visual Field, Control-Display, and Muscle Synergy compatibility principles in joystick cursor-targeting, demonstrating shorter reaction and movement times and fewer reversal errors under VF compatibility. Pilot performance, workload, and response times to cockpit alerts, including rotorcraft alerting and context-sensitive alerting standards from incident data. Static and dynamic anthropometry for flight control force standards; evidence-based re-evaluation of aviation certification requirements.
notes: Identity confidently established: Beringer is a well-documented FAA researcher (Civil Aerospace Medical Institute, Oklahoma City; now retired) with an extensive SAGE/HFES publication record and confirmed EB membership on Human Factors. The three recent citations are all from the Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting (SAGE), not the flagship Human Factors journal itself, but they are the most recent peer-reviewed publications by this author identifiable through web sources; no peer-reviewed articles by Beringer in Human Factors (the flagship journal) dated 2023–2026 were found. The 2002 Beringer & Ball paper appears in HFES Proceedings (Vol. 46), not the flagship journal. The two seminal works (Worringham & Beringer 1989, 1998) are published in Ergonomics (Taylor & Francis) and are the most frequently referenced works associated with this author across the literature. Citation counts could not be retrieved directly from Google Scholar or Scopus in this session, but the influence of the VF principle papers is well-attested by a large body of subsequent literature that explicitly names them.

name: Bisantz, Ann M.
recent_1: Chen, K.-T., Chen, H.-Y. W., Bisantz, A., Shen, S., & Sahin, E. (2023). Where failures may occur in automated driving: A fault tree analysis approach. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 17(2), 147–165.
recent_1_synopsis: Applies fault tree analysis to two real-world automated driving system accidents to systematically trace failure pathways across the human-machine system. The framework identifies concrete loci—system design, interface, and driver training—where interventions can reduce risk in SAE Level 2 and 3 automation.
recent_2: Chen, K.-T., Chen, H.-Y. W., & Bisantz, A. (2023). Adding visual contextual information to continuous sonification feedback about low-reliability situations in conditionally automated driving: A driving simulator study. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 94, 25–41.
recent_2_synopsis: Tests four interface conditions combining continuous sonification (music-based reliability cues) with explanatory or action-recommending visual displays to help drivers anticipate and respond to automation handbacks in a simulator. Findings inform multimodal display design for conditionally automated (SAE Level 3) vehicles by quantifying how visual context augments auditory feedback.
recent_3: Wurst, C., Chen, H.-Y. W., Bisantz, A. M., Hall, S. F., Maggiulli, L., Joseph, K., & Sage, M. (2024). The context of care: Abstraction hierarchy modeling of therapeutic foster care programs. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 18(1), 22–36.
recent_3_synopsis: Applies cognitive work analysis—specifically the abstraction hierarchy—to model the functional structure of a therapeutic foster care agency, mapping purpose, values, functions, and physical resources. The resulting formative model reveals how assessment and treatment-planning work is organized and where administrative burden might be reduced through system redesign.
seminal_1: Jian, J.-Y., Bisantz, A. M., & Drury, C. G. (2000). Foundations for an empirically determined scale of trust in automated systems. International Journal of Cognitive Ergonomics, 4(1), 53–71.
seminal_1_synopsis: Develops and validates a 12-item self-report scale for measuring human trust in automated systems, grounded in a cluster analysis that distinguishes distrust-related from trust-related attitudinal dimensions. With over 2,600 citations on Google Scholar, the 'Trust in Automation' (TiA/Jian) scale has become the dominant psychometric instrument in research on human-automation trust across aviation, driving, robotics, and AI.
seminal_2: Bisantz, A. M., & Vicente, K. J. (1994). Making the abstraction hierarchy concrete. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 40(1), 83–117.
seminal_2_synopsis: Provides one of the first detailed worked examples of constructing an abstraction hierarchy—the core representational tool of cognitive work analysis—applied to a real process-control system, making the methodology tractable for practitioners. The paper is widely credited with enabling broader uptake of ecological interface design and cognitive systems engineering by demonstrating exactly how to operationalize the framework.
expertise_overview: Cognitive engineering, decision making, human-centered system design; cognitive work analysis and ecological interface design for representing work domains and supporting naturalistic reasoning. Developed and validated a 12-item self-report scale for measuring human trust in automation, grounded in cluster analysis distinguishing distrust- and trust-related dimensions, now a dominant psychometric instrument across aviation, driving, robotics, and AI. Influential worked example of constructing the abstraction hierarchy for process control, enabling broader uptake of ecological interface design. Healthcare human factors: emergency department information systems, medication reconciliation, communication, robot-assisted surgery, and therapeutic foster care modeling. Conditionally automated driving: multimodal displays combining sonification with explanatory and action-recommending visual cues for automation handbacks; fault-tree analysis of automated driving accidents.
notes: Identification is unambiguous: Ann M. Bisantz is a SUNY Distinguished Professor and Dean of Undergraduate Education at the University at Buffalo (SUNY), Fellow of HFES, confirmed member of the Human Factors journal editorial board via her SAGE author page. Citation counts for the Jian et al. (2000) trust scale vary by database (Google Scholar reports ~2,632; Semantic Scholar ~678; SciSpace ~1,339); the Google Scholar figure is used here as it is the most inclusive. The 2024 foster care paper citation is confirmed via a UB news release and the journal's table of contents. The 2026 Ergonomics paper on the decision ladder (Wurst, Chen, et al.) appeared in the Google Scholar sorted-by-date listing but could not be independently verified via a DOI fetch; it is omitted from the three recent citations in favor of the two well-confirmed 2023 papers and the 2024 paper. The 2025 medication reconciliation paper (Chen, Wurst, Bisantz et al., Journal of Patient Safety) is confirmed via PubMed Central.

name: Boyle, Linda Ng
recent_1: Lim, C., Prendez, D., Boyle, L. N., & Rajivan, P. (2025). The impact of cybersecurity attacks on human trust in autonomous vehicle operations. Human Factors, 67(5), 485–502. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208241283321
recent_1_synopsis: A driving-simulator experiment with 40 participants showed that simulated cyberattacks on autonomous vehicles significantly degrade operator trust and that trust remains depressed even after a subsequent error-free drive; providing a notification that the vulnerability had been patched did not meaningfully restore trust, underscoring that trust is a dynamic construct requiring active recalibration mechanisms in autonomous systems.
recent_2: Park, S., Xing, Y., Akash, K., Misu, T., Mehrotra, S., & Boyle, L. N. (2024). The impact of pedestrian interactions at intersections on the three levels of drivers' situation awareness. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 107, 167–180.
recent_2_synopsis: Using a balanced incomplete block design with 56 participants in a driving simulator, this study modeled how pedestrian location, direction, and motion predict each of Endsley's three levels of situation awareness (perception, comprehension, projection) via mixed-effects logit models, finding that short one-second time windows were sufficient to predict pedestrian direction while three-second windows were needed to predict location and intent.
recent_3: Donmez, B., McDonald, A. D., Lee, J. D., & Boyle, L. N. (2023). Road user behavior: Describing, inferring, predicting and beyond. Transportation Research Interdisciplinary Perspectives, 22, 100932.
recent_3_synopsis: This methodological review synthesizes how descriptive, inferential, and predictive analytic approaches can be applied to naturalistic and simulator data on road-user behavior, providing a cross-disciplinary framework that bridges human factors, traffic psychology, and machine-learning perspectives on driving safety research.
seminal_1: Ghazizadeh, M., Lee, J. D., & Boyle, L. N. (2012). Extending the Technology Acceptance Model to assess automation. Cognition, Technology & Work, 14(1), 39–49. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-011-0194-3
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper adapted Davis's Technology Acceptance Model to the domain of driving automation by identifying perceived usefulness and ease of use as predictors of drivers' intentions to use automated systems; with over 790 citations it has become a foundational reference for researchers studying driver acceptance and adoption of in-vehicle automation and ADAS technologies.
seminal_2: Donmez, B., Boyle, L. N., & Lee, J. D. (2007). Safety implications of providing real-time feedback to distracted drivers. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 39(3), 581–590.
seminal_2_synopsis: This driving-simulator study demonstrated that real-time alerting feedback can reduce drivers' engagement in secondary distracting tasks, but also raises the risk of introducing an additional attentional burden; with approximately 322 citations it established an influential framework for evaluating the safety trade-offs of in-vehicle distraction-mitigation feedback systems.
expertise_overview: Driving and transportation; driver distraction and attention, automation and autonomy, trust in automation, situation awareness, and statistical crash modeling. How secondary tasks and real-time alerting feedback alter crash risk, showing distraction-mitigation feedback can reduce secondary-task engagement while introducing additional attentional burden. Trust dynamics under cybersecurity threats and system failures: simulated cyberattacks depress operator trust persistently even after error-free driving and patch notifications. Adapted the Technology Acceptance Model to driving automation, identifying perceived usefulness and ease of use as predictors of intention to use ADAS and autonomous vehicles. Models how pedestrian location, direction, and motion predict Endsley's three levels of situation awareness. Integrates naturalistic driving data, driving simulation, and statistical and machine-learning modeling, including young drivers recovering from concussion and older adults.
notes: Identity is confidently established. Boyle is a named HFES Fellow, publishes in Human Factors journal directly (including the 2025 cybersecurity-AV trust paper), and is listed on multiple university profiles confirming her affiliation. Citation counts for the Google Scholar profile (user=-iCRFrsAAAAJ) were confirmed via Scholar scrape. The TAM-automation paper citation count (~790) is from the Scholar profile snapshot; the Donmez et al. 2007 count (~322) is from the same source. Volume/issue/page details for the 2024 Transportation Research Part F paper were confirmed via ScienceDirect metadata in search snippets but the page could not be directly fetched (403); details are consistent across multiple independent sources and are considered reliable.

name: Calhoun, Gloria L.
recent_1: Calhoun, G. L. (2022). Adaptable (not adaptive) automation: Forefront of human–automation teaming. Human Factors, 64(2), 269–277. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208211037457
recent_1_synopsis: This invited perspective paper argues that the human factors research community has over-indexed on adaptive (system-initiated) automation while under-investigating adaptable (human-initiated) automation, and calls for systematic empirical work on hybrid approaches. The piece frames adaptable automation as foundational to successful human–automation teaming, particularly in multi-unmanned-vehicle control contexts.
recent_2: Calhoun, G. L., Bartik, J., Ruff, H. A., Frost, E., & Behymer, K. J. (2021). Enabling human-autonomy teaming with multi-unmanned vehicle control interfaces. Human-Intelligent Systems Integration, 3(2), 155–174. https://doi.org/10.1007/s42454-020-00020-0
recent_2_synopsis: Using transportation-planning expert questions as a design framework, the paper describes the controls and displays developed for a military multi-unmanned-vehicle (UV) command-and-control prototype intended to support joint human–autonomy decision-making and task completion. It provides empirical and design guidance for interface features (delegation, monitoring, re-planning) that enable a single operator to manage heterogeneous UV teams.
recent_3: Calhoun, G. L., & Liggett, K. K. (2019/2023). Controls and displays for aviation research simulation: A historical review. In P. A. Hancock, D. A. Vincenzi, J. A. Wise, & M. Mouloua (Eds.), Human Factors in Simulation and Training (pp. chapter). CRC Press/Routledge.
recent_3_synopsis: This book chapter surveys the evolution of cockpit controls and display technologies within research flight simulators, providing historical context for how display fidelity and control design choices affect pilot performance in simulation environments. Citation count is modest (1 citation per Google Scholar), and the exact page range is unconfirmed; treat as a chapter-level reference.
seminal_1: Middendorf, M., McMillan, G., Calhoun, G., & Jones, K. S. (2000). Brain-computer interfaces based on the steady-state visual-evoked response. IEEE Transactions on Rehabilitation Engineering, 8(2), 211–214. https://doi.org/10.1109/86.847819
seminal_1_synopsis: The AFRL team implemented and evaluated two BCIs that translate the steady-state visual evoked response (SSVER) into a control signal, demonstrating that users could operate physical devices and computer programs via gaze-based neural signals without muscular effort. With over 1,016 citations, this paper is among the foundational references in the SSVEP-BCI literature and has shaped two decades of passive brain–computer interface research.
seminal_2: Calhoun, G. L., Ruff, H. A., Behymer, K. J., & Frost, E. M. (2018). Human-autonomy teaming interface design considerations for multi-unmanned vehicle control. Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science, 19(3), 321–352. https://doi.org/10.1080/1463922X.2017.1315751
seminal_2_synopsis: Drawing on more than a decade of simulation research at AFRL, this paper synthesizes design principles for human-autonomy teaming interfaces when a single operator manages multiple heterogeneous unmanned vehicles alongside intelligent decision-aiding agents. Cited ~89 times, it is the primary design-guidance reference in the emerging multi-UV supervisory control literature and directly informs later work on adaptable automation.
expertise_overview: Aerospace human factors, aviation displays and controls, human-autonomy teaming. Cockpit and UAV ground-station interface design, adaptive versus adaptable automation allocation, performance-based automation triggering, and supervisory control of multiple unmanned air vehicles, addressing operator workload, vigilance, and situation awareness. Argues the field has over-indexed on adaptive system-initiated automation while under-investigating adaptable human-initiated automation, framing adaptable automation as foundational to human-automation teaming in multi-vehicle control. Developed controls and displays—delegation, monitoring, and re-planning features—enabling a single operator to manage heterogeneous unmanned-vehicle teams alongside intelligent decision-aiding agents. Implemented and evaluated steady-state visual evoked response brain-computer interfaces enabling gaze-based device control without muscular effort, a foundational SSVEP-BCI contribution. Three-dimensional auditory cueing, tactile alert displays, and trust in automation.
notes: Identity is unambiguous: Gloria L. Calhoun is consistently identified as a research psychologist at AFRL/711th Human Performance Wing, Wright-Patterson AFB, with h-index 28 and ~3,300 Google Scholar citations (profile: scholar.google.com/citations?user=fob4-HkAAAAJ). Her most-cited paper (Middendorf et al. 2000, IEEE Trans Rehabil Eng, >1,000 citations) predates her primary HF/UAV focus but is genuinely hers. No peer-reviewed publications from 2023–2026 were found; she appears to be retired. The 2022 Human Factors paper (published online August 2021, journal date 2022, Vol 64(2):269-277) is her most recent peer-reviewed journal article. The 2021 Human-Intelligent Systems Integration paper is the next most recent. The book chapter with Liggett appears in the Routledge Human Factors in Simulation and Training volume; exact year may be 2019 (first edition) rather than 2023; treat with caution. For the "three most recent" requirement, only two clearly peer-reviewed post-2019 journal articles could be confirmed; the third entry is a book chapter with lower certainty on publication date.

name: Cao, Caroline
recent_1: Duncan, J.R., Berg, J.J., & Cao, C.G.L. (2025). Improving Performance during Image-Guided Procedures: A Human Factors Perspective. Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology, 36(11), 1637–1647.
recent_1_synopsis: This paper applies human factors engineering principles to interventional radiology, examining how workflow design, display layout, team communication, and environmental factors affect performance during image-guided procedures. It provides a structured HF framework intended to help IR practitioners identify and reduce performance-degrading factors in the procedure suite.
recent_2: Lauinger, A.R., McNicholas, M., Bramlet, M., Bederson, M., Sutton, B.P., Cao, C.G.L., Ahmad, I.S., Brown, C., Jamison, S., Adve, S., Vozenilek, J., Rehg, J., & Cohen, M.S. (2024). recent_2_synopsis: A systematic review of how extended reality (VR, AR, and MR) combined with medical imaging is being applied across clinical training and practice, covering preoperative planning, intraoperative guidance, and simulation-based education. The paper maps the state of the field and identifies gaps in validation and clinical deployment.
recent_3: Allemang-Trivalle, A., Donjat, J., Bechu, G., Coppin, G., Chollet, M., Klaproth, O.W., Mitschke, A., Schirrmann, A., & Cao, C.G.L. (2024). Modeling Fatigue in Manual and Robot-Assisted Work for Operator 5.0. IISE Transactions on Occupational Ergonomics and Human Factors, 12(1–2), 135–147. DOI: 10.1080/24725838.2024.2321460
recent_3_synopsis: Developed and validated a simulation model to quantify worker fatigue under manual versus robot-assisted production conditions in an Airbus manufacturing context, contributing to the Operator 5.0 agenda of sustainable human-robot collaboration. The model demonstrated that robot assistance reduced fatigue accumulation, supporting data-driven ergonomic design of next-generation production systems.
seminal_1: Chellali, A., Mentis, H., Miller, A., Ahn, W., Arikatla, V.S., Sankaranarayanan, G., De, S., Schwaitzberg, S.D., & Cao, C.G.L. (2016). Achieving interface and environment fidelity in the Virtual Basic Laparoscopic Surgical Trainer. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 96, 22–37. DOI: 10.1016/j.ijhcs.2016.07.005
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper established the fidelity requirements—interface realism and environmental correspondence—needed for the VBLaST virtual reality trainer to validly substitute for the physical Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery (FLS) box trainer. With approximately 297 citations, it became a cornerstone reference for evidence-based design and validation of surgical simulation systems and influenced standards for VR-based credentialing.
seminal_2: Zhou, M., Tse, S., Derevianko, A., Jones, D.B., Schwaitzberg, S.D., & Cao, C.G.L. (2012). Effect of haptic feedback in laparoscopic surgery skill acquisition. Surgical Endoscopy, 26(4), 1128–1134. DOI: 10.1007/s00464-011-2011-8
seminal_2_synopsis: Demonstrated through a controlled experiment that haptic (force) feedback during simulator training accelerated laparoscopic skill acquisition in novices, providing empirical evidence that sensory augmentation improves human performance in minimally invasive surgery. With approximately 169 citations, this work is widely cited in surgical ergonomics and drives ongoing debate and design of haptic-enabled training simulators.
expertise_overview: Healthcare human factors, surgical simulation and training, haptics and sensory augmentation, human-robot interaction, and extended reality for skill acquisition and empathy training. Established fidelity requirements—interface realism and environmental correspondence—for the VBLaST virtual reality trainer to validly substitute for the physical Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery box trainer, shaping evidence-based validation of surgical simulators. Controlled experiment showing haptic force feedback accelerates laparoscopic skill acquisition in novices, advancing sensory augmentation in minimally invasive surgery. Human factors in interventional radiology and image-guided procedures: workflow, display layout, team communication, and environmental factors. Reviews extended reality combined with medical imaging across preoperative planning, intraoperative guidance, and simulation-based education. Simulation models quantifying worker fatigue under manual versus robot-assisted production, supporting human-robot collaboration and AI-driven immersive empathy and communication training.
notes: Identity is confirmed with high confidence: multiple institutional sources (UIUC ISE, Bioengineering, Carle Illinois College of Medicine) and HFES records explicitly confirm she is Associate Editor of Human Factors journal. Google Scholar profile (user=T8aQ4g8AAAAJ) confirms h-index of 36, ~4,097 total citations, and 52 peer-reviewed journal articles. The three recent publications selected are the most recent confirmed peer-reviewed journal articles (as opposed to conference papers). The 2025 JVIR paper full text was not directly accessible (HTTP 403) but volume/issue/pages were confirmed via two independent web searches. The VBLaST IJHCS paper citation count (~297) was drawn from Google Scholar and confirmed as her most-cited work; the 2012 Surgical Endoscopy haptic paper (~169 citations) is the second most-cited journal article. Her most-cited item overall is the 1996 ASME conference paper on task and motion analysis in endoscopic surgery (~249 citations on Scholar), but the 2012 journal article was chosen as the second seminal work given it is peer-reviewed journal output with broader accessibility.

name: Cha, Jackie
recent_1: Kennedy, S., Fuller, P., Cha, J. S., Carbonell, A. M., Luo, Q., & Joseph, A. (2025). Exploring the impact of the physical environment on robotic-assisted surgery outcomes and processes: A scoping review. Human Factors, 67(10), 1011–1045. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251333907
recent_1_synopsis: This scoping review synthesizes evidence on how operating room physical attributes — layout, room size, acoustic noise, and dedicated robotic suites — affect workflow efficiency, workload, staff/patient safety, and surgical performance in robotic-assisted surgery. It maps gaps in the literature and informs evidence-based OR design for surgical robotics environments.
recent_2: Ball, M., Fuller, P., Goodwin, C., & Cha, J. S. (2025). Objective and subjective evaluation of non-technical skills and technical leadership skills during simulated critical care scenarios. IISE Transactions on Occupational Ergonomics and Human Factors, 13(4), 264–277. https://doi.org/10.1080/24725838.2025.2551536
recent_2_synopsis: Using sensor-based objective measures and observer ratings in simulated critical care scenarios with medical students, the study finds that passive communication behaviors negatively correlate with leadership scores, providing evidence that automated behavioral metrics can track non-technical skill development during training.
recent_3: Fuller, P., Kennedy, S., Ball, M., Duffie, H., Gainey, M., Luo, Q., Joseph, A., Carbonell, A., & Cha, J. S. (2025). Understanding the challenges of robotic-assisted surgery adoption: Perspectives from stakeholders and the general population on human-interaction, built environment, and training. Applied Ergonomics, 122, 104403. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104403
recent_3_synopsis: Through interviews and surveys of surgical stakeholders and the general public, this study identifies a multi-level barrier taxonomy — human-robot interaction difficulties, physical environment constraints, and training gaps — that impedes wider adoption of robotic-assisted surgery platforms.
seminal_1: Wu, C., Cha, J., Sulek, J., Zhou, T., Sundaram, C. P., Wachs, J., & Yu, D. (2020). Eye-tracking metrics predict perceived workload in robotic surgical skills training. Human Factors, 62(8), 1365–1386. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720819874544
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper demonstrated that objective eye-tracking measures (fixation duration, saccade rate, pupil dilation) reliably predict trainee-perceived workload during robotic surgery simulation. With 168 citations it is among the most-cited works linking ocular physiology to cognitive workload in a surgical human-robot interaction context, directly informing adaptive training system design.
seminal_2: Cha, J. S., Monfared, S., Stefanidis, D., Nussbaum, M. A., & Yu, D. (2020). Supporting surgical teams: Identifying needs and barriers for exoskeleton implementation in the operating room. Human Factors, 62(3), 377–390. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720819879271
seminal_2_synopsis: This needs-analysis study used structured observations and interviews to characterize the ergonomic demands placed on scrub technicians, surgeons, and assistants, and systematically identified physical, workflow, and organizational barriers to adopting upper-body exoskeletons in the OR. With 111 citations, it is credited with opening a research agenda on occupational exoskeleton viability in perioperative settings.
expertise_overview: Healthcare human factors and ergonomics; measuring and improving non-technical skills—teamwork, communication, situational awareness, leadership—in surgical and critical care teams using wearable sensors, eye-tracking, and proximity/communication metrics. Human-robot interaction in clinical environments: cognitive workload, team dynamics, and training for robotic-assisted surgery; perioperative occupational ergonomics including exoskeleton feasibility and physical environment design. Objective eye-tracking measures—fixation duration, saccade rate, pupil dilation—reliably predict perceived workload during robotic surgery simulation, informing adaptive training. Characterized ergonomic demands on scrub technicians, surgeons, and assistants, identifying barriers to upper-body exoskeleton adoption in the operating room. Maps how operating room layout, size, acoustics, and dedicated robotic suites affect workflow, workload, and safety; links passive communication behaviors to leadership scores. Translates multi-modal physiological and behavioral signals into objective performance metrics.
notes: Identification is confident. Jackie S. Cha (also listed as Jackie Cha) is a named Past/Provisional Reviewer for Human Factors; she has published five papers in Human Factors and is an active HFES member with multiple annual meeting presentations. Google Scholar (profile ID w_cIgXwAAAAJ) reports ~833 total citations and h-index 14 as of mid-2024. Publication details for recent papers verified against PubMed, SAGE Journals, and her August 2024 CV PDF (cecas.clemson.edu/echo/). The 2025 IISE Transactions authors list is based on search-result text (Taylor & Francis 403 blocked full page); Ball and Fuller are confirmed co-authors from the conference paper antecedent. No fabricated DOIs — all DOIs confirmed via PubMed or publisher pages.

name: Chen, Huei-Yen Winnie
recent_1: Chen, H.-Y. W., Wurst, C., Lattimer, T. A., Setiowati, N., Bisantz, A., Wahler, R. G. Jr., Jacobs, D. M., Hewner, S., Stoll, J., Casucci, S., & Singh, R. (2025). Challenges and opportunities in the medication reconciliation process in an emergency department: An observational human factors study. Journal of Patient Safety, 21(7 Suppl), S7–S11. https://doi.org/10.1097/PTS.0000000000001362
recent_1_synopsis: Through 32 hours of structured observation across 12 ED sessions, this study applied a human factors lens to identify systemic barriers—fragmented workflows, EHR usability problems, and frequent interruptions—that impede safe medication reconciliation during care transitions for older adults. It proposes delegating routine tasks to trained support staff and enhancing interdisciplinary coordination as countermeasures.
recent_2: Demir, B., Du, J., Hansma, B. J., Chen, H.-Y. W., Gu, H., & Donmez, B. (2023). Cell phone-related driver distraction: Habits predict behavior over and above the theory of planned behavior variables. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 192, 107200. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aap.2023.107200
recent_2_synopsis: Using structural equation modeling with 1,016 Chinese drivers, this study demonstrated that habitual cell phone use while driving is the strongest predictor of distracted driving, accounting for variance beyond attitudes, norms, and perceived behavioral control in the Theory of Planned Behavior. The finding shifts the intervention focus from attitude-change strategies toward habit-disruption approaches.
recent_3: Chen, K.-T., Chen, H.-Y. W., & Bisantz, A. (2023). Adding visual contextual information to continuous sonification feedback about low-reliability situations in conditionally automated driving: A driving simulator study. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 94, 25–41. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trf.2023.01.009
recent_3_synopsis: This simulator study tested four interface conditions combining continuous music-based sonification (conveying real-time automation reliability) with explanatory and/or action-recommending visual displays during conditionally automated driving. Results inform multimodal interface design for keeping drivers appropriately in the loop before takeover events.
seminal_1: Chen, H.-Y. W., Hoekstra-Atwood, L., Donmez, B., & Marulanda, S. (2016). Self-reported engagement in driver distraction: An application of the Theory of Planned Behaviour. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 38, 151–163.
seminal_1_synopsis: Surveying 578 drivers, this paper was among the first to rigorously apply the Theory of Planned Behaviour to voluntary distraction engagement, identifying attitudes, perceived behavioral control, and descriptive (but not injunctive) norms as significant predictors. With approximately 124 citations, it established a psychosocial framework widely adopted in subsequent distracted-driving research.
seminal_2: Chen, H.-Y. W., & Donmez, B. (2016). What drives technology-based distractions? A structural equation model on social-psychological factors of technology-based driver distraction engagement. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 91, 166–174.
seminal_2_synopsis: This complementary SEM-based study showed that attitudes are the dominant social-psychological predictor of technology-based distraction engagement, with descriptive norms (what other drivers do) exerting more influence than injunctive norms (what drivers feel they ought to do). Cited approximately 87 times, it is a cornerstone reference for modeling the motivational antecedents of technology-induced driver distraction.
expertise_overview: Driving and transportation human factors, human-automation interaction, healthcare human factors. Driver distraction: voluntary versus involuntary engagement, habitual cell-phone use as strongest predictor of distracted driving beyond Theory of Planned Behavior constructs, descriptive norms outweighing injunctive norms. Conditionally automated driving via music-based sonification of automation reliability, multimodal explanatory and action-recommending displays, takeover support, fault-tree analysis of automation failures. Visual attention via the visual occlusion paradigm and secondary-task glance metrics, naturalistic driving data, driving simulators, structural equation modeling. Cognitive work analysis, abstraction hierarchy, and work-domain analysis applied to medication management, emergency-department medication reconciliation for older adults, deprescribing communication.
notes: Identification is confident: Huei-Yen (Winnie) Chen is an Associate Professor in the Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering at the University at Buffalo (SUNY), with a PhD from the University of Toronto (2013) under Birsen Donmez. She has published directly in Human Factors journal (the 2022/2023 Senders' Visual Occlusion Model paper; the 2018 voluntary/involuntary distraction paper) and in HFES Proceedings, making her a plausible PR-level reviewer for the journal. Citation counts from Google Scholar: h-index 14, ~855 total citations as of 2025–2026. The 2025 deprescribing conversations paper (Tenzek et al., Basic & Clinical Pharmacology & Toxicology, DOI 10.1111/bcpt.70122) was confirmed via search but the paywall prevented full citation verification; it is listed as a Google Scholar entry credited to her profile. The 2023 sonification paper DOI is confirmed via ScienceDirect abstract. All seminal papers are confirmed via multiple publisher and ResearchGate sources.

name: Chen, Jing
recent_1: See, J. E., & Chen, J. (2026). Vigilance Research Beyond the Laboratory: Methodological Considerations and Practical Insights. Human Factors. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208261435832
recent_1_synopsis: This invited paper addresses the methodological challenges of conducting vigilance research outside the controlled laboratory setting, using illustrative examples from human-automation operator simulators and industrial inspection. It provides practical guidance for researchers seeking to translate vigilance science into real-world operational contexts such as automated driving and inspection tasks.
recent_2: Mishler, S., Parker, C., & Chen, J. (2025). Using anthropomorphism, transparency, and feedback to improve phishing detection aids. Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science, 26(1), 45–65.
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined how design features of a phishing detection aid — specifically anthropomorphism (human-like presentation), transparency (explanation of aid reasoning), and performance feedback — affect user trust, compliance, and aid retention. The findings advance understanding of how to design cybersecurity decision-support tools that users will adopt and appropriately calibrate their trust in.
recent_3: Garcia, K. R., Chen, J., Xiao, Y., Mishler, S., Wang, C., & Hu, B. (2025). Human Perception of AI Capabilities at Classifying Perturbed Roadway Signs. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 55(4), 499–507.
recent_3_synopsis: This paper investigates how people perceive the ability of AI systems to correctly classify adversarially perturbed stop signs, a safety-critical scenario for autonomous vehicles. It reveals gaps between actual AI performance and user mental models, with implications for trust calibration in human-autonomy teaming for transportation.
seminal_1: Proctor, R. W., & Chen, J. (2015). The Role of Human Factors/Ergonomics in the Science of Security: Decision Making and Action Selection in Cyberspace. Human Factors, 57(5), 721–727.
seminal_1_synopsis: This invited paper defined the nascent "science of security" agenda through a human factors lens, using phishing detection and mobile app selection as case studies to argue that HF/E methods and theory can improve cybersecurity outcomes. With over 117 citations, it is widely credited with legitimizing the cybersecurity domain as a core area of human factors research and catalyzed a productive line of empirical work.
seminal_2: Gates, C., Chen, J., Li, N., & Proctor, R. W. (2014). Effective Risk Communication for Android Apps. IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing, 11(3), 252–265.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper demonstrated that presenting users with a summarized risk-score display — rather than raw permission lists — significantly reduced risky Android app-installation decisions. With over 88 citations, it is one of the foundational empirical studies linking HF risk-communication theory to mobile security practice and is widely cited in subsequent mobile privacy and security research.
expertise_overview: Human-automation interaction and trust, cybersecurity human factors, human-autonomy teaming. Trust calibration after automation failures, vigilance, driver workload, warning systems in semi-autonomous and automated driving; methodological guidance for translating vigilance research beyond the laboratory. Risk communication, phishing detection, mobile app permission displays; summarized risk-score displays reduce risky app-installation decisions; anthropomorphism, transparency, and performance feedback in detection aids shape user trust, compliance, retention. Perceptions of AI classification of adversarially perturbed stop signs, human-autonomy teaming in unmanned aerial systems. Grounded in spatial stimulus-response compatibility, response-effect compatibility, display-control relationships.
notes: Identification is high-confidence. The name "Jing Chen" is common, but this individual is unambiguous: she maintains a dedicated professional site (jingchen416.wordpress.com and sites.google.com/site/jingchen416), a Google Scholar profile (uBaMHi8AAAAJ), and is explicitly listed in SAGE journal search results alongside Human Factors publications. Her role as Editorial Board member (EB) is consistent with her sustained publication record in Human Factors and Applied Ergonomics, multiple HFES annual meeting presentations, and HF/E-aligned awards (Earl Alluisi Award for Early Career Achievement, HFES). Citation counts are from Google Scholar as of mid-2026; the 2026 paper (See and Chen) and the 2025 IEEE Transactions paper are her most recently confirmed peer-reviewed journal articles; several additional conference papers from 2024-2025 were omitted in favor of journal articles per the task instructions.

name: Chen, Karen Boru
recent_1: Wu, L., & Chen, K. B. (2024). Examining the effects of gender transfer in virtual reality on implicit gender bias. Human Factors, 66(5), 1504–1519. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208221145264
recent_1_synopsis: Forty participants were embodied in opposite-gender avatars in immersive VR and exposed to sexual harassment scenarios; gender transfer temporarily reduced scores on an Implicit Association Test for gender bias, suggesting VR embodiment as a potential bias-reduction training tool.
recent_2: Sanders, N. E., Şener, E., & Chen, K. B. (2024). Robot-related injuries in the workplace: An analysis of OSHA Severe Injury Reports. Applied Ergonomics, 121, 104324. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104324
recent_2_synopsis: An analysis of 77 OSHA Severe Injury Reports from 2015–2022 characterised the nature and body-part distribution of injuries from both stationary and mobile industrial robots, finding that stationary robots predominantly caused finger amputations and head/torso fractures while mobile robots caused leg/foot fractures, with implications for guard and collision-avoidance system design.
recent_3: Chen, K., & Chen, K. B. (2023). Task-oriented and imitation-oriented movements in virtual reality exercise performance and design. Human Factors, 65(1), 125–136. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208211010100
recent_3_synopsis: Participants performed upper-extremity exercise movements in immersive VR under task-oriented versus imitation-oriented instruction conditions, with shoulder muscle activity and kinematics recorded; findings provided design guidance for VR exercise applications differentiating when each movement-instruction paradigm best supports rehabilitation goals.
seminal_1: Rose, T., Nam, C. S., & Chen, K. B. (2018). Immersion of virtual reality for rehabilitation – Review. Applied Ergonomics, 69, 153–161. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2018.01.009
seminal_1_synopsis: A systematic review of 18 studies synthesising evidence on how VR immersion level, enjoyment, and haptic feedback affect motor-recovery outcomes in rehabilitation; with over 560 citations it has become a primary reference for human factors researchers designing and evaluating VR-based rehabilitative systems.
seminal_2: Chen, K. B., Savage, A. B., Chourasia, A. O., Wiegmann, D. A., & Sesto, M. E. (2013). Touch screen performance by individuals with and without motor control disabilities. Applied Ergonomics, 44(2), 297–302.

expertise_overview: Human performance in virtual and mixed reality, accessible human-computer interaction, occupational safety. VR immersion level and embodiment effects on performance, presence, rehabilitation outcomes; opposite-gender avatar embodiment temporarily reduces implicit gender bias; task-oriented versus imitation-oriented movement instruction for VR exercise design via shoulder muscle activity and kinematics. Immersion, enjoyment, and haptic feedback for motor-recovery outcomes in VR rehabilitation. Motor-control disabilities alter touchscreen accuracy, speed, and error patterns, establishing accessible touch-interface design benchmarks. Gestural interaction in XR, anthropometry and headset design to mitigate eye strain, workplace robot-injury patterns from stationary and mobile industrial robots from OSHA surveillance data.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence: Karen Boru Chen is a single, identifiable researcher at NC State University (ORCID/Google Scholar: btGMySgAAAAJ) with publications in Human Factors, Applied Ergonomics, and HFES proceedings — all core Human Factors journal venues. The 2024 Human Factors paper (Wu & Chen) was e-published December 2022 but appears in the 2024 print volume (66:5); citation counts are from Google Scholar as of mid-2026. The Applied Ergonomics robot-injury paper (Sanders et al., 2024) article number 104324 is confirmed on ScienceDirect; the exact volume and page range were not retrieved due to paywall but the article number is accurate. The 2013 Applied Ergonomics touchscreen paper volume and issue (44:2, 297–302) are from Google Scholar metadata and should be verified against the journal record before formal use.

name: Chowdhury, Suman
recent_1: Wei, L. H., Paulon, G. M., Sarker, P., & Chowdhury, S. K. (2025). Sustained-till-exhaustion effects of firefighter helmets on neck muscle fatigue mechanism. Ergonomics. https://doi.org/10.1080/00140139.2025.2548004

recent_2: Bahreinizad, H., Paulon, G. M., Wei, L., & Chowdhury, S. K. (2025). An MRI-derived head-neck finite element model. Biomechanics and Modeling in Mechanobiology, 24(6), 2195–2215. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10237-025-02013-x
recent_2_synopsis: The authors developed and validated a biofidelic finite element model of the head and cervical spine built from MRI geometry, incorporating scalp, skull, CSF, brain, meninges, vertebrae, discs, 14 ligaments, and 42 neck muscles. The model was validated against four published experimental datasets (NBDL high-acceleration, Ito cervical, Alshareef brain sonomicrometry, Nahum impact) and provides a computational tool for studying traumatic brain injury mechanisms.
recent_3: Wei, L. H., Sudeesh, S., Chakroborty, S., & Chowdhury, S. K. (2025). A comprehensive methodological framework for 3D head anthropometric shape modeling of a small dataset. Ergonomics. https://doi.org/10.1080/00140139.2025.2518306
recent_3_synopsis: This paper proposes a six-step framework combining K-means clustering with Cubic Spline least-squares surface modeling to characterize 3D head shapes from a sample of 36 firefighters. The validated shapes were benchmarked against NIOSH, ANSUR II, CAESAR, and US Army databases, offering a scalable method for designing well-fitted head-mounted protective equipment and VR headsets for specialized occupational groups.
seminal_1: Nabatilan, L. B., Aghazadeh, F., Nimbarte, A. D., Harvey, C. C., & Chowdhury, S. K. (2012). Effect of driving experience on visual behavior and driving performance under different driving conditions. Cognition, Technology & Work, 14(4), 355–363. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-011-0184-5
seminal_1_synopsis: This study used eye-tracking to compare visual scanning strategies and driving error across novice and experienced drivers during simple and complex tasks (including cell phone use), demonstrating that experience shifts gaze from dashboard monitoring to forward roadway attention. With over 100 citations, it is among Chowdhury's most-cited works and is influential in the driving performance and visual attention literature within human factors.
seminal_2: Chowdhury, S. K., Nimbarte, A. D., Jaridi, M., & Creese, R. C. (2013). Discrete wavelet transform analysis of surface electromyography for the fatigue assessment of neck and shoulder muscles. Journal of Electromyography and Kinesiology, 23(5), 995–1003. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelekin.2013.05.001
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper demonstrated that discrete wavelet transform (DWT) decomposition of surface EMG signals outperforms traditional Fourier-based frequency analysis for detecting localized muscle fatigue in the neck and shoulder, providing a more temporally sensitive and robust assessment method. With over 100 citations, it is Chowdhury's most-cited first-authored work and is widely referenced in occupational biomechanics and EMG methodology literature.
expertise_overview: Occupational biomechanics, musculoskeletal fatigue assessment, protective equipment design. Neck strength and endurance, lumbar facet joint kinematics during dynamic lifting, helmet effects on neck muscle fatigue (US-style versus European-style helmets impose differing muscular demand and fatigue onset via EMG amplitude and frequency metrics). Discrete wavelet transform decomposition of surface EMG outperforms Fourier-based analysis for detecting localized neck and shoulder muscle fatigue. Biofidelic finite-element head and cervical spine models (brain, meninges, vertebrae, discs, ligaments, neck muscles) for traumatic brain injury mechanisms; anthropometric shape modeling combining K-means clustering and spline surface fitting for helmet and PPE design. Eye-tracking of novice versus experienced driver visual scanning, physics-based VR for human-robot collaboration.
notes: Identification is high-confidence. Suman K. Chowdhury (also publishing as SK Chowdhury) is clearly the person matching this journal role: he holds a Ph.D. in Industrial Engineering with a Biomechanics and Human Factors Engineering specialization (West Virginia University, 2016), has published directly in Human Factors journal (two confirmed papers: neck strength/endurance 2022 and lumbar facet kinematics 2018), and is active in the HFES community (multiple HFES Annual Meeting proceedings papers). He joined the University of Florida ISE department in August 2024 after positions at Texas Tech University (2019–2024) and Texas A&M (2017–2019). The PR (Past/Provisional Reviewer) role is consistent with his career stage and direct publication record in the journal. No other Suman Chowdhury with an HF/ergonomics profile was identified.

name: Cooke, Nancy
recent_1: Cooke, N. J., Cohen, M. C., Fazio, W. C., Inderberg, L. H., Johnson, C. J., Lematta, G. J., Peel, M., & Teo, A. (2024). From Teams to Teamness: Future Directions in the Science of Team Cognition. Human Factors, 66(6), 1669–1680. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208231162449
recent_1_synopsis: This paper diagnoses critical limitations in existing team cognition theories, laboratory paradigms, and measures as modern teams grow more distributed, heterogeneous, and AI-augmented. It advances a 'teamness' framework that reconceptualizes team cognition as a continuous, interaction-embedded process rather than a fixed structural property, pointing toward new measurement and design approaches.
recent_2: Johnson, C. J., Demir, M., McNeese, N. J., Gorman, J. C., Wolff, A. T., & Cooke, N. J. (2023). The Impact of Training on Human–Autonomy Team Communications and Trust Calibration. Human Factors, 65(7), 1554–1570. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208211047323
recent_2_synopsis: This experimental study examined how targeted training interventions affect communication patterns and trust calibration in human-autonomy teams operating in a synthetic UAV task environment. Results showed that training shaped both the content of team communications and the degree to which human operators appropriately calibrated their trust in autonomous teammates, with implications for HAT training design.
recent_3: Rayo, M. F., Cooke, N. J., Flach, J. M., Hoffman, R. R., Smith, P. J., & Woods, D. D. (2023). Cognitive Systems Engineering at 40, Part I: Deriving Theory from Practice. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 67(1), 183–186. https://doi.org/10.1177/21695067231193648
recent_3_synopsis: This invited panel, marking 40 years since Hollnagel and Woods' foundational CSE paper, reflects on how theory has emerged from practitioners working in complex joint cognitive systems. Cooke and co-authors trace how CSE's core concepts—resilience, distributed cognition, and naturalistic decision-making—matured through applied work in healthcare, aviation, and military command-and-control domains.
seminal_1: Cooke, N. J., Salas, E., Cannon-Bowers, J. A., & Stout, R. J. (2000). Measuring Team Knowledge. Human Factors, 42(1), 151–173. https://doi.org/10.1518/001872000779656561
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper provided the first systematic framework for mapping knowledge-elicitation methods onto different facets of team knowledge—shared mental models, transactive memory, and team situation models—thereby establishing the psychometric scaffolding for the entire field of team cognition measurement. With over 1,000 citations, it remains the foundational methodological reference for researchers measuring cognitive states in teams.
seminal_2: Cooke, N. J., Gorman, J. C., Myers, C. W., & Duran, J. L. (2013). Interactive Team Cognition. Cognitive Science, 37(2), 255–285. https://doi.org/10.1111/cogs.12009
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper challenged the dominant shared-mental-models paradigm by proposing Interactive Team Cognition (ITC) theory, which holds that team cognition is an activity rather than a stored product, must be measured at the team level, and is inseparable from the taskwork context. Cited nearly 1,000 times, ITC reoriented theoretical and empirical work toward process-based, dynamic measurement of team coordination and situation awareness.
expertise_overview: Teams and macrocognition, human-robot and human-automation interaction, cognitive systems engineering. Interactive Team Cognition theory, reconceptualizing team cognition as an activity measured at the team level and inseparable from taskwork rather than a stored shared mental model; continuous interaction-embedded 'teamness' framework for distributed, heterogeneous, AI-augmented teams. Methods mapping knowledge-elicitation techniques onto shared mental models, transactive memory, and team situation models, providing the psychometric foundation for measuring team knowledge, team situation awareness, coordination. Human-autonomy teaming in remotely piloted aircraft, cybersecurity, emergency response; how training shapes communication patterns and trust calibration toward autonomous teammates. Resilience, distributed cognition, and naturalistic decision-making extended into human-AI team design and communication-based workload measurement.
notes: Identification is unambiguous: Nancy J. Cooke (Ph.D. 1987, New Mexico State University) is one of the most prominent figures in the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society — she is a Past President of HFES, served as the first female editor of Human Factors, and currently serves as an Associate Editor (AE) of the journal, matching the supplied role code. All citations are drawn from confirmed search results and publisher pages (SAGE Journals, ASU Pure, Google Scholar, ResearchGate, PubMed); no DOIs or details have been invented. The 2023 HFES Proceedings paper is included as the third recent publication because the two strongest 2023–2025 peer-reviewed journal articles in Human Factors are included first; a 2026 in-press Human Factors review on team workload (Johnson, Gutzwiller, Cohen, Cooke) was confirmed as accepted/in press but lacks finalized volume/issue/page data and is therefore omitted to preserve citation accuracy.

name: Czaja, Sara J.
recent_1: Czaja, S. J., Sharit, J., Pirolli, P., Hernandez, M., & Moxley, J. (2026). The Benefits of a Digital Exercise Intervention for Older Adults: Findings From the Fittle Randomized Controlled Trial. Journal of Applied Gerontology. https://doi.org/10.1177/07334648261419290
recent_1_synopsis: This randomized controlled trial evaluated the Fittle digital exercise platform designed for older adults, examining whether a tailored interactive exercise system improves physical and functional outcomes. The study contributes direct evidence on user-centered design of technology-mediated health interventions for aging populations.
recent_2: Czaja, S. J., Charness, N., Rogers, W. A., Sharit, J., Moxley, J. H., & Boot, W. R. (2024). The Benefits of Technology for Engaging Aging Adults: Findings From the PRISM 2.0 Trial. Innovation in Aging, 8(6), igae042. https://doi.org/10.1093/geroni/igae042
recent_2_synopsis: This randomized controlled trial of the PRISM 2.0 tablet-based software system tested whether a purpose-designed platform could reduce loneliness and social isolation among 245 older adults in rural, senior housing, and assisted living settings, finding that tablet access with training support significantly improved these outcomes regardless of condition.
