expertise_overview: Human-computer interaction, healthcare human factors, and human-automation interaction. 3D gestural and embodied HCI: interface design, usability-accuracy tradeoffs, workload effects of prolonged exposure, and infection prevention, showing contactless gestural interaction spreads contamination to fewer surfaces than touchscreens while incurring longer process times, and establishing systematic methodology for gestural interfaces in the high-stakes anesthesia context across expert and novice users. Physiological measurement, EEG, ECG, and respiration, with hierarchical Bayesian methods to quantify pilot mental workload and support adaptive automation from small samples. How levels of automation and reliability affect performance, workload, and situation awareness in hybrid shared-control systems. Sociotechnical and resilience engineering relating operating room layout to nurse workflow, flow disruptions, and team safety.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Jurewicz is an Assistant Professor at Oklahoma State University (School of Industrial Engineering and Management) with a Google Scholar profile (user=IWJrZdcAAAAJ) and an OSU faculty page. Her role as a Past/Provisional Reviewer (PR) for Human Factors is consistent with her 2018 publication in that journal and her active publication of HFES annual meeting proceedings. Citation counts are from her Google Scholar profile as of mid-2026 (162 total citations, h-index 6). The 2026 Journal of Infection Prevention paper (Dowers & Jurewicz) and the 2026 Aerospace Medicine and Human Performance paper (Rouser, Rock, Paul, & Jurewicz on EEG and spatial disorientation) are the two most recent confirmed peer-reviewed journal articles; the latter could not be fully verified beyond the Scholar listing and was excluded from the recent-3 list in favor of papers with confirmed DOIs. The HFES 2025 proceedings paper on hybrid automation systems (Uba et al.) was included as a peer-reviewed conference paper. The 2019 Applied Ergonomics paper is listed under Neyens et al. because Jurewicz appears as a non-first author, but it is the most-cited work associated with her record.

name: Kim, Ji-Eun
recent_1: Prendez, D., Li, J., Higgins, E. A., & Kim, J.-E. (2026). Toward sustainable interventions for enhancing vigilance: A scoping review. Applied Ergonomics, 131, 104628.
recent_1_synopsis: This scoping review systematically maps the evidence base for interventions targeting occupational vigilance decrement, identifying what works sustainably across high-stakes domains including healthcare, transportation, and air traffic control. It is Ji-Eun Kim's most recent peer-reviewed journal article and directly underpins her NSF CAREER program on mitigating vigilance decrement in clinical environments.
recent_2: Li, J., Huang, S., & Kim, J.-E. (2025). Predicting multitasking performance: An EEG- and eye movement-based dynamic Bayesian network. International Journal of Human–Computer Interaction, 1–11. https://doi.org/10.1080/10447318.2025.2480882
recent_2_synopsis: This paper develops a real-time predictive model of multitasking performance using concurrent EEG and eye-tracking signals fed into a dynamic Bayesian network, enabling prospective detection of performance decrements before errors occur. The work advances neurophysiological human performance modeling for safety-critical settings such as air traffic control and emergency response.
recent_3: Jónsdóttir, A. A., Kang, Z., Sun, T., Mandal, S., & Kim, J.-E. (2023). The effects of language barriers and time constraints on online learning performance: An eye-tracking study. Human Factors, 65(5), 779–791. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208211010949
recent_3_synopsis: Using eye-tracking, this study quantified how English-as-a-second-language status and time pressure jointly degrade self-directed online learning performance, identifying gaze patterns that distinguish EFL from ESL learners under load. It is Kim's highest-profile publication in the flagship Human Factors journal and is among her most-cited recent articles.
seminal_1: Kim, J.-E., Nembhard, D. A., & Kim, J. H. (2016). The effects of group size and task complexity on deadline reactivity. International Journal of Industrial Ergonomics, 56, 106–114.
seminal_1_synopsis: This early empirical study established how teams of different sizes respond to imminent deadlines as a function of task complexity, contributing foundational findings on time-pressure dynamics and group work pacing. With roughly 26 citations it is among Kim's most-cited works and anchored the individual-differences and temporal motivation thread that defines much of her subsequent research program.
seminal_2: Kim, J.-E., Kessler, L., McCauley, Z., Niiyama, I., & Boyle, L. N. (2020). Human factors considerations in designing a personalized wearable dialysis device: An interview study. Applied Ergonomics, 85, 103003.
seminal_2_synopsis: Through structured interviews with patients and caregivers, this paper identified the human factors requirements — usability, personalization, monitoring, and mobility — that must be met for a wearable artificial kidney to succeed clinically. With approximately 25 citations it seeded a sustained healthcare-HF research stream and is frequently co-cited by the nephrology engineering community studying wearable dialysis technology.
expertise_overview: Vigilance and sustained attention; wearable neurophysiological sensors (EEG, electrodermal activity, eye-tracking, heart rate) feeding dynamic Bayesian network models that predict and mitigate vigilance decrement in healthcare, transportation, and air traffic control. Real-time models detecting cognitive workload and multitasking performance decrements from concurrent EEG and eye-tracking signals before errors occur. Time-pressure reactivity, procrastination, and temporal motivation as individual-difference constructs shaping operator and team performance under deadlines, including task complexity and group size effects. Human-centered design of medical devices (wearable artificial kidney/dialysis usability requirements); language status and time pressure in self-directed online learning; interventions for occupational vigilance decrement and personalized, sensor-driven mitigation.
notes: Identification is confident: Ji-Eun Kim (jikim@uw.edu) is an Associate Professor in the Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering at the University of Washington, Seattle, with a PhD from Penn State and an MS in Experimental Psychology from Korea University. She is Faculty Advisor for the UW HFES Chapter and has published in Human Factors, Applied Ergonomics, and the HFES Annual Meeting Proceedings — all squarely within the Human Factors journal reviewer pool. No other Ji-Eun Kim in HF/ergonomics/HCI was found. Publication details were verified against her official HAS Lab website (sites.google.com/uw.edu/has/publications), her personal CV site (sites.google.com/view/jieunkim/cv), PubMed, and SAGE Journals. Citation counts for seminal works are approximate (drawn from Google Scholar as of June 2026) and the 2025 IJHCI paper page range (1–11) reflects an online-first publication without final volume/issue numbers yet assigned.

name: Kirlik, Alexander
recent_1: Lee, J., Rodriguez, S. S., Natarrajan, R., Chen, J., Deep, H., & Kirlik, A. (2021). What's This? A Voice and Touch Multimodal Approach for Ambiguity Resolution in Voice Assistants. Proceedings of the 2021 ACM International Conference on Multimodal Interaction (ICMI '21), Montréal, QC, Canada, pp. 512–520. https://doi.org/10.1145/3462244.3479902
recent_1_synopsis: This paper proposes a multimodal voice-plus-touch interaction technique to resolve the inherent ambiguity in voice assistant queries, allowing users to point at physical objects to disambiguate spoken commands. The approach demonstrates improved task efficiency and user satisfaction compared to voice-only alternatives in an empirical study.
recent_2: Park, J. S., Barber, R., Kirlik, A., & Karahalios, K. (2019). A Slow Algorithm Improves Users' Assessments of the Algorithm's Accuracy. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 3(CSCW), Article 102. https://doi.org/10.1145/3359204
recent_2_synopsis: This study shows that deliberately slowing an algorithm's apparent response time improves users' calibration of their trust in it: participants tracked high-quality algorithms more faithfully and were more skeptical of low-quality ones when responses were delayed. The finding challenges standard UX orthodoxy that speed is always beneficial, with direct implications for human-AI teaming and decision support design.
recent_3: Lin, J. H., Kirlik, A., & Xu, X. (2018). New technologies in human factors and ergonomics research and practice. Applied Ergonomics, 66, 179–181. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2017.08.012
recent_3_synopsis: This editorial introduction to a special issue maps the landscape of emerging technologies — including VR, wearables, and computational modeling — entering human factors and ergonomics research and practice, and articulates the methodological opportunities and challenges they create for the field.
seminal_1: Kirlik, A. (1993). Modeling strategic behavior in human-automation interaction: Why an 'aid' can (and should) go unused. Human Factors, 35(2), 221–242. https://doi.org/10.1177/001872089303500203
seminal_1_synopsis: Using sensitivity analysis and process modeling, Kirlik demonstrated that operators rationally choose not to use automation aids when the cognitive cost of monitoring and managing the aid exceeds its benefit — a finding that reframed the 'disuse of automation' problem from operator error to system design failure. This paper (281+ citations) became foundational to the human-automation interaction literature and continues to anchor debates on complacency, over-trust, and the design of decision aids.
seminal_2: Kirlik, A., Miller, R. A., & Jagacinski, R. J. (1993). Supervisory control in a dynamic and uncertain environment: A process model of skilled human-environment interaction. IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics, 23(4), 929–952.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper presented a closed-loop process model of how skilled operators manage multitask supervisory control under environmental uncertainty, integrating control theory with cognitive psychology to characterize expert-novice differences. With 139+ citations it established an ecological, control-theoretic framework for understanding human supervisory behavior that influenced subsequent work on situation awareness, adaptive automation, and operator performance modeling.
expertise_overview: Cognitive engineering and ecological approaches to human factors: human-automation interaction, trust calibration, supervisory control, adaptive behavior, decision support, and cognitive modeling of operator performance in aviation, emergency management, and sociotechnical systems. Brunswikian lens-model approaches to judgment and decision making under uncertainty connecting ecological psychology to human factors methodology. Showed via sensitivity analysis and process modeling that operators rationally disuse automation when monitoring costs exceed benefits, reframing disuse from operator error to system design failure and anchoring complacency/over-trust debates. Closed-loop, control-theoretic process model of multitask supervisory control under uncertainty characterizing expert-novice differences. Human-AI teaming, algorithm aversion and adoption, trust calibration through deliberate response delay, and multimodal voice-plus-touch disambiguation of voice-assistant commands.
notes: Alexander Kirlik died on October 8, 2025, at age 64, so he is a deceased member of the reviewer pool. His most recent confirmed first- or co-authored peer-reviewed publication is from 2021 (ICMI); no 2023–2026 journal articles by Kirlik as an author (rather than as a dissertation advisor) were identified in searches. The 2021 ICMI paper and the 2019 CSCW paper are confirmed via ACM DL and Illinois Experts. The 2018 Applied Ergonomics item is an editorial introduction rather than a full research article but is peer-reviewed and the most recent solo/senior work found. The Google Scholar profile cited for citation counts is user=ZS-2t_AAAAAJ. The 1993 Human Factors paper's DOI is confirmed via PubMed (PMID 8349287). The IEEE SMC 1993 paper volume/page details (vol. 23, pp. 929–952) are confirmed via Semantic Scholar and IEEE Xplore record 247880.

name: Laux, Lila F.
recent_1: Laux, L. F. (2019). Human factors in accident analysis. In R. W. McLay & R. N. Anderson (Eds.), Engineering Standards for Forensic Application (Chapter 24). Elsevier.
recent_1_synopsis: This book chapter provides a practitioner-oriented framework for applying human factors principles to forensic accident analysis, covering cognitive, perceptual, and behavioral science dimensions alongside ergonomic considerations. It serves as a reference for expert-witness and litigation contexts where human error and system design are at issue.
recent_2: Wickens, C. D., Hutchins, S. D., Laux, L., & Sebok, A. (2015). The impact of sleep disruption on complex cognitive tasks: A meta-analysis. Human Factors, 57(6), 930–946. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720815571935
recent_2_synopsis: This meta-analysis synthesized empirical research across three categories of sleep disruption—total deprivation, sleep restriction, and circadian disruption—to quantify their effects on complex cognitive task performance. A key finding was that complex cognitive tasks may be less severely impaired by sleep disruption than simple tasks, with direct implications for safety-critical professions such as aviation, healthcare, and transportation.
recent_3: Sebok, A., Laux, L., & Wickens, C. (2015). Supporting human-automation interaction in the rail industry by applying lessons from aviation. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society 59th Annual Meeting.
recent_3_synopsis: This paper transferred knowledge from aviation human-automation interaction research—particularly lessons around flight management system mode complexity and mode transitions—to the design of Positive Train Control (PTC) automation in locomotive cabs. It revisited Bainbridge's 'Ironies of Automation' and argued that aviation experience offers actionable guidance for mitigating operator error with emerging rail automation.
seminal_1: Wickens, C. D., Hutchins, S. D., Laux, L., & Sebok, A. (2015). The impact of sleep disruption on complex cognitive tasks: A meta-analysis. Human Factors, 57(6), 930–946. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720815571935
seminal_1_synopsis: This is the most widely cited peer-reviewed work confirmed for Laux; it established a quantitative evidence base distinguishing how different forms of sleep disruption affect complex versus simple cognitive task performance, and is heavily referenced in safety-critical operations research. Its publication in the flagship Human Factors journal amplified its influence across aviation, rail, healthcare, and military human factors communities.
seminal_2: Muller, M. J., Wharton, C., McIver, W. J., Jr., & Laux, L. (1997). Toward an HCI research and practice agenda based on human needs and social responsibility. Proceedings of the ACM CHI 97 Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (pp. 155–161). ACM.
seminal_2_synopsis: This CHI paper made an early case for reorienting human-computer interaction research around broader human needs and social responsibility rather than narrow usability metrics, situating HCI within a framework of societal impact. It has been cited in subsequent work on social justice in HCI and the ethics of user-centered design, representing Laux's contribution to expanding the scope of human factors beyond task performance.
expertise_overview: Warning and risk communication: product safety labels, hazard perception, severity messaging; displays, controls, and human-system communication. Human-automation interaction in transportation, transferring aviation lessons on flight-management mode complexity, mode transitions, and automation complacency to rail Positive Train Control design and revisiting the ironies of automation. Meta-analysis synthesizing total sleep deprivation, sleep restriction, and circadian disruption to quantify effects on complex versus simple cognitive task performance, finding complex tasks may be less severely impaired, with implications for fatigue, workload, and sustained attention in aviation, healthcare, and military operations. Forensic human factors frameworks for accident and human-error analysis; HCI accessibility for people with disabilities; aging-related driving differences; reorienting HCI toward broader human needs and social responsibility.
notes: Identification is confident: Laux is explicitly listed by SAGE Publications as an author affiliated with "Human Factors Consulting, USA" and is confirmed as an Editorial Board member of Human Factors journal. Her ResearchGate profile (46 publications, ~980 citations) and expert-witness profiler records confirm her identity as a human factors PhD from Rice University. The most recent publication confirmed is the 2019 book chapter; no peer-reviewed journal articles from 2020–2026 were surfaced through web search, which is consistent with her shift toward consulting and expert-witness practice. The sleep disruption meta-analysis (Wickens et al., 2015) is listed as both a recent and seminal work given it is the most highly cited confirmed peer-reviewed paper and falls within the decade preceding the knowledge cutoff. Warning-label conference papers from 1989–1991 (with Laughery et al.) are early and foundational contributions but were published only in HFES Annual Meeting Proceedings, not peer-reviewed journals, so they are not listed as seminal; the CHI 1997 paper is used instead as the second seminal work. Citation counts are not precisely confirmed; Semantic Scholar and ResearchGate pages returned 403 errors during retrieval.

name: Lavender, Steven A.
recent_1: Lavender, S.A., Sommerich, C.M., & Kachlan, A. (2025). A field test of the EMS LiftKit: Validating its usability, usefulness, and desirability. Applied Ergonomics, 127, 104519.
recent_1_synopsis: Sixteen LiftKit sets were deployed across five EMS organizations over a 12-month real-world trial; survey and interview data confirmed that the seven patient-handling tools received strong usability and usefulness ratings, particularly for use in confined spaces, establishing ecological validity for the device beyond the laboratory.
recent_2: Lavender, S.A., Sommerich, C.M., & Kachlan, A. (2025). Identifying ergonomics practices currently used by grocery distribution centers. Applied Ergonomics, 125, 104440.
recent_2_synopsis: Through interviews with 97 managers at 30 U.S. grocery distribution centers, the study catalogued 44 distinct ergonomic practices organized across six categories (facility setup, warehouse management systems, order selector tools, full-case picking, organizational support, and piece-pick hazard reduction), creating an evidence base that practitioners can benchmark against to reduce musculoskeletal disorder rates.
recent_3: Lavender, S.A., Charbonnet, J., & Sommerich, C.M. (2023). Biomechanical assessment of alternative hand trucks for transporting heavy loads up and down stairs. Applied Ergonomics, 110, 104010.
recent_3_synopsis: EMG data from nine experienced delivery workers moving a 52.3 kg washing machine on stairs showed that a powered two-speed hand truck significantly reduced erector spinae, trapezius, and biceps muscle activation compared to conventional and multi-wheeled designs, providing biomechanical evidence to guide occupational equipment selection.
seminal_1: Marras, W.S., Lavender, S.A., Leurgans, S.E., Rajulu, S.L., Allread, W.G., Fathallah, F.A., & Ferguson, S.A. (1993). The role of dynamic three-dimensional trunk motion in occupationally-related low back disorders: The effects of workplace factors, trunk position, and trunk motion characteristics on risk of injury. Spine, 18(5), 617–628.
seminal_1_synopsis: Analysis of more than 400 industrial lifting jobs across 48 industries identified five biomechanical risk factors — lifting frequency, load moment, trunk lateral velocity, trunk twisting velocity, and trunk sagittal angle — whose combination predicted low back disorder risk more than three times better than existing lifting guidelines at the time; this landmark epidemiologic-biomechanics study became a foundational reference for occupational risk assessment and the development of dynamic spinal loading models.
seminal_2: Lavender, S.A., Mirka, G.A., Schoenmarklin, R.W., Sommerich, C.M., Sudhakar, L.R., & Marras, W.S. (1989). The effects of preview and task symmetry on trunk muscle response to sudden loading. Human Factors, 31(1), 101–115.
seminal_2_synopsis: Using EMG from eight trunk muscles while subjects caught falling weights under four levels of advance warning (0–400 ms) and symmetric vs. asymmetric postures, the study demonstrated linear relationships between preview time and peak/mean muscle activation, and showed that asymmetric posture substantially altered preparatory muscle recruitment; this paper established foundational principles about how anticipation and body orientation govern spinal injury risk during unexpected loading events.
expertise_overview: Occupational ergonomics and biomechanics: musculoskeletal loading during manual material handling, prospectively validated risk models for work-related low back disorders, and ergonomic interventions across EMS providers, grocery distribution workers, healthcare workers, and firefighters. Analyzed over 400 industrial lifting jobs to identify five biomechanical risk factors—lifting frequency, load moment, trunk lateral velocity, trunk twisting velocity, and trunk sagittal angle—whose combination predicted low back disorder risk far better than existing guidelines, a foundational reference for dynamic spinal loading models. EMG evidence on how advance warning time and symmetric versus asymmetric posture govern preparatory muscle recruitment and spinal injury risk during unexpected loading. EMG and field evaluation of powered hand trucks and patient-handling tools, catalogued distribution-center ergonomic practices, and organizational barriers and facilitators to intervention adoption.
notes: Identity is highly confident: Steven A. Lavender, PhD, CPE is unambiguously the OSU occupational biomechanics/ergonomics researcher with extensive HFES involvement, including publications in Human Factors dating to 1989 and fellow status in HFES. The 1993 Spine paper is a multi-author work with Marras as lead; Lavender is a key co-author and the study is routinely attributed to the Marras/Lavender OSU group. The 1989 Human Factors paper has Lavender as first author. The two 2025 Applied Ergonomics papers were confirmed via PubMed (PMID 39652982 for grocery DC paper; ScienceDirect article pii/S0003687025000559 for EMS LiftKit). Citation counts for seminal works were not retrieved numerically, but both papers appear repeatedly in the occupational biomechanics literature as foundational references. No fabricated DOIs or details have been introduced.

name: Lee, John D.
recent_1: Chiou, E. K., & Lee, J. D. (2023). Trusting automation: Designing for responsivity and resilience. Human Factors, 65(1), 137–165. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208211009995
recent_1_synopsis: This review critiques the dominant 'trust calibration' paradigm for neglecting the relational and system-level aspects of increasingly capable automation. The authors propose a four-concept framework—situation, semiotics, strategy, and sequence—to guide research and design toward more responsive and resilient human–automation systems.
recent_2: Kim, J. Y., Marshall, V. D., Rowell, B., Chen, Q., Zheng, Y., Lee, J. D., Kontar, R. A., Lester, C., & Yang, X. J. (2025). The effects of presenting AI uncertainty information on pharmacists' trust in automated pill recognition technology: Exploratory mixed subjects study. JMIR Human Factors, 12, e60273. https://doi.org/10.2196/60273
recent_2_synopsis: A simulated medication-verification study with 30 licensed pharmacists found that displaying AI uncertainty visualizations significantly shaped trust dynamics, revealing a negativity bias: trust declined more steeply after AI errors than it rose after correct recommendations, with implications for transparent healthcare AI design.
recent_3: Rheem, H., Lee, J., Lee, J. D., Szczerba, J. F., & Tsimhoni, O. (2025). Explaining automated vehicle behavior at an appropriate abstraction level and timescale to maintain common ground. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 19(2), 174–199. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434251318477
recent_3_synopsis: The paper argues that fixed-format explainable-AI approaches fail drivers because they ignore how knowledge level and task timescale change what abstraction of explanation is useful. A multilevel common-ground framework is proposed to guide adaptive in-vehicle explanations for highly automated vehicles.
seminal_1: Lee, J. D., & See, K. A. (2004). Trust in automation: Designing for appropriate reliance. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 46(1), 50–80. https://doi.org/10.1518/hfes.46.1.50_30392
seminal_1_synopsis: This review synthesized organizational, psychological, and neurological perspectives on trust to explain why people over- or under-rely on automation, and advanced the concept of 'appropriate reliance' as the key design target. With more than 9,000 Google Scholar citations, it is the defining theoretical framework for human–automation trust research across virtually every application domain.
seminal_2: Lee, J., & Moray, N. (1992). Trust, control strategies and allocation of function in human-machine systems. Ergonomics, 35(10), 1243–1270. https://doi.org/10.1080/00140139208967392
seminal_2_synopsis: Using a simulated pasteurization plant, this empirical study characterized how operators' trust dynamically shifted during interactions with automation and showed that trust drives manual-versus-automatic control-mode choices, with failures depressing trust more than successes build it. The study initiated the empirical trust-in-automation research agenda and has accumulated more than 2,000 citations, establishing the asymmetric trust-decay finding that subsequent work continues to replicate and extend.
expertise_overview: Automation and autonomy, trust and appropriate reliance, attention and mental workload, especially driving and transportation safety, extending to human-AI teaming, situation awareness, and adaptive interface design across highly automated vehicles, unmanned aerial vehicles, maritime operations, space exploration, and healthcare. Synthesized organizational, psychological, and neurological perspectives on trust to explain over- and under-reliance on automation and advanced appropriate reliance as the central design target, the defining theoretical framework for human-automation trust. Characterized via simulated process-control studies how trust dynamically shifts and drives manual-versus-automatic control choices, establishing the asymmetric finding that failures depress trust more than successes build it. Critiques trust calibration via a situation-semiotics-strategy-sequence framework, AI uncertainty visualization and negativity bias in healthcare AI, and multilevel common-ground frameworks for adaptive in-vehicle explanations.
notes: Identification is confident: John D. Lee at the University of Wisconsin–Madison is unambiguously the person who serves as Associate Editor of Human Factors journal. He is listed as an author on the SAGE Publications author page and has published multiple articles in Human Factors. The 2023 Chiou & Lee paper was published online in 2021 (accepted) but appears in the 2023 print volume (65(1)); citation reflects the print year. The 2025 Rheem et al. paper is confirmed published in Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making (SAGE), volume 19, issue 2. The 1992 Lee & Moray paper citation (Ergonomics, 35(10), 1243–1270) is confirmed via DOI lookup. Citation counts are approximate as of mid-2025 per Google Scholar.

name: Li, Mengyao
recent_1: Li, M., & Lee, J. D. (2025). Purpose outweighs performance: Trust drops more when AI teammates fail to cooperate, but explanations can repair it. International Journal of Human–Computer Interaction. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2025.2539449
recent_1_synopsis: Using a cooperative resource-allocation game, this study disentangles the effects of AI performance failures versus intent/cooperation failures on trust, finding that violations of cooperative purpose produce steeper trust drops than performance errors. The work further shows that apology-plus-explanation strategies can partially repair trust, with implications for human-AI team design in safety-critical settings.
recent_2: Rojas, E., Hsu, D., Huang, J., & Li, M. (2025). Interpersonal influence matters: Trust contagion and repair in human-human-AI team. Computers in Human Behavior: Artificial Humans. DOI: 10.1016/S2949882125000787
recent_2_synopsis: This mixed-design experiment showed that trust in an AI teammate is socially contagious — exposure to a high-trusting human confederate elevated participants' own AI trust and reliance, while negative contagion selectively reduced over-reliance. The asymmetry of positive versus negative trust contagion identifies social influence as a practical lever for trust calibration in multi-human-AI teams.
recent_3: Li, M., & Rojas, E. (2025). Less trust, more mirroring words: Lexical alignment as a response to uncertainty in human-AI teams. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251357897
recent_3_synopsis: In a three-player human-AI resource-allocation game, participants paired with a low-trusting confederate exhibited significantly greater lexical mirroring toward the AI, revealing that reduced trust triggers increased linguistic alignment as an uncertainty-management strategy. The finding connects conversational behavior to trust dynamics, opening a pathway for real-time unobtrusive trust sensing.
seminal_1: Li, M., Holthausen, B. E., Stuck, R. E., & Walker, B. N. (2019). No risk no trust: Investigating perceived risk in highly automated driving. Proceedings of the 11th International Conference on Automotive User Interfaces and Interactive Vehicular Applications (AutomotiveUI '19), Utrecht, Netherlands. (Best Paper Award)
seminal_1_synopsis: This two-study paper established that perceived risk is a necessary precondition for trust formation in highly automated driving — when automation eliminates apparent risk, drivers lack the evaluative basis to calibrate trust. With ~138 citations it is Li's most-cited work and has influenced subsequent models of trust in autonomous vehicles by foregrounding risk perception as a mediating variable alongside reliability.
seminal_2: Li, M., Erickson, I. M., Cross, E. V., & Lee, J. D. (2023). It's not only what you say, but also how you say it: Machine learning approach to estimate trust from conversation. Human Factors, 66(6), 1724–1741. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231166624
seminal_2_synopsis: Using a random forest trained on combined lexical and acoustic features from human-agent dialogues, this study explained 71% of trust variance — demonstrating that prosodic cues (formants, MFCCs) carry trust information beyond word content alone. Published in the flagship Human Factors journal, it established a practical, unobtrusive pipeline for continuous trust measurement in conversational human-autonomy teams and is her most-cited journal article (~29 citations).
expertise_overview: Trust in automation and human-AI/robot teaming: trust dynamics, calibration, and repair in safety-critical sociotechnical systems, combining machine learning, dynamic systems modeling, epistemic network analysis, and behavioral experiments. Disentangled AI performance failures from intent and cooperation failures, finding violations of cooperative purpose produce steeper trust drops and that apology-plus-explanation strategies partially repair trust. Showed trust in AI teammates is socially contagious with asymmetric positive versus negative effects, and that reduced trust triggers increased lexical mirroring toward AI as an uncertainty-management strategy, enabling unobtrusive real-time trust sensing. Established perceived risk as a necessary precondition for trust formation in highly automated driving, and trained random-forest models on lexical and acoustic features—including prosodic cues such as formants and MFCCs—to explain trust variance for continuous trust measurement in conversational human-autonomy teams. Well-being and in-vehicle office design in automated vehicles.
notes: Identification is confident: one Mengyao Li clearly fits the Human Factors journal reviewer profile — she holds a Ph.D. in Human Factors from UW-Madison (2023, advisor John D. Lee, a leading HF scholar), is now faculty at Georgia Tech School of Psychology, and has published directly in Human Factors (SAGE) and HFES Annual Meeting proceedings. The PR (Past/Provisional Reviewer) role is consistent with her career stage (recently graduated, new faculty). No other Mengyao Li in the HF/ergonomics field was found. Citation counts are from Google Scholar as of mid-2025 and may have changed. The 2025 IJHCI paper DOI resolves but the article was listed as Vol 0 No 0 at time of search, indicating advance online publication.

name: Lyons, Joseph
recent_1: Schelble, B. G., Flathmann, C., Aly, H., Lyons, J. B., & McNeese, N. (2026). Human or AI advice? Examining trust, influence, and responsibility in ethically charged human-AI team decision-making. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434261434613
recent_1_synopsis: Using an experiment where participants worked with an advisor whose identity (human vs. AI) and influence level were varied across ethically charged scenarios, this study examined how advisor identity shapes trust, compliance, and perceived responsibility in human-AI team decision-making. It advances understanding of how AI teammates affect moral agency and responsibility attribution in operational contexts.
recent_2: Lyons, J. B., Jessup, S. A., & Vo, T. Q. (2024). The role of decision authority and stated social intent as predictors of trust in autonomous robots. Topics in Cognitive Science, 16(3), 430–449. https://doi.org/10.1111/tops.12601
recent_2_synopsis: This study experimentally manipulated an autonomous security robot's level of decision authority and stated social intent (benevolent vs. self-protective), finding that benevolent intent signaling increased trust and perceived trustworthiness, with effects modulated by how much autonomous decision power the robot held. The work clarifies the joint role of machine agency and intent communication in shaping appropriate reliance.
recent_3: Lyons, J. B., Hamdan, I., & Vo, T. Q. (2023). Explanations and trust: What happens to trust when a robot partner does something unexpected? Computers in Human Behavior, 138, 107473.
recent_3_synopsis: This study found that unexpected robot behaviors degrade human trust and trustworthiness perceptions, but that post-hoc explanations attenuate that decline and shift responsibility attribution toward the robot. The findings support equipping autonomous systems with explanatory capabilities as a trust-maintenance mechanism in human-autonomy teaming.
seminal_1: Lyons, J. B. (2013). Being transparent about transparency: A model for human-robot interaction. In Proceedings of the AAAI Spring Symposium on Trust and Autonomous Systems (SS-13-07). AAAI Press.
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper introduced a foundational two-dimensional transparency model for human-robot interaction, distinguishing robot-to-human transparency (what the system communicates about itself) from robot-of-human transparency (the system's awareness of the human). With approximately 410 Google Scholar citations and over 193 Semantic Scholar citations, it is a generative precursor to the widely-used Situation Awareness-based Agent Transparency (SAT) model and remains a cornerstone reference for designing explainable and trustworthy autonomous systems.
seminal_2: Lyons, J. B., Sycara, K., Lewis, M., & Capiola, A. (2021). Human–autonomy teaming: Definitions, debates, and directions. Frontiers in Psychology, 12, 589585. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2021.589585
seminal_2_synopsis: This review formalized the distinction between human-automation interaction and human-autonomy teaming (HAT), identified five critical elements for effective HATs (machine agency, communicative capability, intent signaling, shared mental models, and interdependence), and mapped open research questions at the intersection of teaming science and autonomous systems. It has become a primary definitional and conceptual reference for the HAT research community.
expertise_overview: Trust in automation and autonomy, human-robot interaction, human-autonomy teaming, supervisory control, and human-AI collaboration. Agent transparency—how autonomous systems communicate their state, reasoning, and intent—via a two-dimensional model distinguishing robot-to-human from robot-of-human transparency that prefigured situation-awareness-based agent transparency frameworks. Appropriate reliance, trust calibration, and social-psychological antecedents of human-machine trust including decision authority, benevolent versus self-protective intent signaling, and explanations for unexpected agent behavior, showing post-hoc explanations attenuate trust decline and shift responsibility attribution. Formalized the distinction between human-automation interaction and human-autonomy teaming, identifying machine agency, communicative capability, intent signaling, shared mental models, and interdependence as critical elements. Teams and macrocognition, including how AI teammates shape ethical decision-making, moral agency, responsibility attribution, and influence dynamics.
notes: Identification is high-confidence. Joseph B. Lyons at AFRL is the clear referent: his publication record (over 100 papers in human factors, human-machine trust, HRI, and human-autonomy teaming), his role as AFRL Senior Scientist for Human-Machine Teaming, his editorial board service (confirmed Associate Editor for Military Psychology; editorial board role at Human Factors is consistent with his publication record in that journal, e.g., Lyons & Stokes 2012, Lyons et al. 2021 in Human Factors), and his fellowship in APA and the Society for Military Psychologists all align with an EB role at Human Factors. The 2013 transparency paper citation count (~410 on Google Scholar per scraped data) and the Frontiers 2021 HAT review are his two most influential works by citation count and conceptual reach. The "Explanations and trust" 2023 paper in Computers in Human Behavior is confirmed by ScienceDirect record. The 2024 Topics in Cognitive Science paper is confirmed by Wiley/PubMed. The 2026 Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making paper is confirmed by SAGE DOI. No fabricated details; all citations drawn from verified sources.

name: Marquard, Jenna
recent_1: Marquard, J. L., Martin, C. L., Theesfeld, S. S. N., Feldmann, A. L., Trofholz, A. C., Wick, E. C., & Melton, G. B. (2026). Manual vs. AI-Assisted Qualitative Analysis: A Methodological Comparison of Nurse Perspectives on Surgical Recovery. International Journal of Qualitative Methods, 25. https://doi.org/10.1177/16094069261429593
recent_1_synopsis: This study compared LLM-assisted versus fully manual qualitative coding of nurse interview data about postoperative recovery, finding that AI-assisted methods required only 4-8 person-hours versus ~30 hours for manual analysis while maintaining comparable thematic completeness. The work demonstrates a hybrid human-AI workflow suitable for implementation science in nursing research.
recent_2: Park, S., Marquard, J. L., Austin, R. R., Martin, C. L., Pieczkiewicz, D. S., & Delaney, C. W. (2025). Exploratory Co-Design on Electronic Health Record Nursing Summaries: Case Study. JMIR Formative Research, 9, e68906. https://doi.org/10.2196/68906
recent_2_synopsis: This co-design study engaged 33 clinical nurses in selecting and arranging information-type cards to define preferred EHR nursing summary layouts, identifying priorities such as unit specimen, activity, diet, and hospital problems. The findings support participatory design as a method to produce nursing summaries that better match clinical workflow and reduce navigation burden.
recent_3: Marquard, J., Austin, R., & Rajamani, S. (2024). Design of patient-facing immunization visualizations affects task performance: an experimental comparison of 4 electronic visualizations. Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association, 31(11), 2429-2439.
recent_3_synopsis: An experimental study with 69 lay participants compared how four EHR-based immunization visualization formats (Epic MyChart, eClinicalWorks, downloadable EHR record, clinic letter) affected accuracy and time-on-task; Epic MyChart users took significantly longer and made more errors, demonstrating that specific design choices substantially impact patient health data comprehension.
seminal_1: Marquard, J. L., Henneman, P. L., He, Z., Jo, J., Fisher, D. L., & Henneman, E. A. (2011). Nurses' behaviors and visual scanning patterns may reduce patient identification errors. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 17(3), 247-256.
seminal_1_synopsis: Using eye-tracking in a simulated medication administration scenario, this study showed that nurses who successfully identified patient ID errors scanned information across multiple artifacts (ID band, chart, medication label) rather than fixating on one artifact at a time. With ~75 citations, this is an influential demonstration of how visual attention strategies relate to patient safety outcomes, linking cognitive HF methods to clinical error prevention.
seminal_2: Brown, P. J., Marquard, J. L., Amster, B., Romoser, M., Friderici, J., Goff, S., & Fisher, D. (2014). What do physicians read (and ignore) in electronic progress notes? Applied Clinical Informatics, 5(2), 430-444.
seminal_2_synopsis: This eye-tracking study revealed that physicians concentrate their reading on the Impression and Plan section of EHR progress notes while largely ignoring automatically imported medication lists, vital signs, and lab values, despite these being prominently included in documentation. With ~91 citations, the paper is widely cited in health IT design and clinical informatics literature as evidence for reconsidering EHR note structure and information display priorities.
expertise_overview: Human factors engineering and health informatics: eye-tracking and visual attention methods for how clinicians and patients search for and process information in electronic health records. EHR visualization and display design, immunization data displays, medication administration safety, patient identification errors, clinical decision support, and patient-centered consumer health IT. Eye-tracking evidence that nurses who detect patient-ID errors scan across multiple artifacts rather than fixating on one, and that physicians concentrate reading on the Impression and Plan section while largely ignoring imported medication lists, vitals, and labs—influential for reconsidering EHR note structure. Experimental comparison of immunization visualization formats showing design choices substantially affect lay comprehension; participatory co-design of preferred EHR nursing summary layouts; LLM-assisted versus manual qualitative coding demonstrating efficient hybrid human-AI workflows for nursing research.
notes: Identity is confidently established. Marquard was previously an Associate Professor at UMass Amherst (Mechanical and Industrial Engineering) and is now a Professor in the Population Health and Systems Cooperative Unit, School of Nursing, University of Minnesota. Both affiliations appear in her ResearchGate and Google Scholar profiles. She has 1,359 total citations, h-index 21, i10-index 42 (per Google Scholar as of 2026). Her HFES connection is confirmed by her role as guest editor for Human Factors in Healthcare journal and her history of HFES-related publications. The most-cited figures cited above (75 and 91) are from her Google Scholar profile at the time of search and may change. Pages for the 2026 IJQM paper were not yet listed in the source; the DOI is confirmed via Sage Journals and University of Minnesota Experts system.

name: Martin, Bernard
recent_1: Garcia, G., Espoz, M., Albuja, C., Yañez, R., Arauz, P.G., & Martin, B.J. (2026). Biomechanical and physiological effects of a passive upper-body exoskeleton during stair ascent and descent. PLOS One, 21(2), e0343438.
recent_1_synopsis: The study evaluated how a passive upper-body exoskeleton (CarrySuit) affects muscle activity, heart rate, joint motion, and perceived discomfort when carrying a 12 kg load on stairs. The device significantly reduced biceps brachii and erector spinae activation and lowered cardiovascular strain without restricting essential movement patterns.
recent_2: Garcia, G., Arauz, P.G., Alvarez, I., Encalada, N., Vega, S., Baldo, M., & Martin, B.J. (2024). Effects of a passive upper-body exoskeleton on whole-body kinematics, leg muscle activity, and discomfort during a carrying task. PLOS One, 19(7), e0304606.
recent_2_synopsis: This study compared whole-body kinematics, leg muscle activity, and self-reported discomfort in 30 participants performing a 10-minute load-carrying task with and without a passive exoskeleton. Leg discomfort was significantly lower with the exoskeleton, extending knowledge of secondary lower-limb effects of upper-body assistive devices.
recent_3: Kim, D., Nicoletti, C., Soedirdjo, S.D.H., Baghi, R., Garcia, M.-G., Läubli, T., Wild, P., Botter, A., & Martin, B.J. (2023). Effect of periodic voluntary interventions on trapezius activation and fatigue during light upper limb activity. Human Factors, 65(7), 1491–1505.
recent_3_synopsis: Thirty adults completed a simulated two-day, 6-hour computer work protocol with and without periodic imposed interventions designed to disrupt monotonous trapezius contraction. Disruptive interventions significantly reduced trapezius fatigue accumulation, providing evidence-based guidance for work-break design to prevent neck-shoulder musculoskeletal disorders.
seminal_1: Chaffin, D.B., Andersson, G.B.J., & Martin, B.J. (2006). Occupational Biomechanics (4th ed.). Wiley-Interscience. [Earlier editions: 1991, 1999; 1st edition 1984 by Chaffin & Andersson]
seminal_1_synopsis: This textbook is the premier reference in occupational biomechanics, synthesizing musculoskeletal biomechanics, ergonomic risk assessment, and injury prevention for industrial and healthcare settings. With over 4,300 citations across editions, it has defined training and practice for ergonomists, engineers, and occupational health professionals worldwide for four decades.
seminal_2: Martin, B.J., & Park, H.S. (1997). Analysis of the tonic vibration reflex: influence of vibration variables on motor unit synchronization and fatigue. European Journal of Applied Physiology and Occupational Physiology, 75(6), 504–511.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper systematically characterized how vibration frequency and amplitude govern motor unit synchronization and consequent muscle fatigue via the tonic vibration reflex in human hand and wrist flexors. With approximately 385 citations, it remains a foundational reference for understanding how occupational vibration exposure from hand-held power tools accelerates neuromuscular fatigue and injury risk.
expertise_overview: Occupational ergonomics and biomechanics, human vibration and its effects on neuromuscular performance, and sensorimotor control for human-machine interaction. Keyboard biomechanics and keystroke force-EMG relationships, musculoskeletal fatigue, and exoskeleton effectiveness for load carrying. Characterized how vibration frequency and amplitude govern motor unit synchronization and muscle fatigue via the tonic vibration reflex in hand and wrist flexors, a foundational account of how hand-held power-tool vibration exposure accelerates neuromuscular fatigue and injury risk. Passive upper-body exoskeletons during stair load-carrying and walking, showing reduced biceps and erector spinae activation, lowered cardiovascular strain, and decreased leg discomfort. Work-break interventions disrupting monotonous trapezius contraction to reduce fatigue and prevent neck-shoulder disorders; vibrotactile feedback for postural stability and proprioceptive studies relevant to aging and rehabilitation.
notes: Identification is highly confident: Bernard J. Martin, Emeritus Professor of Industrial and Operations Engineering and Center for Ergonomics, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor (retired end of 2023). He published directly in Human Factors (the 2023 trapezius fatigue paper; the 1996 keyboard paper) and his career profile in occupational biomechanics and sensorimotor ergonomics aligns naturally with an Editorial Board role. The 2026 PLoS One stair-exoskeleton paper confirms he remains active in research post-retirement. The Occupational Biomechanics textbook citation counts (~4,300 per edition on Google Scholar) reflect both the book's multiple editions and its cross-listing; the figure should be interpreted as the book series' collective impact rather than a single-edition count. No ambiguity with other researchers of the same name was found in the HF/ergonomics field.

name: Mayhorn, Christopher B.
recent_1: Park, S., Watson, A., Mayhorn, C. B., & Singh, M. P. (2026). Challenges faced by older adults in using mobile apps, and a way forward. Computer, 59(2), 39–48. DOI: 10.1109/MC.2025.3599192
recent_1_synopsis: Using real user-app reviews, this paper systematically identifies the cognitive and usability barriers that older adults face when using mobile applications, and proposes the Empathetic Senior Technology Acceptance Model (e-STAM) — an adoption framework that embeds AI agents capable of dynamically recognizing aging-related cognitive challenges. It advances human-technology interaction research by integrating empathetic AI into a classical TAM structure specifically calibrated for older populations.
recent_2: Goodman, K. L., & Mayhorn, C. B. (2023). It's not what you say but how you say it: Examining the influence of perceived voice assistant gender and pitch on trust and reliance. Applied Ergonomics, 106, 103864.
recent_2_synopsis: In a mixed-age sample (77 young adults, 73 older adults) performing a medical symptom-triage task, voice assistants perceived as female were rated more trustworthy regardless of vocal pitch, while pitch alone did not significantly shift trust or reliance. The study demonstrates that social categorization cues derived from voice characteristics shape human–AI interaction even in health-critical decision contexts.
recent_3: Ferreri, N. R., & Mayhorn, C. B. (2023). Identifying and understanding individual differences in frustration with technology. Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science, 24(4), 461–479. DOI: 10.1080/1463922X.2022.2095458
recent_3_synopsis: Using a mixed factorial design with 72 participants completing a simulated online-shopping task, the study found that neuroticism and extraversion predicted heightened frustration when technology malfunctioned, while openness was the strongest predictor of lower frustration. The work advances human–technology interaction theory by linking Big Five personality traits to differential frustration responses, informing the design of more adaptive, user-centered interfaces.
seminal_1: Park, D. C., Hertzog, C., Kidder, D. P., Morrell, R. W., & Mayhorn, C. B. (1997). Effect of age on event-based and time-based prospective memory. Psychology and Aging, 12(2), 314–327. DOI: 10.1037/0882-7974.12.2.314
seminal_1_synopsis: In two experiments this paper demonstrated that aging produces marked declines in both event-based and time-based prospective memory, with the age deficit especially pronounced for time-based tasks that require self-initiated retrieval without external cues. With over 529 citations it became a foundational reference in cognitive aging, prospective memory research, and the design of reminder systems and safety warnings for older adults.
seminal_2: Morrell, R. W., Mayhorn, C. B., & Bennett, J. (2000). A survey of World Wide Web use in middle-aged and older adults. Human Factors, 42(2), 175–182. DOI: 10.1518/001872000779656444
seminal_2_synopsis: Surveying 550 adults aged 40 and older, this paper documented that lack of computer access and limited Web knowledge — not motivation — were the primary barriers to Internet adoption among older cohorts, with the oldest-old showing least interest. Published in Human Factors itself, it became a benchmark citation (nearly 400 citations) for HCI and aging research and shaped subsequent guidelines for accessible web design and digital-inclusion interventions.
expertise_overview: Warnings and hazard/risk communication, including optimizing warning design for diverse user populations, and HCI and aging, examining how cognitive and perceptual lifespan changes affect technology adoption, usability, and decision-making. Cybersecurity human factors, examining phishing susceptibility through signal detection theory, personality, and persuasion principles. Showed aging produces marked declines in event-based and especially time-based prospective memory requiring self-initiated retrieval, a foundational reference for reminder systems and safety warnings, and documented that lack of computer access and limited Web knowledge—not motivation—are the primary barriers to Internet adoption among older adults. Voice assistants, technology frustration, and trust in AI-mediated systems, linking Big Five personality traits to differential frustration responses, showing female-perceived voice assistants are rated more trustworthy in health triage, and proposing empathetic AI-augmented technology acceptance models for older populations. Prospective memory paradigms, surveys, controlled experiments, and signal detection analyses.
notes: Identification is confident: Dr. Christopher B. Mayhorn is a named Fellow of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, has served on HFES committees and the Technical Program Committee, has published in Human Factors, Applied Ergonomics, Ergonomics, and Theoretical Issues in Ergonomics Science, making him a natural fit for the journal's Editorial Board. Citation counts are drawn from his Google Scholar profile (5,139 total citations, h-index 38 as of mid-2026). The Park et al. (2026) Computer paper is listed as Volume 59(2) per the Google Scholar record and was confirmed via the NC State faculty preprint; the DOI 10.1109/MC.2025.3599192 was found in web results but not independently verified through the IEEE portal. The Ferreri & Mayhorn (2023) TIES paper has the confirmed DOI 10.1080/1463922X.2022.2095458.

name: McCarley, Jason
recent_1: McCarley, J. S. (2025). A computational cognitive model of the vigilance decrement. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251369804
recent_1_synopsis: This paper develops a quantitative, dynamic computational model of signal detection to account for the vigilance decrement without invoking a resource-depletion process. The model reproduces hallmark patterns of sustained-attention performance decline, arguing that stronger computational modeling can sharpen theoretical understanding and resolve lingering disagreements about the mechanisms of the vigilance decrement.
recent_2: Ben Yaakov, Y., McCarley, J. S., & Meyer, J. (2025). Selective access to decision support as a function of event uncertainty. Human Factors, 67(4), 285–300. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241277158
recent_2_synopsis: This study investigated how event uncertainty and decision-support display format (integrated vs. separated likelihood cues) shape operators' decisions to query automated diagnostic aids, their sensitivity, and subjective workload. Results showed that integrated displays yielded higher effective sensitivity, and that the challenge of combining likelihood information with raw data constitutes a key barrier to effective use of decision-support systems.
recent_3: Tikhomirov, L., Bartlett, M. L., Duncan-Reid, J., & McCarley, J. S. (2023). Identifying inefficient strategies in automation-aided signal detection. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 29(4), 869–886. DOI: 10.1037/xap0000484
recent_3_synopsis: This paper examined how human operators use automated diagnostic aids in a signal-detection task and identified specific suboptimal strategy patterns that cause performance to fall short of the theoretically achievable optimum. The work provides a framework for diagnosing and potentially correcting inefficient human-automation teaming strategies in applied monitoring contexts.
seminal_1: Peterson, M. S., Kramer, A. F., Wang, R. F., Irwin, D. E., & McCarley, J. S. (2001). Visual search has memory. Psychological Science, 12(4), 287–292. DOI: 10.1111/1467-9280.00353
seminal_1_synopsis: Using eye-tracking to monitor refixations during free-scanning visual search, this study demonstrated that oculomotor search is guided by implicit memory for previously inspected locations, contradicting the prevailing assumption that each fixation is independent. The finding fundamentally reframed models of visual search and attention allocation and has accrued approximately 490 citations, making it one of the most influential empirical contributions to the study of attention in complex environments.
seminal_2: Dixon, S. R., Wickens, C. D., & McCarley, J. S. (2007). On the independence of compliance and reliance: Are automation false alarms worse than misses? Human Factors, 49(4), 564–572. DOI: 10.1518/001872007X215656
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper distinguished and operationalized the separate concepts of compliance (following automation alerts) and reliance (deferring to automation in the absence of alerts), and showed empirically that false-alarm-prone automation impairs both constructs while miss-prone automation primarily degrades reliance. The compliance/reliance framework became a foundational taxonomy for human-automation interaction research and the paper has garnered approximately 400 citations.
expertise_overview: Engineering psychology; attention, vigilance, perceptual-cognitive decision-making. Vigilance decrement mechanisms via psychophysical analysis and computational cognitive modeling; a dynamic signal-detection model reproducing sustained-attention decline without resource depletion. Human-automation interaction distinguishing compliance from reliance, showing false-alarm-prone automation impairs both while miss-prone automation degrades reliance; operator querying of decision-support aids; display format effects (integrated vs. separated likelihood cues) on automation-aided signal detection; suboptimal human-automation teaming strategies. Visual-search work showing oculomotor search is guided by implicit memory for inspected locations. Selective and divided attention, mental workload, displays and controls, distracted driving, lane-keeping under cognitive load.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence: ORCID 0000-0002-8824-7491 uniquely identifies Jason S. McCarley, Professor at Oregon State University School of Psychological Science, Engineering Psychology and Human Factors specialty, h-index 41, ~7,700 total citations (Google Scholar). The 2025 Human Factors paper (DOI 10.1177/10711813251369804) appears first published online in 2025 and may be assigned to a 2025 or 2026 volume; the volume number in the search results was ambiguous. The Applied Attention Theory 2nd edition publication year varies across sources between 2022 and 2023; CRC Press/Routledge is the publisher. Citation counts for seminal papers are approximate figures drawn from Google Scholar as reported in search results and may have grown since the last index update.

name: McDonald, Tony
recent_1: Wei, R., McDonald, A. D., Mehta, R. K., & Garcia, A. (2025). Active inference models of AV takeovers: Relating model parameters to trust, situation awareness, and fatigue. Human Factors, 67(6), 616–634. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208241295932
recent_1_synopsis: Developed and validated an active inference computational model that predicts when drivers take control of automated vehicles, linking model parameters to self-reported trust, situation awareness, and cognitive fatigue via Bayesian analysis. Found that higher fatigue increased uncertainty about takeover necessity, while higher trust increased variability in how drivers perceived environmental conditions.
recent_2: Park, J., Zahabi, M., Zheng, X., Ory, M., Benden, M., McDonald, A. D., & Li, W. (2024). Automated vehicles for older adults with cognitive impairment: a survey study. Ergonomics, 67(6). https://doi.org/10.1080/00140139.2024.2302020
recent_2_synopsis: Surveyed 435 older adults (with and without cognitive impairment) and 188 care stakeholders to understand perceptions and mobility needs related to automated vehicles. Found that cognitive impairment level, mobility limitations, and AV knowledge influenced attitudes, and recommended education and adaptive simulation-based training to improve AV acceptance.
recent_3: Xiao, X., Ma, X., McDonald, A. D., & Mehta, R. K. (2025). What leads to reliance on automated vehicles? An inferential analysis of responses to variable AV performance. Applied Ergonomics, 128. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104509
recent_3_synopsis: Examined how variability in automated vehicle performance shapes driver reliance behavior using an inferential modeling approach. Results revealed that drivers' reliance decisions depend on their uncertainty about AV capability, with implications for designing feedback systems that calibrate appropriate human-automation trust.
seminal_1: McDonald, A. D., Alambeigi, H., Engström, J., Markkula, G., Vogelpohl, T., Dunne, J., & Yuma, N. (2019). Toward computational simulations of behavior during automated driving takeovers: A review of the empirical and modeling literatures. Human Factors, 61(4), 642–688. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720819829572
seminal_1_synopsis: This comprehensive systematic review synthesized both empirical takeover studies and driver behavioral models, identifying that time budget, secondary task engagement, and repeated exposure significantly shape takeover performance and timing. It became highly cited (364+ citations) because it was the first to systematically bridge cognitive modeling traditions with automated driving safety research, recommending evidence-accumulation models as the most promising path for future computational work.
seminal_2: McDonald, A. D., Lee, J. D., Schwarz, C., & Brown, T. L. (2014). Steering in a random forest: Ensemble learning for detecting drowsiness-related lane departures. Human Factors, 56(5), 986–998. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720813512868
seminal_2_synopsis: Applied ensemble machine learning (random forest) to steering wheel angle data from naturalistic driving to detect drowsiness-related lane departures, demonstrating that algorithmic feature engineering can outperform traditional signal-based approaches in ecologically valid settings. This paper established a landmark methodology for unobtrusive, vehicle-based driver state monitoring and has become a foundational reference in the drowsy driving detection literature (129+ citations).
expertise_overview: Cognitive behavioral models coupled with machine learning for driving safety and transportation human factors. Drowsy and distracted driving detection, driver state monitoring from physiological and vehicle-kinematic signals, human-automation interaction in conditionally automated vehicles. An active inference model predicting driver takeover linking parameters to trust, situation awareness, and cognitive fatigue, finding fatigue raises uncertainty about takeover necessity while trust increases perceptual variability. Ensemble random-forest methods on steering-wheel-angle data to detect drowsiness-related lane departures for unobtrusive vehicle-based monitoring. Systematic review of takeover behavior recommending evidence-accumulation models. Reliance calibration under variable automation performance; aging and special populations (older adults with cognitive impairment, automated vehicle acceptance); healthcare applications; active inference and queuing-theory frameworks alongside supervised learning.
notes: The name "Tony McDonald" listed in the journal's reviewer pool corresponds to Anthony D. McDonald (PhD, University of Wisconsin-Madison, 2014), who has published extensively in Human Factors (SAGE) and is currently an Associate Professor at UW-Madison after previously holding a faculty position at Texas A&M University (approx. 2017–2022). Identification is high confidence: his Google Scholar profile (user=14TnSJIAAAAJ) and university directory page confirm his HFES membership and multiple Human Factors publications. The Applied Ergonomics 2025 paper volume/page details were not fully confirmed from primary sources; the citation is accurate in authorship, year, and title but page numbers are approximate. The "Steering in a Random Forest" issue number (56, no. 5) was inferred from Semantic Scholar and ResearchGate cross-references and should be verified against the primary record.

name: McNeese, Nathan J.
recent_1: Hauptman, A. I., Flathmann, C., & McNeese, N. J. (2024). Adapting to the human: A systematic review of a decade of human factors research on adaptive autonomy. Applied Ergonomics, 120, 104336. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104336
recent_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes ten years of HF research on adaptive autonomy, examining how autonomous systems can adjust their behavior to human states and task context. It characterizes the design approaches, dependent variables, and research gaps that should guide the next generation of adaptive AI teammate development.
recent_2: Schelble, B. G., Lopez, J., Textor, C., Zhang, R., McNeese, N. J., Pak, R., & Freeman, G. (2024). Towards ethical AI: Empirically investigating dimensions of AI ethics, trust repair, and performance in human-AI teaming. Human Factors, 66(4), 1037–1055. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208221116952
recent_2_synopsis: This empirical study examines how two trust-repair strategies (apology vs. denial) by an AI teammate following an ethical violation affect human trust and team performance in human-AI teams. Findings provide actionable guidance on designing AI agents that can recover credibility after ethical failures in joint work contexts.
recent_3: Flathmann, C., McNeese, N. J., & O'Neill, T. A. (2025). Designing high-impact experiments for human–autonomy/AI teaming. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434251327697
recent_3_synopsis: This methodological paper identifies four critical decision points—research question framing, team composition, research environment selection, and data collection strategy—that researchers must navigate when designing human-autonomy teaming experiments. It provides a practical framework for producing generalizable, high-impact empirical work in this interdisciplinary area.
seminal_1: O'Neill, T., McNeese, N., Barron, A., & Schelble, B. (2022). Human–autonomy teaming: A review and analysis of the empirical literature. Human Factors, 64(5), 904–938. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720820960865
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly cited review (817+ citations) synthesizes the empirical human-autonomy teaming (HAT) literature, defining the construct, cataloguing research environments and dependent variables, and identifying key themes and future directions. It has become a foundational reference for the HAT field by providing a shared conceptual framework that researchers and practitioners can build upon.
seminal_2: McNeese, N. J., Demir, M., Cooke, N. J., & Myers, C. (2018). Teaming with a synthetic teammate: Insights into human-autonomy teaming. Human Factors, 60(2), 262–273. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720817743223
seminal_2_synopsis: This early empirical paper (508+ citations) compared teams with a synthetic (AI) pilot against human-piloted control teams in a simulated unmanned aerial system task, demonstrating both the feasibility and the performance trade-offs of human-autonomy teaming. It is widely credited as one of the first controlled experiments to operationalize HAT and is foundational to subsequent empirical and theoretical work in the field.
expertise_overview: Human-AI and human-robot teaming; how humans form effective teams with autonomous and AI agents. Trust calibration, trust repair, team situation awareness, adaptive autonomy, ethical AI design, communication strategies enabling AI teammates to contribute productively. Synthesis of a decade of adaptive autonomy research; a human-autonomy teaming review defining the construct, cataloguing research environments and dependent variables. Trust-repair strategies (apology vs. denial) following an AI teammate's ethical violation; methodological decision points (research-question framing, team composition, environment selection, data collection) for generalizable teaming experiments. Controlled experiments comparing synthetic-pilot teams against human-piloted control teams in simulated unmanned-aerial-system tasks. Command-and-control, emergency crisis management, healthcare, and search-and-rescue in safety-critical sociotechnical systems.
notes: Identity is unambiguous: Nathan J. McNeese is a named, active faculty member at Clemson University with a public Google Scholar profile (user=G1CnZ38AAAAJ), an HFES award, and dozens of papers in Human Factors and adjacent HFES/HCI venues. Citation counts for the two seminal papers are drawn from Google Scholar as of mid-2026 and may have grown further. The "Towards Ethical AI" paper by Schelble et al. was accepted in 2022 (online) but appeared in print in Human Factors in 2024 (vol. 66, no. 4); the 2022 DOI date reflects online-first publication.

name: Mead, Sherry E.
seminal_1: Rogers, W. A., Fisk, A. D., Mead, S. E., Walker, N., & Cabrera, E. F. (1996). Training older adults to use automatic teller machines. Human Factors, 38(3), 425–433. https://doi.org/10.1518/001872096778701935
seminal_1_synopsis: This study compared four instructional methods (description-only, text guide, pictorial guide, online tutorial) for teaching 56 older adults (ages 61–81) to use ATMs, finding that online tutorial training produced the best accuracy on both trained and novel transactions. It became a landmark demonstration that training method design — not aging per se — substantially governs older adults' success with new technology, and is among the most-cited works (~176 citations) in the aging-and-technology literature.
seminal_2: Mead, S. E., & Fisk, A. D. (1998). Measuring skill acquisition and retention with an ATM simulator: The need for age-specific training. Human Factors, 40(3), 516–523. https://doi.org/10.1518/001872098779591313
seminal_2_synopsis: Using a simulated ATM, this study manipulated whether training emphasized actions versus concepts for younger and older adults, testing both immediate and 1-month retention performance. The finding that action-oriented training selectively benefited older adults — and that this advantage interacted with task-component type — made the case that age-specific instructional design is necessary for effective technology skill acquisition, influencing subsequent guidelines for training older users (~142 citations).
expertise_overview: Aging, cognition, and human-computer interaction; cognitive mechanisms underlying age-related differences in skill acquisition with new technologies. Instance-based versus strength-based automaticity and goal-directed versus stimulus-driven visual selection in older adults' learning of automated teller machines and the World Wide Web. Comparison of instructional methods for teaching older adults to use ATMs: online-tutorial training produced the best accuracy on trained and novel transactions, with training method design, not aging per se, governing technology success. Action-oriented versus concept-oriented training across age groups and retention intervals, showing action-oriented training selectively benefits older adults and age-specific instructional design is necessary. Accessibility guidelines for health-information websites; enterprise search; adaptive interface design.
notes: Identity is confirmed with high confidence: Sherry E. Mead is the Georgia Tech-trained Engineering Psychology researcher who co-authored multiple papers with Arthur D. Fisk and Wendy A. Rogers, and whose SAGE author page lists her affiliation as Hewlett-Packard/Oracle and her journal as Human Factors. LinkedIn confirms her Engineering Psychology PhD (Georgia Tech, 1994–2000) and Editorial Board membership since 2005. No peer-reviewed academic publications from 2010–2026 were found; her publication record is concentrated in 1995–2002, reflecting a transition to industry practice. The three 'recent publications' array is left empty because no verifiable peer-reviewed papers from the 2023–2026 window (or recent years) were found — fabricating citations would violate the accuracy rule. The 1999 paper (Mead et al.) is correctly attributed to International Journal of Behavioral Development, Vol 23(3), 553–573, via DOI 10.1080/016502599383694 (Taylor & Francis/SAGE listing); an earlier Google Scholar snippet incorrectly listed it as International Journal of Human-Computer Studies. Citation counts (~176 and ~142) are sourced from Google Scholar as of the search date and may have changed.

name: Meyer, Joachim
recent_1: Saad, Y., & Meyer, J. (2025). Context-based human influence and causal responsibility for assisted decision-making. Human Factors, 67(8), 795–808. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251317470
recent_1_synopsis: This study extends the Responsibility Quantification (ResQu) model by showing that how automation is framed—as a decision support system versus an automated decision-maker—significantly affects both the measured level of human causal responsibility and user behavior. Empirical results demonstrate that human contribution to outcomes is substantially higher in decision-support contexts, especially when both human and automation accuracy are high, challenging the original model's context-independence assumption.
recent_2: Ben Yaakov, Y., Denisova, M., Mulugeta, F., & Meyer, J. (2025). Normative or confirmative: Effects of information quality and redundancy in decision support systems. Human Factors, 67(6), 546–559. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208241302787
recent_2_synopsis: This experiment tested whether users preferentially access nonredundant versus redundant decision support (DS) and how correlation between DS and task information affects performance and trust. Participants systematically favored uncorrelated DS sources and performed better with them, although reliability and redundancy interacted to modulate trust.
recent_3: Ben Yaakov, Y., McCarley, J. S., & Meyer, J. (2025). Selective access to decision support as a function of event uncertainty. Human Factors, 67(4), 285–300. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208241277158
recent_3_synopsis: This study introduced a trial-by-trial selective-access paradigm in which participants chose whether to consult a decision support cue before each classification decision, finding that requests for assistance peaked when stimulus information was most ambiguous. Integrated likelihood displays—combining raw data with DS cues in a single perceptual object—outperformed separated display formats, improving both DS utilization and decision accuracy.
seminal_1: Meyer, J. (2001). Effects of warning validity and proximity on responses to warnings. Human Factors, 43(4), 563–572. https://doi.org/10.1518/001872001775870395
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper demonstrated experimentally that both the validity (hit/false-alarm rate) and spatial or temporal proximity of a warning signal jointly determine whether operators comply with or ignore alerts, establishing a foundational empirical base for alarm system design. With approximately 368 citations, it remains a cornerstone reference in the warnings literature, directly informing standards for alarm management in aviation, process control, and healthcare.
seminal_2: Meyer, J. (2004). Conceptual issues in the study of dynamic hazard warnings. Human Factors, 46(2), 196–204. https://doi.org/10.1518/hfes.46.2.196.37335
seminal_2_synopsis: This conceptual paper introduced a systematic framework for classifying operator responses to dynamic warning systems, distinguishing compliance (responding when a warning fires) from reliance (reducing vigilance when no warning fires), and identifying normative, task-related, and operator-based determinants of each. Accumulating roughly 332 citations, it shaped decades of subsequent research on alarm and alert systems and is widely used to structure both empirical studies and design guidelines.
expertise_overview: Quantitative modeling of human decision-making in interactions with intelligent, automated, and advisory systems. Warnings and alarms: compliance/reliance distinctions and normative frameworks, showing alert validity together with spatial or temporal proximity jointly determine whether operators comply with or ignore signals. The Responsibility Quantification (ResQu) model using information theory to measure human causal responsibility in partially automated pipelines, demonstrating that framing automation as decision support versus automated decision-maker shifts measured responsibility and behavior. Adaptive and interactive information displays; selective and trial-by-trial access to decision support (peaking when stimuli are ambiguous); information redundancy and correlation between advisory and task sources; integrated versus separated likelihood display formats. Cybersecurity behavior, information visualization, adaptive user interfaces; automation, trust and reliance, decision support, displays and controls.
notes: Identity is unambiguous: Joachim Meyer at Tel Aviv University is consistently identified across Google Scholar, Wikipedia, CRIS/TAU, SAGE Journals, and PubMed as the human factors / industrial engineering professor with HFES fellowship and extensive publication in Human Factors journal. His Google Scholar profile (user=4LaM9EUAAAAJ) shows 5,891 total citations and h-index 39 as of 2025. The three recent papers cited are all published in Human Factors (SAGE) in 2025 (online 2024, print 2025). Publication years listed reflect the official journal print volume (67) rather than the online-first date. The ResQu model paper (Douer & Meyer, 2020, IEEE TASE) is highly cited within automation responsibility literature but did not appear in the top-10 list; the two seminal papers selected (Meyer 2001, Meyer 2004) are the second and third most-cited works in his Google Scholar profile and both appeared in Human Factors itself, making them especially apt for this journal context.

name: Mirka, Gary A.
