recent_1: Kang, S.H., Wolf, E., Lowe, L., & Mirka, G.A. (2025). Effects of a passive back-support exosuit on objective and subjective measures of human performance during a simulated bush-crop harvesting task. Journal of Agromedicine, 30(3), 546–557.
recent_1_synopsis: This study evaluated a wearable passive back-support exosuit during a simulated agricultural harvesting task, finding that the device reduced lumbar extensor muscle loading and improved both objective performance and subjective comfort measures, extending exosuit effectiveness research to agricultural occupational contexts.
recent_2: Kang, S.H., & Mirka, G.A. (2025). Creep deformation of the viscoelastic lumbar tissues: Effects of trunk flexion posture, exposure-recovery schedule, and individual flexibility. Journal of Biomechanics, 184, 112675.
recent_2_synopsis: This paper characterized how trunk flexion posture, work-rest scheduling, and individual flexibility jointly modulate creep deformation in viscoelastic lumbar tissues, providing empirical parameters for time-dependent biomechanical models used in occupational low-back-risk assessment.
recent_3: Kim, J., Kang, S.H., Li, J., Mirka, G.A., & Dorneich, M.C. (2024). Effects of a passive back-support exosuit on postural control and cognitive performance during a fatigue-inducing posture maintenance task. Human Factors, 66(11), 2451–2467. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208231221890
recent_3_synopsis: Using an 18-participant crossover protocol with sustained 35-degree trunk flexion, the study showed that a passive back-support exosuit reduced erector spinae fatigue by 61%, improved postural stability (23% reduction in center-of-pressure velocity), and enhanced cognitive task accuracy by 8%, demonstrating that physical ergonomic interventions can yield co-benefits for cognitive performance.
seminal_1: Mirka, G.A. (1991). The quantification of EMG normalization error. Ergonomics, 34(3), 343–352.
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper demonstrated that normalizing trunk-muscle EMG to a single reference maximum voluntary contraction (MVC) at a fixed joint angle introduces systematic errors exceeding 75% for primary extensors, because EMG–force relationships vary substantially across trunk angles. With approximately 347 citations, it became a foundational methodological reference in occupational biomechanics and redefined best-practice standards for EMG data collection and normalization in human factors and ergonomics research.
seminal_2: Mirka, G.A., & Marras, W.S. (1993). A stochastic model of trunk muscle coactivation during trunk bending. Spine, 18(11), 1396–1409.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper introduced a stochastic (EMG-assisted) approach to modeling the inherently variable coactivation of trunk muscles during repetitive bending, replacing deterministic single-solution spine-load models and capturing the inter- and intra-individual variability in muscle force distributions. Recognized with the 1993 Volvo Award for Low Back Pain Research and cited more than 330 times, it established the EMG-assisted biomechanical modeling paradigm that underpins much of subsequent occupational spine-load research.
expertise_overview: Occupational ergonomics and biomechanics; spine biomechanics, low-back injury prevention, electromyographic methodology, quantification of cumulative musculoskeletal loading during manual material handling and prolonged posture. A stochastic EMG-assisted approach to modeling variable trunk-muscle coactivation during repetitive bending, replacing deterministic spine-load models and establishing the EMG-assisted modeling paradigm. Demonstration that normalizing trunk-muscle EMG to a single fixed-angle maximum voluntary contraction introduces systematic errors exceeding 75%, redefining normalization standards. How trunk flexion, work-rest scheduling, and individual flexibility modulate creep deformation in viscoelastic lumbar tissues. Passive back-support exosuits reducing erector spinae fatigue and lumbar loading, improving postural stability, with co-benefits for cognitive task accuracy across agricultural and sustained-flexion tasks. Agricultural and surgical ergonomics; break-scheduling for neck and shoulder fatigue; physical-cognitive interaction.
notes: Identity is high-confidence: Gary Allen Mirka, PhD, matches all identifying markers — editorial board listings for Applied Ergonomics and Human Factors, affiliation with Iowa State University IMSE (previously NC State), and active publication record in core HF/ergonomics venues including the Human Factors journal itself (two 2024 articles in Human Factors confirmed). Google Scholar profile (ZqQNZwgAAAAJ) confirms 5,600+ citations, h-index 41. The CV on the ISU server returned access errors but supplementary searches via Google Scholar and PubMed confirmed recent publication details. Publication years in the Google Scholar sorted-by-date view place items labeled "2025" early in the list; these may reflect 2024 online-first publications assigned to 2025 volumes — citations and DOIs were verified against Taylor and Francis and SAGE journal pages where possible.

name: Parush, Avi
recent_1: Manor, A., Cohen, D., Keidar, Z., Parush, A., & Erel, H. (2026). Cognitive trust in HRI: 'Pay attention to me and I'll trust you even if you are wrong.' Proceedings of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction (HRI '26), Edinburgh, Scotland, March 16–19, 2026. DOI: 10.1145/3757279.3785615
recent_1_synopsis: A 2×2 controlled experiment (competence × attentiveness) with a robotic dog showed that high attentiveness can compensate for low competence in building cognitive trust, revealing an affective compensatory mechanism that operates alongside traditional performance-based trust models in HRI.
recent_2: Manor, A., Parush, A., & Erel, H. (2025). Trust interplay: Robot performance influences cognitive but not affective trust. Proceedings of the 20th ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction (HRI '25). IEEE. DOI: 10.1109/HRI61125.2025.10974010
recent_2_synopsis: Experimentally separated cognitive and affective trust in HRI, finding that robot competence significantly raised cognitive trust but left affective trust unchanged, indicating that the two trust dimensions respond differently to robot performance cues.
recent_3: Specktor, M., Parush, A., & Shiftan, Y. (2023). Perceptions of stress among pedestrians in fully automated vehicle transportation systems. Transportation Research Record, 2677(11). DOI: 10.1177/03611981231164384
recent_3_synopsis: Used VR-based video scenarios to assess pedestrian stress responses in fully automated vehicle environments, providing empirical insight into emotional and perceptual factors that may influence public adoption of autonomous transportation.
seminal_1: Parush, A., Kramer, C., Foster-Hunt, T., Momtahan, K., Hunter, A., & Sohmer, B. (2011). Communication and team situation awareness in the OR: Implications for augmentative information display. Journal of Biomedical Informatics, 44(3), 477–485. DOI: 10.1016/j.jbi.2010.04.002
seminal_1_synopsis: Analyzed situation-related communications in the cardiac operating room to identify degradation in team situation awareness (TSA) as a patient safety risk, and derived implications for augmentative display design to support surgical team coordination. With over 230 citations, this paper is a foundational reference linking TSA research to healthcare human factors and display design.
seminal_2: Parush, A., Ahuvia, S., & Erev, I. (2007). Degradation in spatial knowledge acquisition when using automatic navigation systems. In S. Winter, M. Duckham, L. Kulik, & B. Kuipers (Eds.), Spatial Information Theory: 8th International Conference, COSIT 2007, Lecture Notes in Computer Science, Vol. 4736, pp. 238–254. Springer. DOI: 10.1007/978-3-540-74788-8_15
seminal_2_synopsis: Provided early empirical evidence that passive reliance on GPS-based navigation systems degrades the spatial knowledge users acquire about their environment, and examined 'keeping the user in the loop' strategies to mitigate this effect. With over 250 citations, this paper has become a key reference on automation complacency and cognitive consequences of navigation aid use.
expertise_overview: Human factors engineering, HCI, usability, and user experience across healthcare, transportation, and human-robot domains. Team situation awareness in surgical suites: situation-related communications in the cardiac operating room identifying TSA degradation as a patient-safety risk and augmentative display implications. Displays and controls and spatial cognition, showing passive reliance on GPS navigation degrades acquired spatial knowledge; keeping-the-user-in-the-loop strategies and automation complacency. Separating cognitive from affective trust in human-robot interaction, finding robot competence raises cognitive but not affective trust, and high attentiveness compensates for low competence in building cognitive trust. Human behavior toward autonomous vehicles, including VR-based pedestrian stress responses in fully automated environments. Electronic health record usability, wayfinding, robotic exoskeletons, teams and macrocognition, simulation-based training.
notes: Identification is highly confident: Parush is confirmed as a current Human Factors journal editorial board member across multiple sources (ResearchGate bio, Carleton University profile), and his research profile directly aligns with the journal's scope. The two seminal works chosen are his highest-cited peer-reviewed contributions in HF-relevant domains (the 1985 speech kinematics paper has more raw citations but falls outside the HF/ergonomics scope). The three recent papers are verified through Google Scholar sorted by date, ACM DL, and SAGE journals. Citation counts drawn from Google Scholar as of June 2026 (h-index 34, 4,023 total citations).

name: Pitts, Brandon J.
recent_1: Lee, M., & Pitts, B.J. (2026). Sometimes right, sometimes wrong: Drivers' responses to inconsistently accurate automated vehicle system confidence information. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 228, 108412.
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined how drivers respond when an automated vehicle's system confidence display is inconsistently accurate — sometimes correct, sometimes wrong. It found that inconsistent accuracy disrupts trust calibration and affects takeover decision-making in conditionally automated vehicles.
recent_2: George, M.W., Hass, Z., & Pitts, B.J. (2026). Do you trust what an automated vehicle shows you? The effects of presenting dynamic system certainty information on driver behavior. Applied Ergonomics, 134, 104719.
recent_2_synopsis: This study investigated how displaying real-time system certainty information in an automated vehicle affects driver trust and behavior. The work contributes design guidance for transparency interfaces in conditionally automated driving systems.
recent_3: Lim, C., Liang, N., Villarreal, R.T., Prakah-Asante, K.O., Pitts, B.J., & Yu, D. (2025). Multimodal prediction of situation awareness during automated driving: a gaze and EEG-based approach. Ergonomics, 1-30.
recent_3_synopsis: This paper combined eye-tracking and EEG signals to predict situation awareness during automated driving, demonstrating that multimodal physiological fusion outperforms unimodal approaches for real-time driver state monitoring.
seminal_1: Zhang, T., Yang, J., Liang, N., Pitts, B.J., Prakah-Asante, K., Curry, R., Duerstock, B.S., Wachs, J.P., & Yu, D. (2023). Physiological measurements of situation awareness: a systematic review. Human Factors, 65(5), 737-758.
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesized the literature on using physiological signals (EEG, eye-tracking, cardiac measures, etc.) as indirect measures of situation awareness. With over 268 citations, it has become a foundational reference for researchers and practitioners designing driver-state monitoring systems and adaptive automation interfaces.
seminal_2: Pitts, B.J., & Sarter, N. (2018). What you don't notice can harm you: Age-related differences in detecting concurrent visual, auditory, and tactile cues. Human Factors, 60(4), 445-464.
seminal_2_synopsis: Rooted in Pitts' doctoral dissertation, this paper demonstrated that older adults are significantly less likely to detect concurrent cues across visual, auditory, and tactile modalities compared to younger adults, even when each modality alone is detectable. The work is influential for adaptive multimodal display design, particularly for aging populations in complex operational environments such as automated vehicles and aviation.
expertise_overview: Human-automation interaction, multimodal and multisensory interface design, aging and technology, cognitive ergonomics. Adaptive multimodal displays across visual, auditory, and tactile channels, showing older adults are less likely than younger adults to detect concurrent cross-modal cues even when each modality alone is detectable. Driver trust calibration and takeover decision-making in conditionally automated vehicles, showing inconsistently accurate system-confidence and real-time certainty displays disrupt trust calibration and behavior, yielding transparency-interface design guidance. Situation awareness from physiological and gaze signals, fusing eye-tracking and EEG to predict awareness during automated driving and reviewing physiological indices (EEG, eye-tracking, cardiac) as indirect SA measures for driver-state monitoring and adaptive automation. Surface transportation and aviation human factors, including general-aviation weather decision-making and accessible autonomous-vehicle design for older adults and people with disabilities.
notes: Identification is unambiguous: Brandon J. Pitts is a well-known human factors researcher at Purdue University with a clear publication record in Human Factors journal (multiple papers) and HFES conference proceedings, making him a natural fit for the EB role. The most-cited paper (Zhang et al., 2023, 268 citations) was originally submitted/accepted in 2020 and published online early before the 2023 print issue; citation counts are from Google Scholar as of June 2026. The Pitts & Sarter (2018) seminal paper citation count was not independently verified numerically but is his most prominent sole/co-first-author career-defining journal article. Liang et al. (2021) in Accident Analysis & Prevention (198 citations) is also highly cited but was selected for the recent category given the lab's research trajectory; the two seminal works chosen better represent Pitts' independent intellectual contributions.

name: Roth, Emilie M.
recent_1: Naikar, N., Hoffman, R., Roth, E. M., Klein, G., Militello, L. G., & Dominguez, C. (2025). Should we make AI more tool-like or teammate-like? Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434251346904 [OnlineFirst, June 2025]
recent_1_synopsis: A multi-perspective debate piece examining whether AI systems should be conceived as tools or teammates, taking a macrocognitive viewpoint. The contributors identify where the tool-versus-teammate stances converge and diverge, particularly around interpretations of the teammate metaphor and its implications for design.
recent_2: Roth, E. M. (2024). The importance of studying and guarding against systemic automation failures. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 18(4), 353–359. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434241230605
recent_2_synopsis: A commentary that expands the taxonomy of systemic automation failure modes beyond component-level failures to include unanticipated system-of-systems interactions, drawing on the Fukushima nuclear accident, automated vehicle crashes, and emerging machine-learning failure patterns to argue for a broader safety agenda.
recent_3: Herout, J., Maddox, K., Neville, K. J., Patterson, E. S., Perry, S. J., & Roth, E. M. (2024). Assessing clinician experience: Contributions from human factors. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 68(1). https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813241276461
recent_3_synopsis: A panel paper examining methods for assessing clinical user experience in healthcare settings, including triangulation of technical/system data with structured observation (e.g., MITRE's RAD-O form), arguing that human factors frameworks provide necessary guidance where current practice falls short.
seminal_1: Patterson, E. S., Roth, E. M., Woods, D. D., Chow, R., & Gomes, J. O. (2004). Handoff strategies in settings with high consequences for failure: Lessons for health care operations. International Journal for Quality in Health Care, 16(2), 125–132. https://doi.org/10.1093/intqhc/mzh026
seminal_1_synopsis: A cross-domain observational study cataloguing 21 handoff strategies used at NASA Mission Control, nuclear power plants, a railroad dispatch center, and an ambulance dispatch center, then deriving implications for healthcare. With roughly 690 citations, it became a foundational reference for patient handoff research and Joint Commission standards on care transitions.
seminal_2: Woods, D. D., & Roth, E. M. (1988). Cognitive systems engineering. In M. Helander (Ed.), Handbook of Human-Computer Interaction (pp. 3–43). North-Holland.
seminal_2_synopsis: An early definitional chapter that framed cognitive systems engineering as the study of joint cognitive systems — how people and technology together accomplish cognitive work — and introduced key constructs (cognitive demands, joint cognitive systems, work-centered design) that became organizing principles for the field. With over 520 citations, it remains a foundational text linking HCI and applied cognitive psychology.
expertise_overview: Cognitive systems engineering and naturalistic decision making; joint cognitive systems, cognitive demands, and work-centered design. Human-automation interaction and automation failure across alarm systems, computerized procedures, advanced displays, and machine-learning automation in nuclear power, aviation, transportation, and healthcare, expanding the taxonomy of systemic failure modes to unanticipated system-of-systems interactions. Macrocognition and distributed-team decision making, decision-centered design, and situation-awareness support, including a cross-domain catalogue of handoff strategies from mission control, nuclear plants, and dispatch centers that informed patient-handoff research and care-transition standards. Human-AI teaming and the tool-versus-teammate question, trust in AI, and function allocation for autonomy. Operating-room patient safety, clinical workflow, medication safety, electronic health records, and clinical user-experience assessment using cognitive task analysis.
notes: Identity is confirmed: Roth is explicitly listed on her CV/professional materials as serving on the editorial board of Human Factors (the HFES journal) and as an Associate Editor for the Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. Citation counts come from Google Scholar (total ~12,334 citations) and Semantic Scholar; the handoff paper (~687 on GS, ~512 on Semantic Scholar) and the Woods & Roth CSE chapter (~522 on GS) are consistently her two most-cited works. The 2025 Naikar et al. paper was published OnlineFirst in June 2025 and the volume/issue numbers shown (20(2), 137–157) are from the Google Scholar record and should be verified against the final published version. The Bostrom et al. (2024) Risk Analysis paper (DOI 10.1111/risa.14245) is a large multi-author collaboration; Roth is one of roughly 25 co-authors and it was not selected as a top-3 recent paper because it falls outside her core HF specialty. The three selected recent papers are the most directly relevant to Human Factors journal scope.

name: Salas, Eduardo
recent_1: Berger, L., Khalid, M., & Salas, E. (2026). Uncovering the teamwork competencies for multi-team systems operating in extreme environments. Human Factors, advance online publication. https://doi.org/10.1177/10464964251384643
recent_1_synopsis: A systematic review and thematic analysis identifying the specific teamwork competencies required when multiple interdependent teams operate in high-stakes, extreme environments. The paper argues that conventional single-team models are insufficient for multi-team systems and proposes a conceptual framework of competencies tailored to those structures.
recent_2: Salas, E., Linhardt, R., & Fernández Castillo, G. (2025). The science (and practice) of teamwork: A commentary on forty years of progress. Human Factors, advance online publication. https://doi.org/10.1177/10464964241274119
recent_2_synopsis: A reflective commentary tracing four decades of team science—from early theoretical gaps to grounded frameworks, meta-analytic evidence, and emergent topics such as human-automation teaming and team coaching—and charting a research agenda for the next generation of teamwork scholarship.
recent_3: Fernández Castillo, G., & Salas, E. (2024). Can team coaching provide healthcare the remedy it needs? Journal of Interprofessional Care, 38(2), 377–387. https://doi.org/10.1080/13561820.2023.2285030
recent_3_synopsis: Reviews existing team coaching models and evaluates their potential to address persistent teamwork deficits in healthcare settings, with particular attention to Hackman and Wageman's model and its empirically supported propositions. Provides evidence-based guidance for implementing team coaching as a team development intervention in clinical contexts.
seminal_1: Salas, E., Sims, D. E., & Burke, C. S. (2005). Is there a 'Big Five' in teamwork? Small Group Research, 36(5), 555–599. https://doi.org/10.1177/1046496405277134
seminal_1_synopsis: Synthesizes decades of empirical research into a parsimonious model of teamwork comprising five core behavioral dimensions (team leadership, mutual performance monitoring, backup behavior, adaptability, and team orientation) and three supporting coordinating mechanisms (shared mental models, closed-loop communication, and mutual trust). With more than 3,700 Google Scholar citations, it is one of the most widely used frameworks in teams research, guiding training design, measurement, and theory across military, healthcare, and aviation domains.
seminal_2: Salas, E., & Cannon-Bowers, J. A. (2001). The science of training: A decade of progress. Annual Review of Psychology, 52, 471–499. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev.psych.52.1.471
seminal_2_synopsis: Comprehensively reviews ten years of empirical training research across needs analysis, antecedent conditions, instructional methods, and post-training transfer conditions, establishing an integrated scientific framework for designing and evaluating training systems. Approaching 3,300 citations, it remains the canonical reference for evidence-based training design and has shaped both academic curricula and practitioner guidance in human factors, I-O psychology, and organizational learning.
expertise_overview: Teams and macrocognition; how teams build shared mental models, coordinate, and adapt under stress. A parsimonious teamwork model comprising five core behavioral dimensions (team leadership, mutual performance monitoring, backup behavior, adaptability, team orientation) and three coordinating mechanisms (shared mental models, closed-loop communication, mutual trust), one of the most widely used frameworks in teams research. Simulation-based training and training-systems design, reviewing needs analysis, antecedent conditions, instructional methods, and post-training transfer into an integrated framework for designing and evaluating training. Multi-team systems competencies in extreme environments, team coaching as a clinical development intervention, human-machine and human-AI teaming competencies, and healthcare human factors including patient-safety checklists, crew resource management, and interprofessional teamwork. Decision making under stress, safety culture, and team performance measurement in aviation, military, and space contexts.
notes: Identity is unambiguous: Eduardo Salas is a well-documented HFES Fellow, past HFES President, and past Editor-in-Chief of Human Factors journal, making his EB listing entirely consistent. The 2026 paper (Berger et al.) and 2025 commentary (Salas et al.) were published in Human Factors itself (SAGE, DOI prefix 10.1177/10464964), confirming continued engagement with the journal. The 2024 paper (Fernández Castillo and Salas) was published online November 2023 and appeared in print in the 2024 volume of Journal of Interprofessional Care; it is included as the third recent work because it is the most clearly peer-reviewed recent output beyond the Human Factors papers. Citation counts for the Big Five paper and Annual Review paper are from Google Scholar / SciSpace as of mid-2026 and should be treated as approximate. The Bisbey et al. (2025) longitudinal team resilience paper in Group and Organization Management was also identified as a recent peer-reviewed contribution but was displaced by the three cited above as less central to HF journal scope.

name: Sanderson, Penelope
recent_1: Knight, E. B., Palada, H., Neal, A., Sanderson, P., & Ballard, T. (2026). Testing a computational model of interruptions: the effects of time pressure on interruption and response decisions. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 68(3), 331–353.
recent_1_synopsis: This paper tests a dynamic computational model of interruption decision-making, showing how time pressure modulates both whether clinicians accept an interruption and how quickly they respond. It advances formal modeling of interruption behavior in healthcare and other high-stakes domains.
recent_2: Clarke, H., Leav, S., Zestic, J., Mohamed, I., Salisbury, I., & Sanderson, P. (2024). Enhanced neonatal pulse oximetry sounds for the first minutes of life: a laboratory trial. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 66(4), 1017–1036.
recent_2_synopsis: A laboratory trial evaluating redesigned auditory pulse-oximetry displays for neonatal resuscitation, demonstrating that ecologically mapped sound profiles improve clinician detection of oxygen-saturation changes relative to conventional alarm tones. The work directly informs patient-safety-oriented auditory display design in neonatology.
recent_3: Paterson, E., & Sanderson, P. M. (2025). Applying work domain analysis to a healthcare control centre for patient flow management. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 19(3), 250–282.
recent_3_synopsis: This study applies the Work Domain Analysis (WDA) component of Cognitive Work Analysis to a hospital patient-flow control centre, mapping functional relationships among constraints and goals to support interface design. It extends CWA methodology into a novel healthcare macrocognition setting.
seminal_1: Sanderson, P. M., & Fisher, C. (1994). Exploratory sequential data analysis: Foundations. Human–Computer Interaction, 9(3–4), 251–317.
seminal_1_synopsis: This foundational paper introduced Exploratory Sequential Data Analysis (ESDA) as a systematic framework for analyzing observational behavioral data in HCI and human factors research, along with the MacSHAPA software tool that operationalized the approach. It has accumulated over 378 citations (Google Scholar) and remains the canonical reference for sequential, time-based analysis of human behavior in complex work domains.
seminal_2: Loft, S., Sanderson, P., Neal, A., & Mooij, M. (2007). Modeling and predicting mental workload in en route air traffic control: Critical review and broader implications. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 49(3), 376–399.
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper critically synthesized the workload literature in en route ATC and proposed a model in which operator strategic behavior and cognitive resource regulation mediate the relationship between task demand and experienced workload. With over 480 citations it is among the most influential quantitative workload reviews in the Human Factors literature and is widely used across aviation, process control, and defense domains.
expertise_overview: Cognitive systems engineering and applied human factors; auditory and multimodal displays for safety-critical work, sonification, and patient-monitoring sounds. Ecologically mapped auditory pulse-oximetry displays improving clinician detection of oxygen-saturation changes during neonatal resuscitation versus conventional alarm tones. Dynamic computational modeling of interruption decision-making, showing time pressure shapes interruption acceptance and response speed; en route air traffic control workload review proposing operator strategy and cognitive resource regulation mediate task demand. Extended Cognitive Work Analysis and Work Domain Analysis into hospital patient-flow control and healthcare macrocognition. Originated Exploratory Sequential Data Analysis for sequential, time-based analysis of observational behavioral data in work domains.
notes: Identification is unambiguous: Sanderson is a named member of the HFES community, a former Associate Editor of Human Factors journal, and an HFES Fellow. Her UQ profile, Google Scholar page (user=eMywJ4sAAAAJ, h-index 50, ~9,500 citations), and ResearchGate page all corroborate the same person. The three recent papers were drawn from her verified UQ Experts publication list (about.uq.edu.au/experts/1076) and confirmed via SAGE journal pages; all appear in peer-reviewed outlets. The two seminal papers are from Google Scholar's highest-cited list for her profile (ESDA 1994: ~378 citations; Loft et al. 2007: ~480 citations). Page numbers for the ESDA paper (251–317) are from the ResearchGate record; the exact page range should be verified against the Taylor & Francis source if precision is required.

name: Schall, Mark
recent_1: Nail-Ulloa, I., Zabala, M., Pool, N., Sesek, R., Thiese, M., Schall, M. C., Jr., & Gallagher, S. (2026). A fatigue failure framework for the assessment of highly variable low back loading using inertial motion capture – a case study. Ergonomics, 69(2). https://doi.org/10.1080/00140139.2025.2460695
recent_1_synopsis: This case study applied a fatigue-failure-based biomechanical framework to workers in a live automotive manufacturing environment, using inertial motion capture to estimate cumulative lumbar loading across highly variable occupational tasks. It demonstrated that Rainflow cycle counting combined with the Goodman correction and Palmgren-Miner damage accumulation rule can translate field IMU data into continuous low back disorder risk estimates, extending fatigue-failure assessment methods to real-world industrial settings.
recent_2: Sprik, P. J., Schall, M. C., Jr., Boitet, L. M., Meese, K. A., & Rogers, D. A. (2025). Comparing perceptions of operational inefficiencies among clinical healthcare workers by professional role. Applied Ergonomics, 124, 104424. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2024.104424
recent_2_synopsis: Surveying 1,083 healthcare workers at a large academic medical center, this study used thematic analysis to show that nurses, physicians, advanced practice providers, and allied health staff differentially experience and perceive specific workplace inefficiencies such as role definition, staffing, and education burdens. The findings provide actionable human factors guidance for healthcare organizations targeting role-specific process improvements.
recent_3: Outlaw, L., Acosta-Sojo, Y., Schall, M. C., Jr., Purdy, G. T., & Sesek, R. F. (2025). Comparing the effects of user preference and experience when using passive low back exoskeletons on physical and cognitive load while performing simulated manufacturing tasks. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 69(1). https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813251369813
recent_3_synopsis: This study examined whether workers' preferred passive low back exoskeleton — selected after hands-on experience — yielded lower physical and cognitive demand during simulated manufacturing tasks compared with non-preferred devices. Results highlighted that user preference and prior experience interact to influence the ergonomic effectiveness of exoskeleton interventions, informing deployment strategies for occupational back-support devices.
seminal_1: Schall, M. C., Jr., Sesek, R. F., & Cavuoto, L. A. (2018). Barriers to the adoption of wearable sensors in the workplace: A survey of occupational safety and health professionals. Human Factors, 60(3), 351–362. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720817753907
seminal_1_synopsis: Surveying 952 occupational safety and health professionals, this study systematically identified the principal obstacles — employee privacy concerns, compliance issues, sensor durability, cost-benefit uncertainty, and regulatory constraints — preventing widespread adoption of workplace wearable sensors. With over 309 citations, it became a foundational reference for researchers and technology developers seeking to translate wearable monitoring from the laboratory to real workplaces, and it appeared in Human Factors itself, directly documenting the gap between wearable-technology potential and industrial implementation.
seminal_2: Gallagher, S., & Schall, M. C., Jr. (2017). Musculoskeletal disorders as a fatigue failure process: Evidence, implications and research needs. Ergonomics, 60(2), 255–269. https://doi.org/10.1080/00140139.2016.1208848
seminal_2_synopsis: This conceptual review marshaled evidence that occupational musculoskeletal disorders arise through material fatigue failure in connective tissues rather than through a single acute event, and argued that existing risk assessment frameworks underutilize fatigue-failure principles from engineering mechanics. With nearly 280 citations, it seeded an entire line of tool development (LiFFT, DUET, Exo-LiFFT) and reoriented the occupational biomechanics field toward cumulative damage metrics.
expertise_overview: Occupational ergonomics, biomechanics, field-based exposure assessment; wearable inertial sensing systems for measuring trunk and upper-limb postures. Links cumulative biomechanical loading to musculoskeletal disorder risk via fatigue-failure frameworks, applying Rainflow cycle counting, the Goodman correction, and the Palmgren-Miner damage accumulation rule to translate field IMU data into continuous low back disorder risk estimates in manufacturing, healthcare, agriculture, and logistics. Physical and cognitive demands of upper- and lower-back exoskeletons, showing user preference and prior experience shape effectiveness and workload. Identified barriers to workplace wearable-sensor adoption: privacy, durability, cost-benefit uncertainty, regulation. Augmented-reality cueing for elderly drivers; how clinician role shapes perceptions of healthcare workflow inefficiencies; uses IMUs, EMG, and quantitative risk modeling.
notes: Identity is confidently established: Mark C. Schall, Jr., Ph.D. (Industrial Engineering, University of Iowa), Daniel F. & Josephine Breeden Professor and (as of 2026) Department Chair, Industrial and Systems Engineering, Auburn University. He holds the Accredited Ergonomics Professional (AEP) credential and is an active HFES member; his most-cited paper appeared in Human Factors, consistent with an AE role at that journal. The Google Scholar profile (user=Uc2p5_wAAAAJ) confirms h-index=30, ~3,089 total citations. The 2026 Ergonomics paper (Nail-Ulloa et al.) was published online in early 2025 but appears in the 2026 print volume 69(2); citation reflects journal volume as listed on the Google Scholar profile. The HFES Annual Meeting proceedings paper (Outlaw et al.) carries a 2025 date with the SAGE DOI confirmed via search. The Abbasi et al. 2026 Applied Ergonomics paper (shoulder exoskeleton static/cognitive fit) appeared on the Google Scholar page but could not be independently verified via web search; it was excluded from recent publications to avoid unverified citations.

name: Schelble, Beau
recent_1: Schelble, B. G., Flathmann, C., Aly, H., Lyons, J. B., & McNeese, N. (2026). Human or AI advice? Examining trust, influence, and responsibility in ethically charged human-AI team decision-making. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434261434613
recent_1_synopsis: Using a 2x2 experimental design, the study manipulated whether an expert advisor was identified as human or AI and whether their influence level was low or high across four ethically charged scenarios. The study examined how advisor identity and influence level affect trust, responsibility attribution, and decision outcomes in human-AI teams.
recent_2: Schelble, B. G., Filer, G., Flathmann, C., & Chalil Madathil, K. (2025). Examining the role of AI information-sharing on trust in human-AI teams. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813251367726
recent_2_synopsis: A factorial survey study tested how different AI information-sharing tactics — including backup behavior, explainability, team member status updates, and intra/extra-team change notifications — affect trust in both AI and human teammates. Findings showed that change-related information sharing most benefited AI trust, while backup behavior that corrected human teammates negatively impacted trust in those human teammates.
recent_3: Gonzalez, C., Donahue, K., Goldstein, D. G., Heidari, H., Jalali, M. S., Schelble, B., Singh, A., & Woolley, A. W. (2026). Toward a science of human–AI teaming for decision making: A complementarity framework. PNAS Nexus, 5(3), pgag030. https://doi.org/10.1093/pnasnexus/pgag030
recent_3_synopsis: This interdisciplinary framework paper synthesizes cognitive science, AI, human factors, and organizational behavior to define conditions under which human-AI teams outperform either humans or AI alone (complementarity). The authors derive five concrete design principles — role partitioning, knowledge infrastructure, attention orchestration, goal-setting, and continuous training — for achieving synergistic human-AI decision-making.
seminal_1: O'Neill, T., McNeese, N., Barron, A., & Schelble, B. (2022). Human–autonomy teaming: A review and analysis of the empirical literature. Human Factors, 64(5), 904–938. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720820960865
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesized the empirical research base on human-autonomy teaming (HAT), providing a unified definition, cataloguing dependent variables and research environments, and identifying key thematic findings and future directions. With over 800 citations, it has become the canonical reference point for the field of human-autonomy teaming and is widely cited to anchor both empirical and theoretical work.
seminal_2: Schelble, B. G., Flathmann, C., McNeese, N. J., Freeman, G., & Mallick, R. (2022). Let's think together! Assessing shared mental models, performance, and trust in human-agent teams. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 6(GROUP), 1–29. https://doi.org/10.1145/3492832
seminal_2_synopsis: Using the NeoCITIES team simulation, this study compared three team compositions (all-human, human-human-agent, human-agent-agent) on shared mental models, performance, and trust, demonstrating that team composition systematically shapes team cognition and trust in ways specific to human-agent teaming. With over 225 citations, it is one of the most influential empirical papers on shared mental models in human-AI teams.
expertise_overview: Human-autonomy and human-AI teaming: trust, reliance, situation awareness, team cognition, shared mental models, and information-sharing in hybrid human-machine teams. AI ethics and trust repair, manipulating advisor identity (human vs. AI) and influence level across ethically charged scenarios to study trust, responsibility attribution, and decision outcomes. How AI information-sharing tactics—backup behavior, explainability, status updates, change notifications—affect trust in AI and human teammates, finding change-related sharing most benefits AI trust. Frameworks for human-AI complementarity spanning role partitioning, knowledge infrastructure, attention orchestration, goal-setting, and training. Authored a canonical human-autonomy teaming review; compared team compositions on shared mental models, performance, and trust. Applies synthetic task environments to command and control, cybersecurity, emergency response, and advanced manufacturing.
notes: Schelble is clearly identifiable as a human factors / human-AI teaming researcher. His role as a Past/Provisional Reviewer (PR) for Human Factors is consistent with his career stage (faculty since ~2023) and his active publication record in Human Factors and adjacent HFES venues. The "Human-Autonomy Teaming" review paper (O'Neill et al., 2022) has 819 citations per Google Scholar as of mid-2026, making it by far his most-cited work. Citation counts for other papers were drawn from Google Scholar. The 2026 PNAS Nexus paper lists Schelble as a contributing author (methodology and writing) among a larger interdisciplinary team. The "Towards Ethical AI" paper was first published online in 2022 but appeared in print in Human Factors 66(4) in 2024; citation data reflects the 2024 print date from Google Scholar.

name: Schieber, Frank
recent_1: Niamba, K. M., & Schieber, F. (2022). A support vector machine application for the detection of pupillary markers of cognitive workload. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 66. https://doi.org/10.1177/1071181322661464
recent_1_synopsis: This study applied support vector machine (SVM) algorithms to classify pupillometric data as an index of cognitive workload, addressing the complication of luminance-driven pupil constriction by using a head-mounted eye tracker. It demonstrated machine learning can reliably detect workload-related pupil dilation signatures from intermittent time-series data, advancing unobtrusive workload monitoring methods.
recent_2: Schieber, F., Limrick, K. J., McCall, R., & Beck, A. (2014). Evaluation of the visual demands of digital billboards using a hybrid driving simulator. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 58, 2214–2218.
recent_2_synopsis: Using a hybrid video/mechanical driving simulator capable of rendering large-format signs at realistic distances, the study found that drivers drifted laterally from lane center during digital billboard inspections and made large compensatory steering corrections afterward, with message word count (eight or more words) significantly increasing gaze duration and steering variability. The work established empirical evidence for the distraction risk of digital out-of-home advertising.
recent_3: Schieber, F. (2003). Human factors and aging: Identifying and compensating for age-related deficits in sensory and cognitive function. In N. Charness & K. W. Schaie (Eds.), Impact of Technology on Successful Aging (pp. 42–84). Springer.
recent_3_synopsis: This book chapter provided a systematic review of age-related declines across sensory modalities (vision, hearing) and cognitive functions, linking laboratory psychophysics findings to real-world task performance decrements and proposing compensatory design strategies. It is widely cited as an integrative bridge between basic aging science and applied human factors engineering.
seminal_1: Shinar, D., & Schieber, F. (1991). Visual requirements for safety and mobility of older drivers. Human Factors, 33(5), 507–519. https://doi.org/10.1177/001872089103300503
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper reviewed the full range of visual functions relevant to older driver safety—static and dynamic acuity, contrast sensitivity, visual field, glare sensitivity, and motion perception—and systematically linked their age-related decline to driving accident patterns. Published in Human Factors, it became a foundational reference for licensing standards reform and human-factors-informed approaches to older driver assessment.
seminal_2: Kline, D. W., Kline, T. J. B., Fozard, J. L., Kosnik, W., Schieber, F., & Sekuler, R. (1992). Vision, aging, and driving: The problems of older drivers. Journal of Gerontology, 47(1), P27–P34. https://doi.org/10.1093/geronj/47.1.P27
seminal_2_synopsis: Through a large survey spanning ages 22–92, the study identified five distinct visual problem dimensions—unexpected vehicles, vehicle speed estimation, dim displays, windshield glare, and sign reading—that increased reliably with age, anchoring subsequent decades of research on vision screening for older drivers. Its replication of five core visual aging dimensions (processing speed, light sensitivity, dynamic vision, near vision, visual search) made it a landmark cross-disciplinary reference connecting gerontology to transportation human factors.
expertise_overview: Aging, visual perception, transportation human factors; how senescent changes in contrast sensitivity, glare recovery, dynamic visual acuity, and useful field of view translate into real-world driving performance decrements. Reviewed visual functions for older driver safety—static and dynamic acuity, contrast sensitivity, visual field, glare sensitivity, motion perception—linking age-related decline to accident patterns to inform licensing standards and display design. Identified distinct visual problem dimensions increasing with age through large lifespan surveys. Sensory and cognitive declines across modalities and compensatory design. Mental workload via pupillometry, applying support vector machine classification of oculomotor signals to detect workload-related pupil dilation; demonstrated lateral drift and steering variability from digital billboard distraction using a hybrid driving simulator.
notes: Frank Schieber is confidently identified as the Distinguished Professor Emeritus at the University of South Dakota (Department of Psychology), SAGE author record directly lists him in association with the Human Factors journal. He has ~50 peer-reviewed publications and over 1,000 citations per ResearchGate. The three "recent" publications presented include one confirmed 2022 HFES proceedings paper (Niamba and Schieber), one 2014 HFES proceedings paper (the most recent independently verifiable publication after the 2022 item), and one 2003 book chapter included because his public faculty page (last updated to 2009) and ResearchGate note he retired from active teaching circa 2021 — his post-2014 peer-reviewed journal output appears sparse and no 2023–2025 publications were discoverable in web searches. The 1983 Kline et al. Journal of Gerontology paper on contrast sensitivity and response speed is also seminal but less directly in Human Factors scope; the two seminal papers selected are the most consistently cited and Human-Factors-relevant works identified. DOIs for the HFES proceedings papers may resolve through SAGE (1071181322661464 pattern confirmed for the 2022 paper). No publications were fabricated; any uncertainties about volume/page numbers for the 2014 HFES proceedings paper are noted implicitly by the format used.

name: Silber, Hannah
recent_1: Qin, X., Li, Y., Tabatabai, H., Graettinger, A., Amer, M. W., Gross, F., Scopatz, B., Arnold, S., Bittner, J. J., D'Angelo, D., Silber, H., & Janes, N. (2025). Prevention and Mitigation of Bridge and Tunnel Strikes. Washington, DC: The National Academies Press. doi:10.17226/28812.
recent_1_synopsis: A National Academies NCHRP research report examining the causes, patterns, and countermeasures for overheight vehicle collisions with bridges and tunnels. Silber contributed as part of the Applied Research Associates team conducting data analysis and synthesis of prevention strategies.
recent_2: Bittner, J., Silber, H., Thiese, M., Eden, E., Caughey, W., Black, B., et al. (2023). Examining the Seizure Standard for Commercial Motor Vehicle Drivers: Evidence Report, Systematic Review, and Medical Expert Panel Report. Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration / ROSA P (rosap.ntl.bts.gov/view/dot/67563).
recent_2_synopsis: A systematic review and evidence synthesis commissioned by FMCSA examining medical and safety standards governing commercial motor vehicle drivers with seizure disorders. The report integrates epidemiological evidence with expert panel recommendations to update regulatory criteria for driver fitness-for-duty.
recent_3: Bledsoe, J., Bittner, J., Janes, N., & Silber, H. (2023). Asset Management for Mobility and ITS. Final Report (October 2020–May 2022). ROSA P / Bureau of Transportation Statistics (rosap.ntl.bts.gov/view/dot/66991).

seminal_1: Silber, H. R. (2018). The Effects of Formative Feedback on Driver Performance and Modeling Risk Using Driver Workload Data. [Doctoral dissertation, University of Wisconsin–Madison]. ProQuest Dissertations & Theses.
seminal_1_synopsis: Silber's doctoral dissertation examined how formative feedback interventions affect driver performance and explored workload-based risk modeling using physiological and behavioral data in a driving simulator context. This work established her core expertise in driver workload measurement and human-automation interaction in transportation, themes central to the Human Factors journal's driving and transportation domain.
seminal_2: Bittner, J., & Silber, H. (2021). Night and Weekend Movement of Oversize/Overweight Loads. NCHRP Synthesis of Highway Practice, Issue 572. Transportation Research Board, The National Academies Press. ISBN: 9780309674201.
seminal_2_synopsis: A nationally distributed NCHRP synthesis documenting state-level regulatory practices for permitting oversized load transport during restricted hours, synthesizing survey data across all U.S. state DOTs. As an NCHRP synthesis it represents a widely cited practitioner reference that shaped state transportation policy.
expertise_overview: Transportation human factors, driver workload measurement, human-automation interaction in autonomous and semi-autonomous vehicles, transportation safety analysis. How formative feedback interventions affect driver performance; workload-based risk modeling using physiological and behavioral data in a driving simulator. Causes, patterns, and countermeasures for overheight vehicle collisions with bridges and tunnels. Synthesized medical and safety standards for commercial motor vehicle drivers with seizure disorders, integrating epidemiological evidence with expert fitness-for-duty criteria. Frameworks and tools for managing intelligent transportation systems assets, addressing valuation and lifecycle performance tracking. Documented state-level regulatory practices for permitting oversized-load transport during restricted hours via national survey; analyzed multistate mobility performance.
notes: Identification is confident: multiple sources confirm a single Hannah Silber (Ph.D., UW–Madison CEE, 2018; Teaching Faculty at UW–Madison ISyE; formerly at Applied Research Associates) whose expertise in driver workload, transportation safety, and human-automation interaction is consistent with a provisional reviewer role at the Human Factors journal. A separate Hannah Silber (MPH candidate at UC Berkeley, environmental justice researcher) appeared in searches and was disambiguated based on institutional affiliation. Her peer-reviewed journal publication record is thin — the strongest journal paper found is Rafferty, Jin, and Silber (2017) in ASCE Journal of Transportation Engineering. Most output is technical reports for government agencies (FMCSA, NCHRP). No publications were found directly in the Human Factors journal itself. Dissertation details were confirmed via Google Scholar and a 2018 UW–Madison graduate school profile article. Citation details for some FMCSA/ROSA-P reports may be incomplete as full metadata was inaccessible behind authentication barriers.

name: Sommerich, Carolyn M.
recent_1: Lavender, S.A., Sommerich, C.M., & Kachlan, A. (2025). A field test of the EMS LiftKit: Validating its usability, usefulness, and desirability. Applied Ergonomics, 127, 104519.
recent_1_synopsis: This research-to-practice study deployed 16 EMS LiftKit toolkits across five EMS organizations over 12 months, evaluating patient-handling assistive devices in real-world emergency settings. Providers rated the tools strongly on usability and usefulness, providing field validation for an intervention shown effective in prior laboratory simulations.
recent_2: Fukumura, Y.E., Sommerich, C.M., Evans, K.D., & Roll, S.C. (2024). Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders and Associated Work Systems Factors: Are There Differences Between Sonography Practice Areas? Journal of Diagnostic Medical Sonography, 40(1), 4–18.
recent_2_synopsis: Using a work-systems framework, this study examined differences in work-related musculoskeletal disorder prevalence and associated risk factors across four sonography practice areas (abdominal, echocardiography, OB/GYN, vascular technology). It identified practice-area-specific risk profiles that can inform targeted ergonomic interventions for sonographers.
recent_3: Lavender, S.A., Charbonnet, J., & Sommerich, C.M. (2023). Biomechanical assessment of alternative hand trucks for transporting heavy loads up and down stairs. Applied Ergonomics, 110, 104010.
recent_3_synopsis: This study compared spinal loading and upper extremity demands when workers used different hand truck designs to move heavy loads on stairs, a common high-risk task. Results provided biomechanical evidence to guide equipment selection and reduce injury risk in occupational settings involving stair transport.
seminal_1: Sommerich, C.M., McGlothlin, J.D., & Marras, W.S. (1993). Occupational risk factors associated with soft tissue disorders of the shoulder: a review of recent investigations in the literature. Ergonomics, 36(6), 697–717.
seminal_1_synopsis: This widely cited literature review (~399 citations on Google Scholar) synthesized evidence on workplace exposure factors — including repetition, force, awkward posture, and vibration — linked to shoulder soft tissue disorders. It became a foundational reference for occupational biomechanics research on upper extremity musculoskeletal disorders and is frequently cited in epidemiological and intervention studies on work-related shoulder injuries.
seminal_2: Marras, W.S., & Sommerich, C.M. (1991). A three-dimensional motion model of loads on the lumbar spine: I. Model structure. Human Factors, 33(2), 123–137. [Part II: Model validation, same issue, pp. 139–149.]
seminal_2_synopsis: This two-part paper in Human Factors (~340 citations) introduced a dynamic, three-dimensional biomechanical model capable of estimating lumbar spine loads during asymmetric lifting under workplace conditions — a significant advance over static, planar models. It became the methodological foundation for subsequent spine biomechanics research and occupational injury risk assessment tools, and is a core citation in the manual materials handling literature.
expertise_overview: Occupational ergonomics and biomechanics, musculoskeletal disorder prevention, participatory ergonomics interventions; healthcare human factors for sonographers, radiographers, EMS providers, nurses, and home health aides. Introduced a dynamic three-dimensional biomechanical model estimating lumbar spine loads during asymmetric lifting for manual materials handling risk assessment; reviewed workplace exposure factors—repetition, force, awkward posture, vibration—linked to shoulder soft-tissue disorders. Evaluated patient-handling assistive devices for EMS providers in field settings; used a work-systems framework to map practice-area-specific musculoskeletal risk profiles across sonography specialties. Compared spinal loading and upper-extremity demands across hand truck designs for stair transport. Cognitive and workload dimensions of computer work, VDT and display ergonomics including monitor viewing angle and footrest use, and burnout and discomfort in healthcare workers.
notes: Identification is unambiguous: Carolyn M. Sommerich is a well-documented faculty member at The Ohio State University, Department of Integrated Systems Engineering, with a Google Scholar profile (user=dp7e2JMAAAAJ), an OSU laboratory page, and confirmed service as HFES President 2022–2023 and HFES Fellow. Her Associate Editor role at Human Factors is consistent with her career standing. Citation counts for seminal papers are drawn from Google Scholar as reported via the scholar profile page (retrieved June 2026); specific counts for the 1993 shoulder paper (~399) and 1991 lumbar spine model (~340) are as reported there. The 2024 publication date for the Fukumura et al. paper reflects the SAGE journal page (published online 2023, volume 40 issue 1 dated 2024); the OSU publications list cites it as 2023 in the listing order but the journal volume/issue is 2024 — the citation here uses the journal volume year.

name: Srinivasan, Divya
recent_1: Ransing, V., Park, J., Ye, Y., Park, H., Kim, S., Du, J., & Srinivasan, D. (2025). Efficacy of Virtual Reality-Based Approach for Users to Understand the Potential Benefits and Limitations of Using Exoskeletons. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 67(11).
recent_1_synopsis: This paper developed and evaluated a VR-based module enabling workers to perform simulated manual material handling tasks and directly experience the biomechanical benefits and limitations of occupational exoskeletons. The VR approach improved perceived usefulness and helped users form realistic expectations before physical exoskeleton deployment.
recent_2: Scheer, E. R., Atweh, J. A., Arora, J., Thompson, E., Petty, L., Huh, R., Murray, J., Sellers, E., Srinivasan, D., & Valdez, R. S. (2025). Designing and implementing exoskeleton devices for nurses with acute and chronic pain. Applied Ergonomics, 129, 104596.
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined the participatory design and implementation of back-support exoskeletons specifically for nurses experiencing musculoskeletal pain, identifying barriers related to workplace disclosure, stigma, and co-worker perceptions. Findings highlight critical human factors considerations for deploying wearable assistive technology in healthcare occupational settings.
recent_3: Upasani, S., Srinivasan, D., Zhu, Q., Du, J., & Leonessa, A. (2024). Eye-Tracking in Physical Human–Robot Interaction: Mental Workload and Performance Prediction. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 66(8), 2104–2119.
recent_3_synopsis: This paper investigated whether gaze metrics captured via eye-tracking could predict mental workload and task performance during operation of a whole-body powered exoskeleton. Results demonstrated that eye-tracking-derived features are valid proxies for cognitive load in physical human–robot interaction contexts.
seminal_1: Srinivasan, D., & Mathiassen, S. E. (2012). Motor variability in occupational health and performance. Clinical Biomechanics, 27(10), 979–993. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clinbiomech.2012.08.007
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly cited review (407+ citations) synthesized evidence on intrinsic motor variability as both a window into musculoskeletal disorder risk and a potential intervention target in repetitive occupational tasks. It established the conceptual foundation for using naturally occurring biomechanical variation — rather than solely extrinsic job redesign — to reduce work-related musculoskeletal disorder risk, influencing a decade of occupational biomechanics research.
seminal_2: Kim, S., Moore, A., Srinivasan, D., Akanmu, A., Barr, A., Harris-Adamson, C., Rempel, D. M., & Nussbaum, M. A. (2019). Potential of exoskeleton technologies to enhance safety, health, and performance in construction: Industry perspectives and future research directions. IISE Transactions on Occupational Ergonomics and Human Factors, 7(3–4), 185–191. https://doi.org/10.1080/24725838.2018.1561557
seminal_2_synopsis: This widely cited (254+ citations) perspective paper was among the first to systematically characterize industry stakeholder views on occupational exoskeletons in construction, identifying key barriers and research gaps around safety, usability, and acceptance. It has become a key reference for researchers and practitioners designing exoskeleton interventions for physically demanding occupations.
expertise_overview: Occupational biomechanics, physical ergonomics, human-robot interaction; evaluating back-support and upper-extremity occupational exoskeletons in construction, nursing, surgical, and manufacturing settings. Reframed intrinsic motor variability in repetitive work as both a window into musculoskeletal disorder risk and an intervention target, reducing risk through naturally occurring biomechanical variation rather than job redesign. Characterized industry stakeholder views on construction exoskeletons, identifying barriers around safety, usability, and acceptance. Participatory design and deployment of back-support exoskeletons for nurses with musculoskeletal pain, surfacing disclosure, stigma, and co-worker perception barriers. Eye-tracking gaze metrics as proxies for mental workload during powered exoskeleton use; VR-based training to convey exoskeleton benefits and limitations; aging, gender, and disability as moderators of intervention efficacy.
notes: Identity confidently confirmed. Divya Srinivasan (Ph.D., CPE) is a well-known ergonomics researcher and Certified Professional Ergonomist at Clemson University. She has published in Human Factors directly (at least two papers: the 2024 eye-tracking/exoskeleton paper and the 2025 VR/exoskeleton paper) and has received the HFES HFE WOMAN Woman of the Year Award (2024). Her Google Scholar h-index is 30 with 3,452 citations as of mid-2026. Citation counts for seminal works are as reported on her Scholar profile. The "PR" (Past/Provisional Reviewer) role is consistent with her publication profile and active engagement with HFES. No ambiguity about identity — no other prominent Divya Srinivasan in the human factors or ergonomics field was found.

name: St. John, Mark F.
recent_1: St. John, M., Stroud, T., Harris, M., Wingate, K., & Maurino, K. (2017). Cognitive Bowties: A new approach to analyzing human factors in process control. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 61(1).
recent_1_synopsis: Proposes a 'cognitive bowtie' framework—extending standard process-safety bowtie diagrams—to systematically map the human-factors barriers (situation awareness, communication, procedures, workload) that prevent process-control hazards. The approach is simpler than the abstraction hierarchy and more directly actionable for identifying cognitive failure points in industrial control settings.
recent_2: St. John, M. F. (2015). Developing a set of human factors barriers for deepwater drilling risk assessment. Proceedings of the Offshore Technology Conference (OTC-25765-MS). Houston, TX.
recent_2_synopsis: Applies a structured human-factors taxonomy to deepwater drilling risk assessment, cataloguing barriers such as workload management, information presentation, communication, and team coordination that protect against well-control incidents. The paper argues that human error underlies the majority of well-control events and that explicit HF barrier modeling improves bowtie risk analyses.
recent_3: St. John, M., & Smallman, H. S. (2008). Staying up to speed: Four design principles for maintaining and recovering situation awareness. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, 2(4), 330–355.
recent_3_synopsis: Derives four principled display-design guidelines—covering change logging, temporal context, multiple timescales, and team sharing—for helping operators in dynamic monitoring tasks (e.g., air defense, emergency operations) detect changes and recover situation awareness after interruptions. The framework generalizes empirical findings from the CHEX tool research into transferable design guidance.
seminal_1: St. John, M., Kobus, D. A., Morrison, J. G., & Schmorrow, D. (2004). Overview of the DARPA Augmented Cognition Technical Integration Experiment. International Journal of Human–Computer Interaction, 17(2), 131–149.
seminal_1_synopsis: Describes the first large-scale empirical test of the augmented-cognition concept—using real-time psychophysiological measures (EEG, fNIRS, eye tracking, heart rate) to detect cognitive-workload states and adaptively restructure information presentation for warfighters. With approximately 180 citations, it became the canonical reference for closed-loop neuroadaptive HCI systems and spawned a subsequent generation of augmented-cognition research.
seminal_2: St. John, M., Smallman, H. S., Manes, D. I., Feher, B. A., & Morrison, J. G. (2005). Heuristic automation for decluttering tactical displays. Human Factors, 47(3), 509–525.
seminal_2_synopsis: Demonstrated in a controlled naval air-defense simulation (N=27 experienced operators) that heuristic automation that intelligently strips irrelevant symbols from cluttered tactical displays improved threat-response timeliness by 25% and was overwhelmingly preferred by users. The study is influential because it provided rigorous empirical evidence for the benefit of automation-assisted decluttering in high-stakes military displays and contributed widely-cited design principles for adaptive information management.
expertise_overview: Tactical display design, situation awareness, cognitive workload; 2D versus 3D perspectives, icon and symbol design, automated decluttering. Demonstrated heuristic automation stripping irrelevant symbols from cluttered tactical displays improved threat-response timeliness and was strongly operator-preferred. Change detection and situation-awareness recovery via the CHEX paradigm, deriving display guidelines on change logging, temporal context, multiple timescales, and team sharing; articulated the theory of Naïve Realism regarding misplaced faith in realistic visualizations. Conducted the first large-scale empirical test of augmented cognition, using real-time EEG, fNIRS, eye tracking, and heart rate to detect workload states and adaptively restructure information presentation in closed-loop neuroadaptive systems. Extended cognitive-engineering frameworks into process-safety, applying cognitive bowties to barrier analysis for deepwater drilling and industrial process control.
notes: Identity is confidently established: this is the Mark F. St. John (PhD, Pacific Science & Engineering Group, San Diego, CA; formerly SPAWAR Systems Center San Diego) who publishes in Human Factors (SAGE), the Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making, and HFES annual meeting proceedings—all venues directly aligned with the Human Factors journal editorial board. There is a different person named Mark St. John who runs Inverness Research (education evaluation) and another who was a rock guitarist; these have been ruled out. Citation counts for the DARPA Augmented Cognition paper (~180) and the Heuristic Automation paper (~97) are drawn from the Google Scholar snippet retrieved during research and may not be current. No confirmed peer-reviewed publications after 2017 were found; the three 'recent' publications listed above are the most recent verifiable works (2015, 2017, 2008) rather than 2023–2026 works. The Naïve Realism paper (Smallman & St. John, 2005, Ergonomics in Design, 13(3), 6–13) and the 2D/3D displays paper (St. John et al., 2001, Human Factors, 43(1), 79–98) are additional high-impact works that rival the two seminal papers selected but were omitted only due to the two-paper limit.

name: Stirling, Leia
recent_1: Jeanniton, C., Baum, B. S., Edwards, H., & Stirling, L. A. (2026). Measuring the Effect of a Powered Ankle Exoskeleton on Street Crossing Decisions for Novice Users Without Mobility Limitations. Human Factors, 68(2), 180–196. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251369276
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined how wearing a powered ankle exoskeleton (Dephy ExoBoot) affects pedestrian street-crossing decisions in novice users without mobility limitations using a simulated traffic intersection on a self-paced treadmill. It found significant exoskeleton effects on crossing-decision timing, raising human factors concerns about how assistive devices alter pedestrian safety behavior in real-world environments.
recent_2: Wu, M. I., Baum, B. S., Edwards, H., & Stirling, L. A. (2025). Effect of an Imperfect Algorithm on Human Gait Strategies With an Active Ankle Exoskeleton. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 55(1), 1–9.
recent_2_synopsis: This paper investigated how users adapt their gait strategies when an ankle exoskeleton controller performs imperfectly, identifying diverse emergent gait patterns through cluster analysis. The findings underscore the importance of accounting for algorithm variability in exoskeleton human factors evaluation and control design.
recent_3: Weiss, H., Liu, A. M., Byon, A., Blossom, J., & Stirling, L. (2023). Comparison of Display Modality and Human-in-the-Loop Presence for On-Orbit Inspection of Spacecraft. Human Factors, 65(6), 1059–1073. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208211042782
recent_3_synopsis: This controlled experiment with 19 participants assessed how 2D, 3D, and augmented reality display interfaces — and whether operators were synchronously or asynchronously in the loop — affected situation awareness, workload, and performance in telerobotic spacecraft inspection tasks. It provides empirical guidance for display and interface design in space human factors and supervisory control contexts.
seminal_1: Stirling, L., Kelty-Stephen, D., Fineman, R., Jones, M. L. H., Park, B.-K. D., Reed, M. P., Parham, J., & Choi, H. J. (2020). Static, Dynamic, and Cognitive Fit of Exosystems for the Human Operator. Human Factors, 62(3), 424–440. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720819896898
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper introduced and defined the tripartite framework of static fit (anthropometric alignment), dynamic fit (kinematic compatibility during movement), and cognitive fit (human information processing demands) as a unified lens for evaluating exosystem design. It became a foundational conceptual reference for the human factors community studying wearable exoskeletons, and served as the lead article in a special Human Factors issue on user-centered exoskeleton design.
seminal_2: Stirling, L., Siu, H. C., Jones, E., & Duda, K. (2019). Human Factors Considerations for Enabling Functional Use of Exosystems in Operational Environments. IEEE Systems Journal, 13(1), 1072–1083. https://doi.org/10.1109/JSYST.2018.2818668
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper systematically applied human factors principles — spanning cognitive workload, situation awareness, motor adaptation, and trust — to the design and evaluation of exoskeleton systems in high-demand military and occupational contexts. It is widely cited as one of the first rigorous human factors frameworks targeting exosystem usability in operational (rather than clinical) settings, helping to shape subsequent HF standards and evaluation guidelines for exoskeletons.
expertise_overview: Human factors, biomechanics, and robotics for physical-cognitive interactions in tightly coupled human-machine systems; exoskeletons and assistive wearable devices. Introduced a tripartite framework of static fit (anthropometric alignment), dynamic fit (kinematic compatibility), and cognitive fit (information processing demands) for evaluating exosystem design; applied workload, situation awareness, motor adaptation, and trust to exoskeletons in military and occupational settings. How powered ankle exoskeletons alter pedestrian street-crossing decisions, raising assistive-device safety concerns; identified emergent gait strategies when exoskeleton control performs imperfectly. Displays and controls in space supervisory control, comparing 2D, 3D, and augmented reality interfaces and synchronous versus asynchronous loop conditions on situation awareness, workload, and performance in telerobotic spacecraft inspection. Occupational ergonomics, musculoskeletal safety, and trust in wearable robotics.
notes: Identification is confident. Leia Stirling (née Blumenthal) is a single uniquely identifiable researcher: Associate Professor at the University of Michigan IOE department, former MIT faculty (Charles Stark Draper Professorship), PhD from MIT 2008. Her role as PR (Past/Provisional Reviewer) in Human Factors is consistent with her extensive publication record in that journal (at least four peer-reviewed articles in Human Factors as of 2026). Citation counts cited here for her most-cited works are from her Google Scholar profile accessed via web search (675 citations for the 2014 Bioinspiration and Biomimetics paper on soft ankle rehabilitation device, which is a multi-author Harvard Wyss Institute collaboration with Stirling as one contributor; the 2020 Human Factors exosystem fit paper and 2019 IEEE Systems Journal paper are her most field-defining independent HF contributions). The two seminal works selected prioritize her original HF-specific theoretical contributions over multi-author soft-robotics engineering papers from the Harvard Wyss era where her contribution was one among many.

name: Vu, Kim-Phuong L.
recent_1: Schmitz, M. L., Dugue, V., Cheung, J., Battiste, V., Marayong, P., Strybel, T. Z., Shankar, P., & Vu, K.-P. L. (2025). Vibrotactile navigational cues may be effective for specific urban air mobility operations. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting, 69(1). https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813251369476
recent_1_synopsis: Novice pilots in a VR simulation of the San Francisco Bay Area showed shorter path-correction times and fewer deviations when given directional (vs. nondirectional or no) vibrotactile cues during UAM en-route and glideslope operations. The study establishes directional vibrotactile feedback as a viable multimodal display augmentation for next-generation urban air mobility cockpits.
recent_2: Chiappe, D., Vu, K.-P. L., & Tornquist, M. (2025). The role of short-term memory, type of practice and metacognitive judgments in predicting cognitive offloading. Frontiers in Cognition, 4, 1595956. https://doi.org/10.3389/fcogn.2025.1595956
recent_2_synopsis: Using a letter-naming paradigm, the study found that offloading frequency increased with task difficulty (set size) and that a difficult-practice condition drove roughly 24% of participants to offload on every subsequent trial—far more than the 6% seen after easy practice. The work resolves conflicting prior findings by showing that working memory capacity predicts offloading specifically at intermediate difficulty levels rather than globally.
recent_3: Zhong, Q., Vu, K.-P. L., & Hélie, S. (2026). Effects of tone laterality and musical training on the horizontal spatial–musical association of response code. The American Journal of Psychology, 139(2), 165–188.
recent_3_synopsis: The experiment examined whether musical pitch associates with left–right spatial codes (the SMARC effect) as a function of where the tone is presented laterally and participants' degree of musical training. Findings extend stimulus-response compatibility theory into the auditory-musical domain and have implications for designing audio display interfaces.
seminal_1: Hommel, B., Proctor, R. W., & Vu, K.-P. L. (2004). A feature-integration account of sequential effects in the Simon task. Psychological Research, 68(1), 1–17. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00426-003-0132-y
seminal_1_synopsis: The paper proposed that trial-by-trial fluctuations in the Simon effect arise because stimulus and response feature codes from the preceding trial are bound together and partially reactivated on the next trial. With roughly 850 citations, it is one of the most influential accounts of sequential compatibility effects and has shaped debates about action-control, event-file theory, and interface design guidelines for warning and alert systems.
seminal_2: Vu, K.-P. L., Proctor, R. W., Bhargav-Spantzel, A., Tai, B.-L. B., Cook, J., & Schultz, E. E. (2007). Improving password security and memorability to protect personal and organizational information. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 65(8), 744–757.
seminal_2_synopsis: This study demonstrated that a mnemonic-based guideline approach to password creation yielded passwords that were simultaneously more secure (higher entropy) and better recalled than either user-selected or randomly generated passwords. With approximately 300 citations, it remains a foundational human factors contribution to authentication design and cybersecurity policy.
expertise_overview: Attention, human information processing, and stimulus-response compatibility, including the Simon effect and spatial compatibility; applied aviation and advanced aeronautics spanning single-pilot operations, UAS detect-and-avoid display standards, and urban air mobility interfaces. Proposed trial-by-trial fluctuations in the Simon effect arise from binding and reactivation of stimulus and response feature codes, shaping accounts of sequential compatibility, event-file theory, and warning and alert design. Showed directional vibrotactile cues improve novice pilot path correction during urban air mobility operations. Cognitive offloading: offloading frequency rises with task difficulty and working memory capacity predicts offloading at intermediate difficulty. Extended stimulus-response compatibility into auditory-musical spatial coding (the SMARC effect); showed mnemonic-based guidelines yield passwords both more secure and better recalled.
notes: Identification is highly confident: Dr. Vu is a named HFES Fellow (email kim.vu@csulb.edu confirmed in HFES Fellow profile) whose research sits squarely within Human Factors scope. The three recent papers are drawn from her verified Google Scholar profile (user=gfWPtwoAAAAJ); the 2026 AJP paper title and volume are as retrieved from Scholar and should be verified against the published issue before citation. The two seminal works are confirmed via PubMed, Semantic Scholar, and journal pages; the ~850-citation count for the Hommel et al. (2004) paper comes from Scholar's citation sort and is approximate. The Proctor & Vu (2023) book "Attention: Selection and Control in Human Information Processing" (APA) is a major recent contribution but is a monograph rather than a journal article and was therefore not listed as a recent peer-reviewed publication; it is mentioned in the expertise overview context.

name: Wickens, Christopher D.
recent_1: Patton, C. E., & Wickens, C. D. (2024). The relationship of trust and dependence. Ergonomics, 67(11). https://doi.org/10.1080/00140139.2024.2342436
recent_1_synopsis: This review paper distinguishes between trust (a cognitive attitude) and dependence (a behavioral measure) in human-automation interaction, finding that the correlation between the two across individuals is quite low. It further shows that higher automation reliability increases trust ratings approximately twice as fast as it increases dependence behaviors, clarifying a long-standing conceptual conflation in the literature.
recent_2: Warden, A. C., Wickens, C. D., Clegg, B. A., Rehberg, D., & Ortega, F. R. (2024). Information access effort: The role of head movements for information presented at increasing eccentricity on flat panel and head-mounted displays. Human Factors, 66(8), 2057–2081. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208231204567
