recent_2_synopsis: This study examined performance costs when two information sources were positioned at increasing eccentricities on flat-panel and augmented-reality head-mounted displays. Results showed that head movements played a significant role in preserving accuracy at greater eccentricities without meaningfully hindering response time, with implications for AR display design.
recent_3: Poole, C. A., Warden, A. C., Wickens, C. D., Raikwar, A., Clegg, B. A., Buckman, M., & Ortega, F. R. (2026). Information access costs with an augmented reality head-mounted display. Human Factors. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251377311
recent_3_synopsis: This paper extended the information access effort research program to AR head-mounted displays, measuring performance costs for a spatial integration task when information sources were presented at increasing eccentricities. The work quantifies how AR display design parameters affect the scan-clutter trade-off for operators performing complex real-world tasks.
seminal_1: Wickens, C. D. (1984). Processing resources in attention. In R. Parasuraman & R. Davies (Eds.), Varieties of attention (pp. 63–101). Academic Press.
seminal_1_synopsis: This foundational book chapter formalized Multiple Resources Theory (MRT), positing that humans possess several distinct pools of cognitive resources differentiated along dimensions of processing stage, sensory modality, processing code, and visual channel. MRT became one of the most widely used frameworks in human factors for predicting dual-task interference, workload, and display design across aviation, driving, and HCI applications, and it continues to be extended and tested more than four decades after publication.
seminal_2: Wickens, C. D. (2008). Multiple resources and mental workload. Human Factors, 50(3), 449–455. https://doi.org/10.1518/001872008X288394
seminal_2_synopsis: This peer-reviewed synthesis updated the 4-D Multiple Resources Model, reviewed 50 years of empirical development, and elaborated the theory's predictions regarding shared vs. separate resource pools and their role in workload assessment. Published in the flagship journal of HFES, it became a canonical reference point for workload research and display design guidelines, accumulating hundreds of citations and serving as the standard modern statement of MRT.
expertise_overview: Attention and mental workload, human-automation interaction, aviation and aerospace human factors, displays and controls, augmented/virtual reality interface design. Originated Multiple Resources Theory, formalizing distinct cognitive resource pools differentiated by processing stage, sensory modality, processing code, and visual channel to predict dual-task interference, guide workload assessment, and inform display design across aviation, driving, and HCI. Distinguished trust as a cognitive attitude from dependence as a behavioral measure, showing low cross-individual correlation and that reliability raises trust roughly twice as fast as dependence. Quantified information access costs and the scan-clutter trade-off on flat-panel and AR head-mounted displays, showing head movements preserve accuracy at greater eccentricities. Updated the 4-D multiple resources model and its shared-versus-separate-resource predictions across 50 years of evidence.
notes: Christopher D. Wickens is unambiguously identified: he is an emeritus/research professor at Colorado State University (formerly University of Illinois), a Fellow of HFES, and a career-long contributor to Human Factors journal topics. His editorial board role (EB) is plausible given his stature. The 2026 paper (Poole et al.) was found listed on SAGE/Human Factors as published in 2026 (ahead of print or in-issue); it is included as the most recent peer-reviewed contribution. Seminal work is dominated by book chapters and review articles rather than single empirical papers; the 1984 Parasuraman/Davies edited volume chapter is the original MRT formulation, while the 2008 Human Factors article is the most-cited peer-reviewed journal statement. Citation counts were not directly retrievable from paywalled databases but are consistent with widely described "hundreds to thousands" of citations across the MRT corpus. The 2024 HFES Proceedings paper on MRT misconceptions was also confirmed published but was not included as a "recent" publication given that a 2026 and 2024 journal paper were available and stronger choices.

name: Wilson, Glenn F.
recent_1: Wilson, G.F., & Eggemeier, F.T. (2020). Psychophysiological assessment of workload in multi-task environments. In D.L. Damos (Ed.), Multiple Task Performance (Chapter 15). CRC Press / Taylor & Francis. DOI: 10.1201/9781003069447-15
recent_1_synopsis: This chapter (originally published in 1991 and reprinted in the 2020 digital edition of the Damos volume) provides a comprehensive review of EEG, cardiac, ocular, and other physiological indicators of mental workload across complex multi-task environments. It serves as a standard reference for choosing and interpreting psychophysiological workload metrics in applied human factors research.
recent_2: Christensen, J.C., Estepp, J.R., Wilson, G.F., & Russell, C.A. (2012). The effects of day-to-day variability of physiological data on operator functional state classification. NeuroImage, 59(1), 57–63.
recent_2_synopsis: This paper examined how natural day-to-day physiological variability affects the accuracy of machine-learning classifiers trained to assess operator cognitive state from EEG and other biosignals. It demonstrated that cross-day generalization of classifiers is substantially degraded and highlighted the need for adaptive or recalibrated models in real-world adaptive automation systems.
recent_3: Wilson, G.F., & Russell, C.A. (2007). Performance Enhancement in an Uninhabited Air Vehicle Task Using Psychophysiologically Determined Adaptive Aiding. Human Factors, 49(6), 1005–1018.
recent_3_synopsis: This study demonstrated that dynamically triggering automation support in a UAV task based on real-time psychophysiological state classification (EEG, cardiac, ocular measures fed through an artificial neural network) significantly improved operator performance compared to no-aiding and fixed-aiding conditions. It provided empirical proof-of-concept for closed-loop, physiologically-driven adaptive automation in aviation.
seminal_1: Wilson, G.F., & Russell, C.A. (2003). Real-time assessment of mental workload using psychophysiological measures and artificial neural networks. Human Factors, 45(4), 635–644. DOI: 10.1518/hfes.45.4.635.27088
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper showed that a system combining multiple physiological signals (EEG, EOG, cardiac, respiration) with artificial neural network classifiers could distinguish cognitive workload states in real time with approximately 85% accuracy across participants performing cognitively demanding tasks. It is widely cited as one of the first demonstrations of a practical, real-time psychophysiological workload monitoring system capable of feeding an adaptive automation architecture.
seminal_2: Parasuraman, R., & Wilson, G.F. (2008). Putting the brain to work: Neuroergonomics past, present, and future. Human Factors, 50(3), 468–474. DOI: 10.1518/001872008X288349
seminal_2_synopsis: This influential paper articulated the scope and agenda of neuroergonomics as a discipline — the application of neuroscience to the study of human performance in real work settings — and charted its key applications including real-time workload assessment, adaptive automation, neuroengineering for disability, and selection and training. Co-authored by the field's founders, it is among the most-cited programmatic statements in the neuroergonomics literature and appeared in a special 50th-anniversary issue of Human Factors.
expertise_overview: Attention and mental workload measurement, adaptive automation, neuroergonomics in aviation and air traffic control. Real-time assessment of operator cognitive state using EEG, cardiac (heart rate, HRV), electrooculographic, and respiratory signals with machine-learning and artificial neural network classifiers. Demonstrated closed-loop, physiologically driven adaptive automation in UAV tasks where state-triggered aiding outperformed no-aiding and fixed-aiding conditions, with multi-signal classifiers distinguishing workload states in real time at roughly 85% accuracy. Documented how day-to-day physiological variability degrades cross-day classifier generalization, motivating recalibrated models. Synthesized EEG, cardiac, and ocular workload indicators across multi-task environments and articulated the neuroergonomics agenda spanning workload assessment, adaptive automation, neuroengineering for disability, and selection and training.
notes: Glenn F. Wilson is confidently identified as the human factors researcher on the HFES editorial board; he is well-known in the Human Factors journal (multiple publications in that journal) and his affiliation with Wright-Patterson AFRL and Physiometrex is consistently confirmed across multiple sources. His primary publication period as a lead author was approximately 1987–2012; a book chapter reprinted digitally in 2020 is the most recent clearly attributable output found. No peer-reviewed journal articles from 2020 onward could be confirmed under his name. The 2007 UAV adaptive aiding paper is listed as 'recent' because it is his most recent primary empirical journal article found, and the 2012 NeuroImage paper and 2020 chapter round out the three most recent confirmed outputs. Citation counts for individual papers were not recoverable from public pages but the 2003 and 2008 Human Factors papers are extensively cited in the neuroergonomics and workload literature.

name: Wiltshire, Travis J.
recent_1: Gorman, J. C., & Wiltshire, T. J. (2024). A typology for the application of team coordination dynamics across increasing levels of dynamic complexity. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 66(1), 5–16.
recent_1_synopsis: This paper develops a three-level typology (Systems I–III) for classifying team coordination dynamics (TCD) approaches according to the degree of dynamic complexity they can accommodate, from systems with stationary behavioral attractors to those requiring continuous adaptive re-coordination. It provides practitioners and researchers with a theoretically grounded framework for choosing appropriate TCD measurement and modeling strategies.
recent_2: Wiltshire, T. J., van Eijndhoven, K., Halgas, E. A., & Gevers, J. M. P. (2024). Prospects for augmenting team interactions with real-time coordination-based measures in human–autonomy teams. Topics in Cognitive Science, 16(3), 391–429.
recent_2_synopsis: The paper reviews and synthesizes approaches to measuring team coordination dynamics, identifies gaps in the literature, and advances a conceptual roadmap for adaptive feedback systems that could use real-time coordination metrics to augment performance in human–autonomy team contexts. It connects team coordination science to adjacent fields such as psychotherapy and relationship science to inform future system design.
recent_3: Habaraduwa, U., Mavromoustakos Blom, P., & Wiltshire, T. J. (2025). Implicit coordination dynamics: A synchrony-based study on team positioning and performance in competitive Dota 2. IEEE Transactions on Games, 17(2), 442–450.
recent_3_synopsis: Using multivariate synchrony analysis of in-game positional data from competitive Dota 2 matches, this study quantifies implicit (non-verbal) coordination and links synchrony patterns to team performance outcomes, demonstrating the feasibility of unobtrusive, behavioral data streams for assessing teamwork quality.
seminal_1: Fiore, S. M., Wiltshire, T. J., Lobato, E. J. C., Jentsch, F. G., Huang, W. H., & Axelrod, B. (2013). Toward understanding social cues and signals in human–robot interaction: Effects of robot gaze and proxemic behavior. Frontiers in Psychology, 4, 859.
seminal_1_synopsis: This empirically grounded study examined how robot gaze and proxemic social cues influence human perceptions of a robot's social presence and emotional state during a hallway navigation scenario, finding that proxemic behavior was the more influential cue. With 276+ citations it became a foundational reference for HRI social cognition research, establishing the theoretical link between human social judgment processes and human–robot team dynamics.
seminal_2: Wiltshire, T. J., Butner, J. E., & Fiore, S. M. (2018). Problem-solving phase transitions during team collaboration. Cognitive Science, 42(1), 129–167.
seminal_2_synopsis: Integrating dynamical systems theory with collaborative problem-solving theory, this study provided the first direct empirical evidence that teams undergo qualitative phase transitions during problem solving—identified by entropy peaks in communication coding sequences—and that these transitions correlate with team performance. With 143+ citations it is a defining paper in the empirical study of macrocognition and team cognition dynamics.
expertise_overview: Teams and macrocognition, human-autonomy teaming, human-robot interaction, applying dynamical systems theory and multivariate synchrony analysis to team coordination over time. Methods span recurrence quantification, cluster-phase metrics, cross-wavelet synchrony, and entropy-based phase detection, applied across aviation, space missions, healthcare simulation, emergency response, and competitive gaming telemetry. Developed a three-level typology classifying team coordination dynamics approaches by dynamic complexity and a roadmap for adaptive feedback systems using real-time coordination metrics. Provided first direct empirical evidence that teams undergo qualitative phase transitions during collaborative problem solving, marked by communication entropy peaks correlating with performance. Quantified implicit non-verbal coordination from in-game positional data and showed proxemic social cues outweigh gaze in shaping perceived robot social presence. Also contributes EEG-based neuroimaging and physiological-behavioral workload measurement in training contexts.
notes: Identification is high-confidence: Wiltshire has published directly in Human Factors (Gorman & Wiltshire, 2024), attended and published in the HFES Annual Meeting proceedings (Fiore et al., 2024), and his research profile at Tilburg University consistently matches the human factors / ergonomics / cognitive systems domain. The "PR" (Past/Provisional Reviewer) role is consistent with his career stage (assistant professor, ~2,364 Google Scholar citations, h-index 27 as of 2024). The most-cited paper (Fiore et al., 2013 on HRI social cues, 276 cites) lists Wiltshire as second author; the most-cited sole/first-authored papers are the Problem-Solving Phase Transitions paper (2018, 143 cites) and the Interpersonal Coordination Dynamics in Psychotherapy paper (2020, 157 cites). The 2013 HRI paper was selected as a seminal work because of its citation impact and field influence even though Wiltshire was second author. The Habaraduwa et al. 2025 IEEE Transactions on Games paper page numbers (442–450) and volume (17, issue 2) are sourced from dblp and appear reliable. The van Weelden et al. 2024 Cognitive Systems Research paper (vol. 88, article 101282) is confirmed from multiple sources including ScienceDirect. The AIxVR 2026 prototyping paper is a workshop/conference paper and was not included in the recent list in favor of peer-reviewed journal articles.

name: Wooldridge, Abigail R.
recent_1: Hale-Lopez, K.L., Saenz, M.M., Verma, N., Chakravarthy, S., Handler, J., Ebert-Allen, R., Bond, W.F., & Wooldridge, A.R. (2025). A SEIPS-Based Analysis to Understand Safety Culture During Postpartum Hemorrhage. Healthcare, 13(5), 499. https://doi.org/10.3390/healthcare13050499
recent_1_synopsis: Applies the Systems Engineering Initiative for Patient Safety (SEIPS) framework to analyze the work system factors shaping safety culture during obstetric hemorrhage response, identifying barriers and facilitators to timely, effective care. The study contributes a structured macroergonomic lens to maternal safety—an area of growing focus in healthcare human factors.
recent_2: Mayes, E., Gehlbach, J.A., Jeziorczak, P.M., & Wooldridge, A.R. (2023). Machine Learning to Operationalize Team Cognition. Human Factors in Healthcare, 3, 100036. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.hfh.2023.100036
recent_2_synopsis: Demonstrates how machine learning methods can be used to quantitatively operationalize team cognition constructs (e.g., shared mental models, situation awareness) in patient handoff data, offering a scalable alternative to manual qualitative coding. This methodological contribution bridges computational approaches with macroergonomic theory.
recent_3: Luo, B.T., Barton, H.J., Wooldridge, A.R., & Kelly, M.M. (2023). Human Factors Engineering for the Pediatric Hospitalist. Hospital Pediatrics, 13(11), e365–e370. https://doi.org/10.1542/hpeds.2023-007258
recent_3_synopsis: Introduces human factors engineering principles to a pediatric hospitalist audience, illustrating how HFE concepts such as work system analysis and cognitive task analysis apply to clinical workflows and patient safety improvement in inpatient pediatrics. The paper serves as a translational bridge between HFE science and clinical practice.
seminal_1: Carayon, P., Wooldridge, A., Hoonakker, P., Hundt, A.S., & Kelly, M.M. (2020). SEIPS 3.0: Human-Centered Design of the Patient Journey for Patient Safety. Applied Ergonomics, 84, 103033. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2019.103033
seminal_1_synopsis: Extends the widely used SEIPS (Systems Engineering Initiative for Patient Safety) framework to its third iteration, centering the patient journey as a first-class unit of analysis and integrating patient experience, care transitions, and co-production of health. With approximately 590 citations, it is among the most influential recent frameworks in healthcare human factors, defining how researchers conceptualize and design patient-centered sociotechnical systems.
seminal_2: Wooldridge, A.R., Carayon, P., Hoonakker, P., Hose, B.-Z., Shaffer, D.W., Brazelton, T., Eithun, B., Rusy, D., Ross, J., Kohler, J., Kelly, M., Springman, S., & Gurses, A.P. (2022). Team Cognition in Handoffs: Relating System Factors, Team Cognition Functions and Outcomes in Two Handoff Processes. Human Factors, 66(1), 271–291. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208221086342
seminal_2_synopsis: Develops and empirically tests a macroergonomic model linking work system factors (tools, tasks, environment, organization, people) to team cognition functions (situation awareness, shared mental models, team communication) and handoff outcomes in pediatric trauma care, across two distinct handoff types. Published directly in Human Factors and recognized by PSNet, this paper is a foundational reference for researchers studying team performance and care transitions.
expertise_overview: Macroergonomics and healthcare human factors: care transitions, team cognition, patient safety, sociotechnical system design. Leading contributor to the SEIPS family, extending SEIPS to its third iteration by centering the patient journey as unit of analysis and integrating patient experience, care transitions, and co-production of health. Methods span mixed methods, process modeling, epistemic network analysis, and machine learning for behavioral data, applied to primary care, pediatric trauma, obstetrics, and surgical care. Developed and tested a macroergonomic model linking work system factors to team cognition functions—situation awareness, shared mental models, team communication—and handoff outcomes in pediatric trauma. Applied SEIPS to obstetric hemorrhage safety culture, used machine learning to operationalize team cognition constructs in handoff data, and translated human factors engineering principles for inpatient pediatrics. Also leads work on diversity, equity, and inclusion in the discipline.
notes: Identification is confident: Wooldridge is unambiguously the same person across UIUC's ISE department profile, her HFSS lab publications page, Google Scholar (h-index 15, ~1,780 citations as of 2025), and direct publication records in Human Factors (SAGE). Her PR (Past/Provisional Reviewer) role for Human Factors is consistent with her record of publishing in the journal and her active HFES leadership. Citation counts for the SEIPS 3.0 paper (~590) are from Google Scholar as scraped in this session. The "Team Cognition in Handoffs" paper was published online in 2022 but appears in Human Factors Volume 66(1), 2024 print — this is consistent with SAGE's OnlineFirst publishing workflow. All citations are drawn from her official lab publications page (hfss.ise.illinois.edu/publications/) and corroborated by Google Scholar and journal DOIs.

name: Xiao, Yan
recent_1: Xiao, Y., Hsu, Y.-J., Hannum, S. M., Abebe, E., Kantsiper, M. E., Pena, I. M., et al. (2025). Assessing patient work system factors for medication management during transition of care among older adults: an observational study. BMJ Quality & Safety, 34(1), 8–17.
recent_1_synopsis: This observational study developed and evaluated measures of patient work-system factors affecting medication management as older adults transitioned from hospital to home. The study found that most patients managed medications alone, faced low self-efficacy, and were unable to complete administration tasks, identifying risk factors that should be assessed before discharge to prevent medication discrepancies.
recent_2: Young, R. A., Xiao, Y., Fulda, K. G., White, A., & Gurses, A. P. (2025). Safety-I versus Safety-II: A mixed-methods study revealing the imbalance of approaches in primary care medication safety. Journal of Patient Safety, 21(7 Suppl), S81–S88.
recent_2_synopsis: Using a mixed-methods synthesis of a multiyear, multisite study on ambulatory medication safety, this paper applied Safety-I and Safety-II lenses to show that published best-practice guidance is predominantly Safety-I (reducing variability) while frontline patient and professional perspectives reveal complex distributed work-system challenges that demand the adaptive, strengths-based Safety-II framing.
recent_3: Xiao, Y., Urban, R. W., Roye, J. L., Reid, M. B., Schmitt, J., Borders, J., Klein, G., et al. (2024). Cue detection and self-debriefing techniques in virtual simulation: Techniques using cognitive engineering inspired expertise development approaches. Simulation in Healthcare. https://doi.org/10.1097/SIH.0000000000000826
recent_3_synopsis: This paper operationalized cognitive-engineering principles (tacit knowledge, sensemaking, mental models) to create cue-detection and expert-comparison self-debriefing exercises within a virtual simulation module on deteriorating patients. Among 189 nursing students who completed the module, 53–88% generated reflections specific enough for thematic analysis, demonstrating feasibility of the approach for accelerating clinical expertise.
seminal_1: Faraj, S., & Xiao, Y. (2006). Coordination in fast-response organizations. Management Science, 52(8), 1155–1169.
seminal_1_synopsis: Based on in-depth ethnographic observation of a medical trauma center, this paper introduced a coordination-practice perspective distinguishing 'expertise coordination' (protocols, plug-and-play teaming, knowledge sharing) from 'dialogic coordination' (joint sensemaking, epistemic contestation, cross-boundary intervention) as complementary mechanisms for error-free operation under uncertainty. With over 1,400 citations, it became a foundational text in organizational theory, CSCW, and healthcare macrocognition research.
seminal_2: Xiao, Y., Hunter, W. A., Mackenzie, C. F., Jefferies, N. J., Horst, R. L., & the LOTAS Group. (1996). Task complexity in emergency medical care and its implications for team coordination. Human Factors, 38(4), 636–645.
seminal_2_synopsis: Using video analysis of actual trauma resuscitations, this study identified four components of task complexity in emergency medicine — multiple concurrent tasks, uncertainty, changing plans, and compressed procedures — and linked each to specific team coordination failures. Published directly in Human Factors and accumulating over 200 citations, it established the empirical and conceptual foundation for understanding team coordination breakdowns in high-stakes clinical environments.
expertise_overview: Healthcare human factors, patient safety, team performance, health information technology usability. Trauma resuscitation video analysis identified four task-complexity components—multiple concurrent tasks, uncertainty, changing plans, compressed procedures—each linked to specific team coordination failures. Introduced a coordination-practice perspective distinguishing expertise coordination from dialogic coordination as complementary mechanisms for error-free operation under uncertainty. Extended into workflow analysis, artifact-mediated collaboration, and clinical information system design. Applies work-system modeling (SEIPS) and participatory design to medication safety during hospital-to-home transitions for older adults, finding low self-efficacy and incomplete administration that predict discrepancies, and contrasts Safety-I best-practice guidance against adaptive Safety-II framing. Operationalized cognitive-engineering principles—tacit knowledge, sensemaking, mental models—into self-debriefing exercises for simulation-based clinical training on deteriorating patients.
notes: Identification is confident. Yan Xiao is a well-established human factors researcher with a Google Scholar h-index of 52 and over 11,000 citations; his 1996 paper was published in the Human Factors journal itself, making his fit as a Reviewer (R) for the journal unambiguous. Citation counts for the two seminal papers are drawn from his Google Scholar profile (accessed June 2026): ~1,461 for Faraj & Xiao (2006) and ~217 for Xiao et al. (1996). The Simulation in Healthcare 2024 DOI suffix is confirmed via PubMed (PMID 40062978) but the full DOI string should be verified. The three recent papers are all peer-reviewed journal articles confirmed via PubMed/PMC and journal websites.

name: Xu, Jie
recent_1: Pan, Y., & Xu, J. (2025). Human-machine plan conflict and conflict resolution in a visual search task. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 193, 103377.
recent_1_synopsis: Using a hybrid foraging visual search paradigm, this study experimentally dissociates human–machine plan conflicts (arising from differing search strategies rather than errors by either party) and evaluates conflict-resolution approaches. It advances understanding of human–AI teaming by showing that strategy mismatches—not mistakes—are a primary source of collaboration breakdown.
recent_2: Zhang, Y., Dong, S., Niu, R., Chu, Y., Pan, Y., & Xu, J. (2026). Neuroergonomics evaluation of teamwork in a fast-paced communication and shared decision-making task. International Journal of Industrial Ergonomics, 112, 103892.
recent_2_synopsis: This fNIRS hyperscanning study found that trained teams exhibited lower prefrontal inter-brain synchrony (IBS) relative to untrained controls during a fast-paced team task, interpreting reduced IBS as a signature of more efficient coordination that reduces mutual prediction demand. IBS features classified trained versus control teams with 73–79% accuracy.
recent_3: Ge, X., Xu, H., Zhang, K., Ni, H., Xu, J., & Song, X. (2026). Effects of human–automation authority allocation on multitasking performance under workload conditions. International Journal of Human–Computer Interaction. Advance online publication. https://doi.org/10.1080/10447318.2026.2647133
recent_3_synopsis: Comparing human-led versus shared authority allocation strategies across fluctuating workload conditions, the study found that shared authority allocation supported better performance during low-to-high workload transitions while human-led allocation was superior during high-to-low transitions and produced lower subjective fatigue, informing adaptive automation design.
seminal_1: Xu, J., Le, K., Deitermann, A., & Montague, E. (2014). How different types of users develop trust in technology: A qualitative analysis of the antecedents of active and passive user trust in a shared technology. Applied Ergonomics, 45(6), 1495–1503.
seminal_1_synopsis: Using qualitative analysis of 54 participants in paired technology-mediated tasks, this study identified technology, user, and task factors as the three antecedent categories of trust and showed that active and passive users invoke similar overall trust levels through different evaluative pathways. It is among Xu's most-cited first-authored works (~122 citations on Google Scholar) and is widely referenced in healthcare HF and human-automation trust research.
seminal_2: Montague, E., Xu, J., Chen, P., Asan, O., Barrett, B. P., & Chewning, B. (2011). Modeling eye gaze patterns in clinician–patient interaction with lag sequential analysis. Human Factors, 53(5), 502–516.
seminal_2_synopsis: Analyzing 110 videotaped primary-care encounters, this study applied lag sequential analysis to establish that patients' gaze systematically follows the clinician's within two seconds whereas clinicians do not reciprocate, revealing the clinician as the unidirectional driver of nonverbal attention dynamics in medical encounters. Published in the flagship Human Factors journal (~90 citations), it anchored a line of healthcare HF work on eye-tracking and clinician–patient–computer interaction.
expertise_overview: Human factors engineering, neuroergonomics, human-computer interaction, human-AI interaction. Healthcare work on physician-patient communication and eye-gaze behavior used lag sequential analysis to show patients' gaze follows the clinician within two seconds without reciprocation, establishing the clinician as unidirectional driver of nonverbal attention; related work covers surgical stress, simulation-based training, and nuclear main control room evaluation. Trust research identifies technology, user, and task factors as antecedent categories and shows active and passive users reach similar trust through different pathways. Neuroergonomics work fuses EEG and eye-tracking for workload and uses fNIRS hyperscanning to show trained teams exhibit lower prefrontal inter-brain synchrony, classifying trained versus control teams at 73-79% accuracy. Human-AI teaming work dissociates human-machine plan conflicts arising from strategy mismatches rather than errors and evaluates authority-allocation strategies across fluctuating workload transitions.
notes: Identity is confidently established. Two researchers named Jie Xu appear in ergonomics-adjacent fields, but the profile here—PhD from University of Wisconsin–Madison in industrial and systems engineering, postdoc at Vanderbilt CRISS (human factors), current appointment at Lingnan University Psychology Department with an affiliated role at Zhejiang University—is the clear match for a PR-level Human Factors journal reviewer. Google Scholar user ID hQEaLDIAAAAJ, h-index 18, ~1,444 citations as of mid-2026. For the seminal papers, the 2011 Human Factors journal paper lists Montague as first author with Xu as second; the 2014 Applied Ergonomics paper lists Xu as first author. Both are confirmed via PubMed, PMC full text, and journal DOIs. Citation counts are from Google Scholar and should be treated as approximate.

name: Xu, Wei
recent_1: Xu, W., Gao, Z., & Dainoff, M. J. (2026). An HCAI methodological framework: Putting it into action to enable human-centered AI. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 56(1), 78–94. https://doi.org/10.1109/THMS.2025.3631590
recent_1_synopsis: Proposes a five-component human-centered AI methodological framework (HCAI-MF) covering requirement hierarchies, method taxonomies, processes, interdisciplinary collaboration, and multi-level design paradigms, providing practitioners with a concrete operational guide for implementing HCAI principles across AI system development.
recent_2: Xu, W., & Gao, Z. (2025). An intelligent sociotechnical systems (iSTS) framework: Enabling a hierarchical human-centered AI (hHCAI) approach. IEEE Transactions on Technology and Society, 6(1), 31–46.
recent_2_synopsis: Extends classical sociotechnical systems theory to the AI era by introducing an iSTS framework that emphasizes human-centered joint optimization across individual, organizational, ecosystem, and societal levels, then maps this onto a hierarchical HCAI (hHCAI) design approach.
recent_3: Xu, W., Gao, Z., & Ge, L. (2024). New research paradigms and agenda of human factors science in the intelligence era. Acta Psychologica Sinica, 56(3), 363–382. https://doi.org/10.3724/SP.J.1041.2024.00363
recent_3_synopsis: Proposes reconceiving the field as 'human factors science' to unify engineering psychology, ergonomics, HFE, and HCI under a common paradigm, then outlines priority research directions for adapting human factors methods and theory to AI-saturated sociotechnical environments.
seminal_1: Xu, W. (2019). Toward human-centered AI: A perspective from human-computer interaction. Interactions, 26(4), 42–46. https://doi.org/10.1145/3328485
seminal_1_synopsis: An early and widely-cited manifesto arguing that AI development must be grounded in human factors and HCI principles — emphasizing usability, human control, and user-centered design — that helped crystallize the 'human-centered AI' movement and is credited with influencing subsequent HCAI research agendas (~664 Google Scholar citations).
seminal_2: Xu, W., Dainoff, M. J., Ge, L., & Gao, Z. (2023). Transitioning to human interaction with AI systems: New challenges and opportunities for HCI professionals to enable human-centered AI. International Journal of Human–Computer Interaction, 39(3), 494–518. https://doi.org/10.1080/10447318.2022.2041900
seminal_2_synopsis: Provides a comprehensive analysis of how the transition from traditional HCI to human-AI interaction creates new competency demands for HCI professionals, articulating a research and practice agenda for the field that has become a key reference for HCAI scholars (~525 Google Scholar citations).
expertise_overview: Human-centered AI, human-AI interaction, cognitive systems engineering: automation and autonomy, human-machine systems, trust in automation, displays and controls. Developed a five-component methodological framework for human-centered AI covering requirement hierarchies, method taxonomies, processes, interdisciplinary collaboration, and multi-level design paradigms. Extended sociotechnical systems theory to the AI era with an intelligent sociotechnical systems framework emphasizing human-centered joint optimization across individual, organizational, ecosystem, and societal levels, mapped onto a hierarchical design approach. Argued for reconceiving the field as human factors science to unify engineering psychology, ergonomics, HFE, and HCI. Authored a manifesto grounding AI development in usability, human control, and user-centered design, and analyzed how the transition from traditional HCI to human-AI interaction creates new competency demands. Earlier work spans flight deck automation and aviation situation awareness.
notes: Identification is high-confidence. Wei Xu (许为) is the only prominent human factors/HCI scholar by this name associated with HFES fellowship and the Human Factors journal's scope. His Google Scholar profile (user=O8sGy-sAAAAJ) and ResearchGate profile (Wei-Xu-151) are consistent and clearly document this individual at Zhejiang University. The PR (Past/Provisional Reviewer) role code is plausible given his HFES Fellow status and publication record in adjacent venues. Citation counts (~664 and ~525 respectively for the two seminal papers) are drawn from the Google Scholar profile as of mid-2026. The IEEE THMS paper is dated Vol. 56 (2026) per the DBLP index but reflects work formally accepted in 2025 (DOI assigned 2025); it is listed as a recent publication accordingly. The 'Six Grand Challenges' paper (IJHCI 2023, ~769 citations) was omitted from seminal works because Xu is one of roughly 20 co-authors and it is not his independent contribution.

name: Yamani, Yusuke
recent_1: Yamani, Y., Jackson, A., Ashimi, F., & Politowicz, M. S. (2026). Gaze transition entropy and automation trust in a multitasking workspace. Scientific Reports, 16, 11122. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-026-41338-0
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined how visual scanning entropy relates to performance-based trust in automation during a multitasking (MATB-II-style) workspace. Results showed credible positive correlations between gaze transition entropy and performance-based trust, indicating that operators who trust their automation more exhibit more random (less predictable) eye movements across the display areas.
recent_2: Yamani, Y., Long, S. K., Sato, T., Braitman, A. L., Politowicz, M. S., & Chancey, E. T. (2025). Multilevel confirmatory factor analysis reveals two distinct human-automation trust constructs. Human Factors, 67(2), 166-180. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208241263774
recent_2_synopsis: Using multilevel CFA on data from multiple prior experiments, this paper tested whether the Chancey et al. (2017) and Jian et al. (2000) trust scales measure the same construct. The scales were found to capture distinct trust dimensions (performance/process/purpose vs. trust/distrust), with only weak cross-scale correlations, arguing that researchers should not treat them as interchangeable.
recent_3: Cui, Z., Sato, T., Jackson, A., Jayarathna, S., Itoh, M., & Yamani, Y. (2024). Gaze transition entropy as a measure of attention allocation in a dynamic workspace involving automation. Scientific Reports, 14, 23405. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-74244-4
recent_3_synopsis: This paper validated gaze transition entropy (GTE) and stationary gaze entropy (SGE) as measures of attention allocation during multi-task operation with automation support, using the MATB-II paradigm with eye tracking. Both entropy measures decreased under high tracking task load and increased with frequent communication interruptions, demonstrating that entropy indices capture dynamic attention strategies that traditional percent-dwell-time measures cannot.
seminal_1: Chancey, E. T., Bliss, J. P., Yamani, Y., & Handley, H. A. H. (2017). Trust and the compliance-reliance paradigm: The effects of risk, error bias, and reliability on trust and dependence. Human Factors, 59(3), 333-345. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720816682648
seminal_1_synopsis: This highly cited paper (230+ citations) provided a theoretical framework linking trust to the compliance-reliance paradigm by demonstrating that false alarms and missed alerts influence operator behavior through two independent trust mechanisms — trust in signals (driving compliance) and trust in nonsignals (driving reliance). The finding that the compliance-reliance distinction is not simply a reflection of two trust types has been foundational for subsequent research on human-automation trust measurement and design.
seminal_2: Karpinsky, N. D., Chancey, E. T., Palmer, D. B., & Yamani, Y. (2018). Automation trust and attention allocation in multitasking workspace. Applied Ergonomics, 70, 194-201. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2018.03.008
seminal_2_synopsis: This influential study (80+ citations) empirically established a negative correlation between automation trust and visual attention allocated to the automation in a multi-task workspace, and further showed that high task load lowered performance- and process-based trust ratings. It seeded a productive line of research — including later meta-analyses and entropy-based gaze studies — on how trust shapes attentional resource allocation when working alongside automated systems.
expertise_overview: Visual attention, perception, and cognition in technology-rich environments; automation and autonomy, attention and workload, transportation human factors. Linked the compliance-reliance paradigm to two independent mechanisms—trust in signals driving compliance, trust in nonsignals driving reliance—showing the distinction is not simply two trust types, and established a negative correlation between automation trust and visual attention allocated to automation, with high task load lowering performance- and process-based trust. Validated gaze transition entropy and stationary gaze entropy as attention-allocation measures using the MATB-II paradigm, capturing dynamic strategies beyond percent-dwell-time, and related gaze entropy positively to performance-based trust. Showed established trust scales measure distinct dimensions and should not be treated as interchangeable. Also addresses automated and connected vehicle takeover, driver hazard anticipation training, intersection safety, multitasking workspaces, and advanced air mobility.
notes: Identity is confirmed: Yusuke Yamani is listed as an Associate Editor of Human Factors journal and is an Associate Professor at Old Dominion University. The two seminal papers were selected based on Google Scholar citation counts reported in search results (Chancey et al. 2017 at ~232 citations; Karpinsky et al. 2018 at ~83 citations); Yamani is a co-author on both. The 2026 Scientific Reports paper (Yamani et al., 2026, article 11122) was identified via web search but could not be fully verified via direct fetch due to Nature paywall redirects; the authors list (Yamani, Jackson, Ashimi, Politowicz) and article number come from search snippet data and should be treated as reliable but unconfirmed at the full-text level. All other citations were verified via PubMed or SAGE journal pages.

name: Yang, X. Jessie
recent_1: Kim, J. Y., Lester, C., & Yang, X. J. (2025). Beyond binary decisions: Evaluating the effects of AI error type on trust and performance in AI-assisted tasks. Human Factors, 67(10), 1062–1083. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251326795
recent_1_synopsis: This study moves beyond oversimplified binary-performance models of AI errors by demonstrating that, in multi-class decision tasks, different AI error types produce distinctly different outcomes for operator trust and task accuracy. A key finding is that some error types provided false reassurance that degraded performance while others paradoxically prompted verification behaviors that improved performance, showing that error type matters more than error frequency alone.
recent_2: Chung, H., Holder, T., Shah, J. A., & Yang, X. J. (2026). A systematic review and taxonomy of human-agent teaming testbeds. Human Factors, 68(2), 197–238. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208251376898
recent_2_synopsis: The paper proposes a ten-attribute taxonomy for human-agent teams and applies it to systematically analyze 103 testbeds drawn from 235 empirical studies. It reveals that the field overwhelmingly studies single-human/few-agent dyads with designated human leadership, identifying significant gaps in research on larger, more dynamic team configurations.
recent_3: Chung, H., & Yang, X. J. (2025). Trust in the team as a function of trust in individual agents: Scale validation and modeling. Human Factors. https://doi.org/10.1177/10711813251358794
recent_3_synopsis: This paper examines how trust in a multi-human multi-agent team emerges from trust placed in individual constituent agents, validating a scale for measuring team-level trust in a collaborative block-moving task with two humans and two agents. It provides both a validated measurement instrument and a model of how individual-agent trust aggregates to team trust.
seminal_1: Yang, X. J., Unhelkar, V. V., Li, K., & Shah, J. A. (2017). Evaluating effects of user experience and system transparency on trust in automation. Proceedings of the 2017 ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction (HRI '17), 408–416. https://doi.org/10.1145/2909824.3020230
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper reframed trust in automation as a dynamic variable rather than a static steady-state measure, introducing the concept of 'trust of entirety' to capture how trust accumulates across the full human-automation interactive experience. With approximately 337 citations, it is highly influential because it established a new empirical and conceptual basis for studying how transparency and user experience shape trust dynamics — a framework that has anchored a substantial subsequent literature on adaptive automation.
seminal_2: Guo, Y., & Yang, X. J. (2021). Modeling and predicting trust dynamics in human-robot teaming: A Bayesian inference approach. International Journal of Social Robotics, 13(8), 1899–1909. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12369-020-00703-3
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper developed a personalized, computational model of trust dynamics in which Bayesian inference is used to learn individual-level parameters from observed human behavior during repeated interaction with a robotic agent. With approximately 200 citations, it is influential for demonstrating that trust trajectories cluster into distinct behavioral profiles (Bayesian decision-makers, disbelievers, oscillators), providing a foundation for adaptive autonomy systems that tailor their behavior to individual users' trust patterns.
expertise_overview: Trust in automation and autonomy—measurement, modeling, and calibration of dynamic trust as humans interact with automated aids, autonomous vehicles, and collaborative robots. Reframed trust as dynamic rather than static, introducing trust of entirety to capture how trust accumulates across the full interactive experience, and developed a personalized computational model using Bayesian inference to learn individual-level trust parameters, revealing behavioral profiles such as Bayesian decision-makers, disbelievers, and oscillators. Human-agent teaming contributions include a ten-attribute taxonomy applied across 103 testbeds and a validated scale modeling how individual-agent trust aggregates to team-level trust in multi-human multi-agent settings. Showed AI error type, not frequency alone, shapes trust and accuracy, with some errors providing false reassurance and others prompting beneficial verification. Also spans conditionally automated driving, eye-tracking-based situation awareness prediction, takeover performance, AI-assisted decision-making, and uncertainty communication.
notes: Identity is unambiguous: X. Jessie Yang (also written Xi Jessie Yang) is confirmed as an editorial board member of Human Factors (SAGE) via the journal's own editorial board page. She is an Associate Professor at the University of Michigan IOE with a Google Scholar h-index of 41 and over 6,500 career citations. The three recent papers selected are all published in Human Factors (SAGE) itself or confirmed peer-reviewed journals (2025–2026). The two seminal works are the most-cited items in her Google Scholar profile (337 and 200 citations respectively as of mid-2026). Citation counts for the HRI 2017 paper and the IJSR 2021 paper are taken from Google Scholar data retrieved from her profile page.

name: Yang, Xiao
recent_1: Yang, X., Gazzanigo, M., Fang, F., & House, S. (2025). Vagally mediated heart rate variability modulates the association between the perceived workload and the Stroop effect on behavioral performance. Physiological Reports, 13(14), e70466. https://doi.org/10.14814/phy2.70466
recent_1_synopsis: This study demonstrated that resting cardiac vagal control (vmHRV) moderates the relationship between subjective workload perception and cognitive inhibition performance on the Stroop task; individuals with low vmHRV showed a strong coupling between increased perceived workload and degraded inhibitory control, whereas high-vmHRV individuals were buffered against this effect, supporting the Neurovisceral Integration Model of emotion-cognition integration.
recent_2: Yang, X., Heberlein, K., Reid, A., Jiao, D., & Fang, F. (2024). Mental workload modulates the effects of baroreceptor afferents on sensorimotor processing. Journal of Clinical and Basic Psychosomatics, 2(2), 2248. https://doi.org/10.36922/jcbp.2248
recent_2_synopsis: This paper showed that concurrent working memory load reverses the typical cardiac timing effect on reaction time (normally slower during cardiac systole than diastole), establishing that top-down executive resources can override automatic baroreceptor-mediated inhibition of sensorimotor responses — a key finding for understanding heart-brain interactions under cognitive demand.
recent_3: Yang, X., Nackley, B., & Friedman, B. H. (2023). Comparison between the Effects of Acute Physical and Psychosocial Stress on Feedback-Based Learning. Brain Sciences, 13(8), 1127. https://doi.org/10.3390/brainsci13081127
recent_3_synopsis: By directly comparing a cold pressor (physical) stressor against a mental arithmetic (psychosocial) stressor, the study found that both stress types enhanced positive feedback learning in proportion to cardiovascular reactivity, without differential effects between stressor types, suggesting stress facilitates learning through multiple autonomic pathways rather than a single hormonal mechanism.
seminal_1: Yang, X., Jennings, J. R., & Friedman, B. H. (2017). Exteroceptive stimuli override interoceptive state in reaction time control. Psychophysiology, 54(12), 1940–1950. https://doi.org/10.1111/psyp.12958
seminal_1_synopsis: Using a cardiac-cycle-gated reaction time paradigm, this study demonstrated that while cardiac timing (systole vs. diastole) modulates response speed in the absence of competing stimuli, the presence of an accessory exteroceptive visual stimulus (fear or neutral face) fully overrides these interoceptive cardiac effects — an early and foundational result for understanding the hierarchy of interoceptive versus exteroceptive influences on human sensorimotor performance.
seminal_2: Yang, X., Spangler, D. P., Thayer, J. F., & Friedman, B. H. (2021). Resting heart rate variability modulates the effects of concurrent working memory load on affective startle modification. Psychophysiology, 58(8), e13833. https://doi.org/10.1111/psyp.13833
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper showed that working memory load attenuated affective startle response modification, and that this attenuation was significantly stronger in low-vmHRV individuals — directly linking individual differences in cardiac vagal tone to the capacity for context-appropriate emotional responding under cognitive load, supporting the Generalized Unsafety Theory of Stress (GUTS) and providing a psychophysiological account relevant to psychopathology and operator performance under multitask conditions.
expertise_overview: Psychophysiology, cognitive psychology, neuroergonomics: how autonomic regulation—particularly vagally mediated heart rate variability and baroreceptor afferents—moderates information processing, mental workload, and emotion-cognition interaction under realistic task demands. Applies ECG, EEG, ERP, and fNIRS to workload assessment, stress effects on learning and decision-making, and operator performance. Showed resting cardiac vagal control moderates the coupling between perceived workload and Stroop inhibitory control, buffering high-vmHRV individuals, consistent with the Neurovisceral Integration Model. Demonstrated working memory load reverses the cardiac timing effect on reaction time, evidencing top-down override of baroreceptor-mediated inhibition, and that exteroceptive emotional stimuli override interoceptive cardiac effects on sensorimotor responses. Found physical and psychosocial stressors enhance positive-feedback learning in proportion to cardiovascular reactivity, and that working memory load attenuates affective startle modification most strongly in low-vmHRV individuals, supporting the Generalized Unsafety Theory of Stress.
notes: Identification confidence is moderate-to-high. There are multiple researchers named Xiao Yang; the one identified here is an Assistant Professor in the Psychology Department at Old Dominion University (x2yang@odu.edu), whose research in psychophysiology, mental workload, and emotion-cognition interaction fits the Human Factors editorial board role. Her Google Scholar profile (user=M6WFQjgAAAAJ) lists h-index 13 and 435 citations as of recent data. A distinct Xiao Yang at Johns Hopkins BME focuses on nanobioelectronics (unrelated), and X. Jessie Yang at University of Michigan focuses on human-robot trust (also relevant to HF but a different person). The ODU Xiao Yang's participation in HFES conferences and publication in HFES Proceedings (Quesnel & Yang, 2023, on cellphone separation and Stroop) strongly supports this identification. The 2021 Psychophysiology paper article number appears as e13833 in some sources; the volume/issue details (58, 8) are confirmed but the exact article number may be e13833 rather than e13833 — treat with minor caution. Citation counts for seminal papers are drawn from Google Scholar and may not be fully current.

name: Young, Kristie
recent_1: Young, K.L., Stephens, A.N., & McDonald, H. (2024). Executive function and drivers' ability to self-regulate behaviour when engaging with devices. Current Psychology, 43, 25732–25742. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12144-024-06224-w
recent_1_synopsis: Using a four-week trip diary and the BRIEF-A executive function assessment with 25 licensed drivers (ages 20–65), this study found that deficits in behavioural regulation and metacognition were associated with greater device engagement while driving, suggesting that individual cognitive capacity shapes willingness and ability to self-regulate distraction.
recent_2: Koppel, S., Young, K., Liang, H., Molesworth, B., Eusebio, J., Senserrick, T., Williamson, A., Olivier, J., Howell, F.J., McDonald, H., Haworth, N., Klauer, S.G., & Charlton, J.L. (2025). Using Australian Naturalistic Driving Study data to explore non-driving task engagement across young novice and experienced drivers. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 113, 374–385.
recent_2_synopsis: Analysing 84 naturalistic driving trips from the Australian NDS, this study found that young novice drivers spent over 58% of driving time engaged in non-driving tasks and undertook longer task episodes while moving compared to experienced drivers, highlighting the heightened distraction risk for this demographic in real-world conditions.
recent_3: Currie, G., Reynolds, J., Logan, D., & Young, K.L. (2023). 'Tram Wrong Way' international experience and mitigation of track switch errors. Transportation Research Record, 2677(6), 631–643. https://doi.org/10.1177/03611981221149432
recent_3_synopsis: Drawing on incident databases and global tram operator practice across Melbourne, Toronto, Zurich, and San Diego, this study found that wrong-way tram switch errors cluster at 10% of switches and disproportionately involve operators in their first year; point-and-call cognitive procedures reduced incidents by 30–40%, providing actionable human factors guidance for urban rail safety.
seminal_1: Hosking, S.G., Young, K.L., & Regan, M.A. (2009). The effects of text messaging on young drivers. Human Factors, 51(4), 582–592. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720809341575
seminal_1_synopsis: Using an instrumented vehicle with young novice drivers, this study demonstrated that retrieving and sending text messages increased eyes-off-road time by approximately 400%, lane-position variability by 50%, and missed lane changes by 140% relative to undistracted baseline — one of the earliest and most widely cited empirical demonstrations of the hazard of phone-based texting while driving, published in the HFES flagship journal.
seminal_2: Regan, M.A., Lee, J.D., & Young, K.L. (Eds.). (2009). Driver Distraction: Theory, Effects, and Mitigation. CRC Press / Taylor & Francis. ISBN 9780849374265.
seminal_2_synopsis: Co-edited by Young alongside Michael Regan and John Lee, this comprehensive volume synthesising contributions from over 40 experts established the theoretical and empirical foundations of the driver distraction field — covering definitions, measurement, cognitive mechanisms, and countermeasures — and has been the standard reference work for researchers and practitioners in transportation human factors for over 15 years.
expertise_overview: Transportation human factors and road safety; driver distraction across cognitive, visual, and manual secondary-task effects. Naturalistic driving methods for real-world attention allocation, showing young novice drivers spend over half their driving time on non-driving tasks and that texting increases eyes-off-road time, lane-position variability, and missed lane changes. Individual differences in executive function, behavioral regulation, and metacognition shaping self-regulation of distraction. Automated-vehicle human factors: Level 3 takeover, driver willingness to engage and resume control, HMI design for connected/automated vehicles. Public-transport operator error, point-and-call procedures for urban rail safety, acceptability of passive alcohol-sensing technology. Co-authored a reference synthesizing the driver distraction field.
notes: Identity is confidently established: Dr. Kristie L. Young, Monash University Accident Research Centre, Melbourne, Australia. There is a second Google Scholar profile (user 6mAzTXMAAAAJ) for a different Kristie Young unrelated to human factors — the correct profile is user 2QNXR-cAAAAJ with ~6,000 citations. The 2025 naturalistic driving paper (Koppel et al.) was available online (ScienceDirect DOI S1369847825001743) but paywalled; volume/page details were confirmed via search engine snippets reporting the accepted manuscript metadata. The edited book (Regan, Lee, Young 2009) is treated as a seminal contribution because co-editing it was career-defining for Young and it is among the most-cited works associated with her name; it functions as both a primary scholarly contribution and a highly influential reference. Citation counts for individual papers were not directly accessible from open web sources during this search.

name: Yu, Denny
recent_1: Obuseh, M., Singh, S., Anton, N. E., Gardiner, R., Stefanidis, D., & Yu, D. (2025). Feasibility of large language models for assessing and coaching surgeons' non-technical skills. npj Health Systems, 2, Article 25. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44401-025-00027-2
recent_1_synopsis: This study demonstrates that open-source LLMs (Llama 3.1 and Mistral) can analyze robotic surgery transcripts to identify exemplar and non-exemplar communication behaviors and autonomously generate structured coaching feedback, achieving ~74–75% accuracy without explicit training on human coaching data. The work offers a scalable, low-subjectivity alternative to manual non-technical skills assessment in surgical training.
recent_2: Lim, C., Obuseh, M., Cha, J. S., Steward, J., Sundaram, C. P., & Yu, D. (2025). Neural insights on expert surgeons' mental workload during live robotic surgeries. Scientific Reports, 15, Article 12073. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-96064-w
recent_2_synopsis: Using EEG recorded from expert surgeons across 13 live robotic urological procedures, this paper identifies neural signatures of varying mental workload intraoperatively, demonstrating that brain-region-specific activation patterns differentiate cognitive demand levels in real surgical conditions rather than simulation. The findings provide physiological grounding for real-time adaptive automation in the operating room.
recent_3: Lim, C., Nasir, M., Nestorowich, J., Berry, C., Ray, P., Pitts, B. J., & Yu, D. (2025). In-vehicle multi-sensory experiences can reduce mental stress. Applied Ergonomics, 129, Article (online first). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104559
recent_3_synopsis: This study evaluates a multi-sensory relaxation system (combining audio, lighting, scent, and haptics) used in a stationary vehicle, showing measurable reductions in physiological and subjective stress markers. It expands the scope of automotive human factors from safety-only concerns to driver wellbeing and passive recovery in everyday environments.
seminal_1: Zhang, T., Yang, J., Liang, N., Pitts, B. J., Prakah-Asante, K., Curry, R., Duerstock, B., Wachs, J. P., & Yu, D. (2023). Physiological measurements of situation awareness: A systematic review. Human Factors, 65(5), 737–758. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720820969071
seminal_1_synopsis: This systematic review synthesizes evidence linking physiological signals — eye tracking, heart rate, EEG, electrodermal activity, and respiration — to direct measures of situation awareness (SA) across aviation, driving, and other high-stakes domains. With approximately 269 citations, it has become a standard reference for researchers designing objective SA assessment methods, directly informing the physiological sensing agenda that defines Yu's lab.
seminal_2: Wu, C., Cha, J., Sulek, J., Zhou, T., Sundaram, C. P., Wachs, J., & Yu, D. (2020). Eye-tracking metrics predict perceived workload in robotic surgical skills training. Human Factors, 62(8), 1365–1386. https://doi.org/10.1177/0018720819874544
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper established that objective eye-tracking metrics — pupil diameter, gaze entropy, fixation duration, and PERCLOS — reliably predict NASA-TLX workload ratings during robotic surgery tasks, providing the first validated ocular workload proxy for surgical skills assessment. It is widely cited in surgical ergonomics and human factors literature and helped launch an entire line of work on objective, sensor-based performance evaluation in operating-room environments.
expertise_overview: Human factors engineering, biobehavioral sensing, and technology design in healthcare and surgical settings. Cognitive workload and situation awareness from EEG, eye tracking, electrodermal activity, heart rate, and respiration; validates ocular metrics (pupil diameter, gaze entropy, fixation duration, PERCLOS) against NASA-TLX during robotic surgery. Intraoperative neural signatures of mental workload in live robotic procedures for real-time adaptive automation in the operating room. Human-robot interaction, physical and surgical ergonomics, exoskeleton interventions, non-technical skills assessment, LLM-based coaching feedback from surgery transcripts. Systematic review linking physiological signals to situation awareness across domains; driver wellbeing through multi-sensory relaxation systems and stress reduction.
notes: Identification is high-confidence: Denny Yu at Purdue University is the sole prominent researcher of this name active in human factors and ergonomics, has published multiple papers in the Human Factors journal (SAGE), received the 2021 Human Factors Prize from HFES, and his role as Associate Editor is consistent with his seniority and citation record. The two seminal papers were selected based on citation counts reported on his Google Scholar profile (~269 and high counts respectively); the systematic review carries the larger raw count but was published online in 2020 (print 2023). For the third recent paper (Applied Ergonomics 2025), the exact article number/pages were not retrievable from the paywalled ScienceDirect page; the DOI suffix is approximate and should be verified. The npj Health Systems (2025) and Scientific Reports (2025) citations are confirmed via PubMed Central and search result metadata.

name: Zhang, Xudong
recent_1: Zhou, Y., Reddy, C., & Zhang, X. (2024). The deflection of fatigued neck. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 121(34), e2401874121. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2401874121
recent_1_synopsis: Using high-precision dynamic stereo-X-ray imaging during sustained neck exertion tasks, this study provided the first direct experimental evidence that muscle fatigue mechanically alters cervical spine curvature, increasing injury and pain risk. Findings have direct implications for workplace neck ergonomics and the design of head-mounted wearable devices.
recent_2: Reddy, C., Zhou, Y., Yin, W., & Zhang, X. (2024). Advanced subject-specific neck musculoskeletal modeling unveils sex differences in muscle moment arm and cervical spine loading. Journal of Biomechanics, 171, 112181. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbiomech.2024.112181
recent_2_synopsis: Individualized musculoskeletal neck models for 23 participants revealed that females have smaller extensor muscle moment arms and experience higher cervical spinal compression during exertion than males, providing a biomechanical basis for the well-documented higher neck pain incidence in women.
recent_3: Chen, Y., Yin, W., Zheng, L., Mehta, R., & Zhang, X. (2025). Biodynamic modeling and analysis of human-exoskeleton interactions in simulated patient handling tasks. Human Factors, 67(7), 641–655. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187208241311271
recent_3_synopsis: Subject-specific biomechanical models evaluated how a back-support exoskeleton affects lumbar and shoulder joint loading during three patient-care tasks (side-turning, upper-body lifting, sit-up assistance), finding that the device reduced peak joint moments by roughly 20–27% while increasing joint flexion motion, informing task-specific exoskeleton design for occupational safety.
seminal_1: Aiyangar, A. K., Zheng, L., Tashman, S., Anderst, W. J., & Zhang, X. (2014). Capturing three-dimensional in vivo lumbar intervertebral joint kinematics using dynamic stereo-X-ray imaging. Journal of Biomechanical Engineering, 136(1), 011004. https://doi.org/10.1115/1.4025793
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper pioneered a high-speed dynamic stereo-radiography technique coupled with volumetric bone tracking to measure true 3-D lumbar segmental kinematics during functional lifting in living participants — the first method capable of achieving this in vivo. It established a methodological and normative baseline that underpins subsequent work on spine pathology, implant design, and occupational lifting biomechanics worldwide.
seminal_2: Zhang, X., Xiong, J., & Bishop, A. M. (2003). Effects of load and speed on lumbar vertebral kinematics during lifting motions. Human Factors, 45(2), 296–306. https://doi.org/10.1518/hfes.45.2.296.27242
seminal_2_synopsis: An early in vivo study using skin-surface motion capture to quantify center-of-rotation and segmental angular displacement for lumbar vertebrae L2–L5 under varied load and speed conditions during sagittal lifting, establishing that COR locations and vertebral displacements are largely invariant to moderate load and speed manipulations. Published in Human Factors, this work influenced occupational biomechanics standards and laid groundwork for Zhang's subsequent high-precision imaging programme.
expertise_overview: Musculoskeletal biomechanics and human-machine systems engineering; in vivo dynamic imaging, subject-specific computational modeling, occupational ergonomics of the spine and upper extremity. High-speed dynamic stereo-radiography with volumetric bone tracking measuring true 3-D lumbar segmental kinematics during functional lifting in living participants, establishing normative baselines for spine pathology, implant design, and occupational lifting biomechanics. Manual material handling, work-related musculoskeletal disorder prevention, patient-handling biomechanics. Sex and age differences in musculoskeletal capacity, showing smaller female cervical extensor moment arms and higher spinal compression; muscle fatigue mechanically alters cervical curvature. Back-support and upper-limb exoskeleton evaluation via subject-specific models, quantifying reductions in lumbar and shoulder joint loading. Methods: stereo X-ray imaging, digital human and musculoskeletal modeling, flexible wearable sensors.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence: multiple corroborating sources (TAMU faculty profile, NC State seminar page, SAGE author page, and PubMed records) consistently identify this Xudong Zhang as Professor of ISE/ME/BME at Texas A&M University, director of the Musculoskeletal Biomechanics Laboratory, HFES Fellow, and Associate Editor of Human Factors. He should not be confused with other researchers named Xudong Zhang on Google Scholar (who work in SLAM/computer vision or biosensors). Full Google Scholar citation metrics were inaccessible due to access restrictions; seminal papers were identified based on methodological novelty, venue (Human Factors; Journal of Biomechanical Engineering), and prominence in citing literature. The 2003 Human Factors paper citation details are confirmed via PubMed PMID 14529200; the 2014 ASME paper is confirmed via PubMed PMID 24149991.

name: Zhang, Yiqu
recent_1: Tan, X., & Zhang, Y. (2025). Driver situation awareness for regaining control from conditionally automated vehicles: A systematic review of empirical studies. Human Factors, 67(4), 367–403.
recent_1_synopsis: A systematic review synthesizing empirical findings on how drivers recover situation awareness during takeovers from conditionally automated vehicles (SAE Level 3). The paper maps factors — takeover request design, lead time, individual differences — that shape driver readiness to resume manual control, providing design guidance for SAE L3 systems.
recent_2: Ma, Z., & Zhang, Y. (2024). Fostering drivers' trust in automated driving styles: The role of driver perception of automated driving maneuvers. Human Factors, 66(7), 1961–1976.
recent_2_synopsis: A driving-simulator experiment examining how different automated driving styles (e.g., aggressive vs. conservative) shape driver perception, calibrate trust, and influence willingness to delegate control. Findings inform how AV behavior should be designed to build appropriate — neither over- nor under-trust — reliance.
recent_3: Ma, Z., & Zhang, Y. (2024). Driver-automated vehicle interaction in mixed traffic: Types of interaction and drivers' driving styles. Human Factors, 66(2), 544–561.
recent_3_synopsis: Investigates how human drivers' own driving styles moderate their behavioral responses when sharing the road with AVs in mixed-traffic environments. The study identifies distinct interaction patterns between aggressive/conservative human drivers and AVs, with implications for macroscopic traffic safety in partially automated fleets.
seminal_1: Ma, Z., & Zhang, Y. (2021). Driver trust, acceptance, and takeover behaviors in fully automated vehicles: Effects of automated driving styles and driver's driving styles. Accident Analysis & Prevention, 159, 106238.
seminal_1_synopsis: This simulator study (~166 citations) is one of the first to jointly examine how the AV's own driving style and the human driver's dispositional driving style interact to shape trust, acceptance, and takeover quality in fully automated vehicles. It established a foundational two-factor framework — AV behavior style × human driver style — that has been widely adopted in subsequent HF/AV interaction research.
seminal_2: Wang, S., Zhang, Y., Wu, C., Darvas, F., & Chaovalitwongse, W.A. (2015). Online prediction of driver distraction based on brain activity patterns. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 16(1), 136–150.
seminal_2_synopsis: An early (~106 citations) brain-computer interface study demonstrating real-time, EEG-based prediction of driver distraction using machine learning on neural signals. It helped establish a neurophysiological approach to operator state monitoring that has influenced subsequent work on passive driver monitoring and adaptive in-vehicle systems.
expertise_overview: Human factors and intelligent transportation systems; human-automation interaction in connected and automated vehicles. Trust calibration: how automated-vehicle driving styles shape driver perception, trust, acceptance, and willingness to delegate control; two-factor framework linking AV behavior style with human dispositional driving style to predict takeover quality. Situation awareness recovery and takeover performance in conditionally automated driving, mapping how takeover request design, lead time, and individual differences shape readiness to resume manual control. Computational cognitive models of warning response and takeover reaction time; mixed-traffic interaction patterns between aggressive and conservative human drivers and AVs. EEG-based machine-learning prediction of driver distraction for operator-state monitoring; healthcare situation awareness in AI-assisted settings.
notes: The HFES reviewer pool lists this member as "Yiqu Zhang" (role code PR — Past/Provisional Reviewer). All available evidence — Penn State IME directory (user ID yuz450), Google Scholar profile (user=hYfPDtQAAAAJ), HFES conference presenter records, and multiple SAGE Human Factors journal publications — points to Yiqi Zhang (张贻齐), Associate Professor at Penn State. The "Yiqu" vs "Yiqi" discrepancy is almost certainly a romanization variant (data-entry difference) of the same Chinese name. No other human-factors-adjacent researcher named "Yiqu Zhang" was found in any academic database. Identification confidence is high. Citation counts sourced from Google Scholar as of June 2026 (total ~1,309 citations; h-index 22). The three most recent peer-reviewed journal papers selected prioritize 2024–2025 Human Factors publications; a 2026 paper exists (Accident Analysis & Prevention) but has only 1 citation and the publication year sits at the edge of the knowledge window.

name: Flathmann, Christopher
recent_1: Flathmann, C., McNeese, N. J., Sengupta, S., & Johnson, E. (2025). Exploring trust, acceptance, and behavioral differences when humans collaborate with large language models as tools and teammates. ACM Transactions on Interactive Intelligent Systems, 15(4). https://doi.org/10.1145/3764591
recent_1_synopsis: An empirical online experiment with 778 participants examined how framing a commercially available LLM as a 'tool' versus a 'teammate' alters users' trust, acceptance, and behavior. The study shows that the relational framing of AI shapes human collaboration patterns in ways relevant to human-AI team design.
recent_2: Flathmann, C., McNeese, N. J., & O'Neill, T. A. (2025). Designing high-impact experiments for human-autonomy/AI teaming. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434251327697
recent_2_synopsis: A methodological guide that articulates four key decision points — research question, team composition, research environment, and data collection — that researchers must navigate when designing human-autonomy teaming (HAT) studies. The paper addresses the interdisciplinary complexity of HAT research to help new investigators produce rigorous, high-impact work.
recent_3: Schelble, B. G., Flathmann, C., Aly, H., Lyons, J. B., & McNeese, N. J. (in press/2026). Human or AI advice? Examining trust, influence, and responsibility in ethically charged human-AI team decision-making. Journal of Cognitive Engineering and Decision Making. https://doi.org/10.1177/15553434261434613
recent_3_synopsis: Investigates how the source of advice (human vs. AI) shapes team members' trust, behavioral compliance, and perceived moral responsibility in high-stakes, ethically loaded decisions. Findings have implications for designing AI advisory systems where accountability and ethical agency must be preserved.
seminal_1: Schelble, B. G., Flathmann, C., McNeese, N. J., Freeman, G., & Mallick, R. (2022). Let's think together! Assessing shared mental models, performance, and trust in human-agent teams. Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction, 6(GROUP), Article 13, 1–29. https://doi.org/10.1145/3492832
seminal_1_synopsis: This Honorable Mention paper (226+ citations) empirically examined how shared mental models, team performance, and trust co-develop in human-agent teams using a controlled teaming simulation. It is highly influential because it extended established team cognition theory to human-AI contexts and provided one of the first rigorous empirical baselines for human-agent team effectiveness.
seminal_2: Flathmann, C., Schelble, B. G., Rosopa, P. J., McNeese, N. J., Mallick, R., & Madathil, K. C. (2023). Examining the impact of varying levels of AI teammate influence on human-AI teams. International Journal of Human-Computer Studies, 177, 103061. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhcs.2023.103061
seminal_2_synopsis: Systematically manipulated the degree to which an AI teammate could influence human decision-making in a collaborative task environment, finding that moderate AI influence optimized team performance without undermining human autonomy. The paper is seminal in defining the 'AI social influence' construct and in establishing empirical guidelines for calibrating AI assertiveness in teaming systems.
expertise_overview: Human-autonomy teaming and trust in automation; how AI teammates' social influence, communication strategies, and autonomy levels shape human trust, acceptance, and team cognition. Established the 'AI social influence' construct, showing moderate AI influence optimizes team performance without undermining human autonomy; relational framing of an LLM as 'tool' versus 'teammate' alters trust and collaboration. Extends shared mental model theory and situation awareness frameworks to human-AI teams, providing early empirical baselines for how shared mental models, performance, and trust co-develop in human-agent teaming. Ethics and responsible AI design: how AI teammates' moral personas and the human-versus-AI source of advice affect compliance and perceived moral responsibility in high-stakes decisions. Methodological frameworks for designing human-autonomy teaming experiments and generative-AI-based simulation.
notes: Identification is confident. Flathmann is a single, clearly identifiable researcher with a substantial public profile: an assistant professor at Clemson University (School of Computing), a personal publications page (chrisflathmann.com), a Google Scholar profile (h-index 22, ~1,478 citations as of mid-2026), and an ACM Digital Library author page. His role as PR (Past/Provisional Reviewer) at Human Factors is consistent with his publication history in HFES Annual Meeting Proceedings and his co-authorship with established Human Factors scholars (McNeese, O'Neill, Salas, Lyons). The 'Let's Think Together' paper is listed as his most-cited single work at 226 citations on Google Scholar. Citation counts for other papers come from the Google Scholar profile summary fetched during research.
